Legal Reference on the Powers of the Bangsamoro Government

Under Republic Act No. 11054 (Bangsamoro Organic Law)
Volume I — Powers (a) through (zz) — 44 of 55 Enumerated Powers

Source: Republic Act No. 11054, Art. V, Sec. 2

Companion Laws: BAA No. 13 (Administrative Code), BAA Nos. 1-89

Format: Legal Sources tables + 9-theme Q&A Legal Briefer per power

Date: March 2026

Note: This is Volume I, covering powers (a) through (aa) — 27 of the 55 powers enumerated under Art. V, Sec. 2 of the Bangsamoro Organic Law. Volume II will cover the remaining 11 powers: (oo) Power sector through (ccc) Water supply. Each power is a self-contained legal reference with Legal Sources tables and a 9-theme Q&A Legal Briefer following the analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.
(a)
Administration of Justice
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(a)

Power 1 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(a) Administration of Justice

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(a) —"Administration of justice;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(a) —enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes —Art. X, Sec. 1 ("in consonance with the Constitution"); Art. X, Sec. 2 ("shall form part of the Philippine judicial system subject to the supervision of the Supreme Court"); Art. X, Sec. 19 ("Regular courts ... shall continue to exercise their judicial functions, as provided by national law")
Lead Ministry/Office Bangsamoro Attorney-General's Office (BAGO) —BAA 5 (organic act); BAA 13, Book IV, Title II, Chapter 2 (Administrative Code)
Supporting Agencies Shari'ah courts (under Supreme Court supervision); Office for Tribal Justice System (BAA 13, Book III, Title V); Bangsamoro Support Fund to Judiciary (BAA 85, Ch. XXXIV)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 5 —Governance and Institutional Development
Priority Code None. Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) does not list a justice code among the 7 priority codes. However, Art. X, Sec. 17 mandates Parliament to "enact laws to promote and support the traditional or tribal justice systems."

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data —BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(a)"Administration of justice;"Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over the administration of justice within its territorial jurisdiction —encompassing the Shari'ah court system, traditional/tribal justice, and alternative dispute resolution.
Art. X, Sec. 1"The Bangsamoro justice system shall be administered in accordance with the unique cultural and historical heritage of the Bangsamoro."

"The dispensation of justice in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region shall be in consonance with the Constitution, Shari'ah, traditional or tribal laws, and other relevant laws."		Establishes a multi-source justice system —constitutional law, Shari'ah, and traditional/tribal laws all serve as legitimate sources. The two quoted passages are separate paragraphs within Sec. 1.
Art. X, Sec. 2"Shari'ah courts within the Bangsamoro territorial jurisdiction shall form part of the Philippine judicial system subject to the supervision of the Supreme Court. The regular courts within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region shall continue to function under the supervision of the Supreme Court. The Congress of the Philippines may create additional Shari'ah courts in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region upon the recommendation of the Supreme Court."Shari'ah courts are integrated into the national judiciary. The Supreme Court retains supervisory authority. Congress —not Parliament —creates additional Shari'ah courts.
Art. X, Sec. 3"The following are the sources of Shari'ah: Principal Sources: (a) Al-Qur'an or the Koran; (b) Al-Sunnah or traditions of Prophet Muhammad SAW; Secondary Sources: (a) Al-Ijma or Consensus; and (b) Al-Qiyas or Analogy."The BOL codifies the classical four-source hierarchy of Islamic law for Shari'ah court adjudication and Parliament's Shari'ah legislation.
Art. X, Sec. 4"The Parliament shall have the power to enact laws on personal, family, and property law jurisdiction. The Parliament has the power to enact laws governing commercial and other civil actions not provided for under Presidential Decree No. 1083, as amended ... and criminal jurisdiction on minor offenses punishable by arresto menor or ta'zir which must be equivalent to arresto menor or fines commensurate to the offense."Parliament's Shari'ah legislative authority: personal, family, property, commercial law (where PD 1083 is silent), and minor criminal offenses (arresto menor/ta'zir).
Art. X, Sec. 5-6"The Shari'ah Circuit Courts ... shall exercise exclusive original jurisdiction..." / "The Shari'ah District Courts ... shall exercise exclusive original jurisdiction..."Enumerates jurisdiction for Circuit Courts (6 categories) and District Courts (10 categories) with defined monetary thresholds.
Art. X, Sec. 7-9"There is hereby created within the Bangsamoro territorial jurisdiction, as part of the Philippine judicial system, a Shari'ah High Court."Creates the Shari'ah High Court —5 Justices. Exclusive appellate jurisdiction over Shari'ah District Court cases. Decisions final except questions of law appealable to the Supreme Court.
Art. X, Sec. 10-15"Justices of the Shari'ah High Court shall have the same rank, prerogatives, salaries, allowances, benefits, tenure, and privileges as the Justices of the Court of Appeals."Compensation parity, Shari'ah Rules of Court, special bar examinations, practice eligibility, SC appointment of personnel, Shari'ah Integrated Bar.
Art. X, Sec. 16"The Parliament may create a Shari'ah public assistance office, a Shari'ah special prosecution service, a Shari'ah academy, and the Office of the Jurisconsult of Islamic Law."Permissive authority to create 4 supporting institutions. The DOJ prosecution service handles Shari'ah criminal cases pending the prosecution service's creation.
Art. X, Sec. 17"The Parliament shall enact laws to promote and support the traditional or tribal justice systems that are appropriate for the indigenous peoples."Mandatory legislative mandate for traditional/tribal justice. Not yet fulfilled.
Art. X, Sec. 18"There is hereby created an Office for Tribal Justice System that shall be responsible for overseeing the study, preservation, and development of the tribal justice system within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region."The BOL directly creates the Office for Tribal Justice System.
Art. X, Sec. 19"Regular courts in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region shall continue to exercise their judicial functions, as provided by national law."Regular courts remain under the national judiciary, beyond Bangsamoro legislative authority.
Art. X, Sec. 20"The Bangsamoro Government shall adopt the principles of conciliation and mediation in settling disputes, with the Parliament enacting the necessary legislation to institute the mechanism for alternative dispute resolution."ADR mandate —Parliament must enact ADR legislation. Not yet fulfilled.

National Laws

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
PD 1083 (1977)Code of Muslim Personal LawsBOL Art. X, Sec. 4 expressly references it —Parliament's authority extends to areas "not provided for under Presidential Decree No. 1083"Directly applicable. Substantive law applied by Shari'ah courts.
BP 129 (1980)Judiciary Reorganization ActOrganizes the national court system including Shari'ah courtsDirectly applicable to regular courts in BARMM.
RA 12018 (2024)Additional Shari'ah Judicial Districts and Circuit CourtsCongress exercising Art. X, Sec. 2 authority to create additional Shari'ah courtsDirectly applicable.
RA 12304 (2025)Equitable Access to Shari'ah Courts ActImproves access to Shari'ah courts nationwideDirectly applicable.

RA 7160 (1991)Local Government CodeKatarungang Pambarangay (barangay justice) operates in BARMM LGUsDirectly applicable to BARMM LGUs.

Shari'ah Sources

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Al-Qur'an and Al-SunnahPrincipal sources of Shari'ah as codified in BOL Art. X, Sec. 3The interpretive foundation for Shari'ah court adjudication and Parliament's Shari'ah legislation.
'Adl (Justice / Equity)Central Quranic principle (Quran 4:58, 5:8, 16:90)The animating principle of the Bangsamoro justice system. Requires impartiality, proportionality, and rights protection.
Sulh (Mediation / Settlement)Islamic dispute resolution through mediation —Prophetic tradition encouraging reconciliationIslamic foundation for the ADR mandate (Art. X, Sec. 20).
Ta'zir (Discretionary Punishment)Punishment for offenses not fixed by Quran/Sunnah, at the judge's/legislature's discretionBOL limits Shari'ah criminal jurisdiction to "arresto menor or ta'zir which must be equivalent to arresto menor" —1-30 days imprisonment maximum.
Qadi SystemClassical Islamic institution of the judge appointed by the rulerThe Shari'ah court system is the modern Philippine adaptation. Judge qualifications (Art. X, Sec. 8) bridge classical and modern legal traditions.
PD 1083Code of Muslim Personal Laws (1977)The primary substantive Shari'ah legislation. Parliament's authority extends to areas not covered by PD 1083.

Jurisprudence

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically adjudicate the jurisdictional boundary between Bangsamoro administration of justice authority and the national judiciary under the BOL framework.

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over administration of justice?

A. Article V, Section 2(a) enumerates "Administration of justice" as the first of the 55 powers. This encompasses a multi-layered justice system: (1) the Shari'ah court system (Art. X, Sec. 2-16), (2) traditional or tribal justice systems (Art. X, Sec. 17-18), and (3) alternative dispute resolution (Art. X, Sec. 20). The entire Article X —20 sections —elaborates this power.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power?

A. Article X contains 20 sections organized into four clusters:

(1) Framework (Sec. 1-4): multi-source justice system, Shari'ah courts integrated into national judiciary under Supreme Court supervision, four sources of Shari'ah codified, Parliament's legislative authority over Shari'ah personal/family/property/commercial/minor criminal matters.23

(2) Court Structure (Sec. 5-15): three-tier Shari'ah court system —Circuit Courts, District Courts, Shari'ah High Court —with defined jurisdiction, qualifications, compensation parity, Shari'ah Rules of Court, special bar examinations, and the Shari'ah Integrated Bar.4

(3) Supporting Institutions (Sec. 16-18): permissive authority for 4 supporting institutions (prosecution service, public assistance office, academy, Jurisconsult); mandatory mandate for traditional/tribal justice laws; direct creation of the Office for Tribal Justice System.56

(4) Regular Courts and ADR (Sec. 19-20): regular courts continue under national law; Parliament must institutionalize ADR.7

Q. Does the BOL mandate specific legislation?

A. Yes. Two mandatory mandates: (1) Art. X, Sec. 17: "The Parliament shall enact laws to promote and support the traditional or tribal justice systems"; (2) Art. X, Sec. 20: Parliament must enact ADR legislation. One permissive authority: Art. X, Sec. 16 (4 supporting institutions). Neither mandatory mandate has been fulfilled as of BAA 89.567

Q. What bodies does the BOL create?

A. (1) Shari'ah High Court (Art. X, Sec. 7) —not yet fully organized; (2) Office for Tribal Justice System (Art. X, Sec. 18) —established under BAA 13; (3) BAGO (BAA 5 per Art. XVI, Sec. 8); (4) BTJRC (BAA 89).

Q. What limitations does the BOL place on this power?

A. Five constraints: (1) constitutional consonance (Art. X, Sec. 1); (2) Supreme Court supervision of Shari'ah courts (Art. X, Sec. 2); (3) Congress creates courts (Art. X, Sec. 2); (4) criminal jurisdiction ceiling —arresto menor/ta'zir only (Art. X, Sec. 4); (5) regular courts untouched (Art. X, Sec. 19).2378

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply?

A. (1) Art. X, Sec. 20: autonomous region constitutional framework; (2) Art. VIII (Judicial Power): Supreme Court has plenary supervisory authority over all courts; (3) Art. III, Sec. 1: due process and equal protection apply to all justice proceedings; (4) Art. III, Sec. 12, 14, 16: right to counsel, presumption of innocence, speedy trial.101112

Q. What constitutional mandates for justice institutions exist?

A. Art. VIII, Sec. 5(5) grants the Supreme Court the power to promulgate rules of procedure —the Supreme Court governs procedure in Shari'ah courts; Parliament legislates substantive Shari'ah law.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What national laws apply in BARMM?

A. All apply directly: (1) PD 1083; (2) BP 129 (court organization); (3) RA 12018 (additional Shari'ah courts); (4) RA 9285 (ADR —suppletory); (5) RA 7160 (Katarungang Pambarangay).913

Q. What national institutions exercise authority?

A. Supreme Court (plenary supervisory authority over all courts); DOJ prosecution service (Shari'ah criminal cases pending prosecution service creation); JBC (judicial appointments). These retain authority permanently.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which office is primarily responsible?

A. BAGO —BAA 5, BAA 13 Book IV, Title II, Ch. 2 (Sec. 10-24).1415

Q. What are BAGO's functions under BAA 13?

A. Functions (Sec. 13): (a) chief legal counsel to the Bangsamoro Government; (b) review/vet/interpret documents and contracts for the Chief Minister; (c) recommend actions on administrative complaints; (d) assist the Intergovernmental Relations Body; (e) other functions assigned by law.16

Powers (Sec. 14): require information from officers; summon officers; administer oaths; propose organizational changes; supervise policy formulation; appear in Parliament committees.17

Divisions (Sec. 17): (a) Litigation Division —represent government in court, initiate contract enforcement, deputize legal officers; (b) LROD —review executive issuances, draft contracts, "Formulate and review policies on Shari'ah matters to ensure compliance with Shari'ah rules and principles, and provide written Shari'ah opinions to the Chief Minister and the Cabinet"; (c) IRD; (d) AFD.18

Additional: Legal Liaison Network (Sec. 18); all ministries notify BAGO of litigation within 3 days (Sec. 22); BAGO settles disputes involving government corporations (Book V, Sec. 29); protection from personal liability (Sec. 24).19

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government exercise authority?

A. Supreme Court supervises all courts, appoints judges, promulgates Rules of Court, administers bar exams. DOJ prosecution service handles Shari'ah criminal cases. Congress creates additional Shari'ah courts (exercised through RA 12018). This power is not devolved in the traditional sense —the judiciary is constitutionally independent.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished?

A. (1) BAGO fully operationalized; (2) BAA 89 (Transitional Justice) enacted; (3) Office for Tribal Justice System established; (4) Shari'ah Public Assistance Office established; (5) Multiple resolutions urging Shari'ah court expansion and judge appointments; (6) Support Fund to Judiciary institutionalized.

Q. What is holding BARMM back?

A. (1) Shari'ah High Court not yet fully organized; (2) no Shari'ah substantive legislation enacted under Art. X, Sec. 4; (3) no traditional/tribal justice law or ADR law despite mandatory mandates.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) Insufficient Shari'ah courts despite RA 12018; (2) no Shari'ah legislation beyond PD 1083 (1977); (3) no recognition of tribal justice at the national level.

Q. What should BARMM replicate, adapt, or replace?

A. Replicate: procedural due process standards, bar examination rigor, judicial compensation parity. Adapt: Katarungang Pambarangay to integrate tribal dispute resolution and sulh. Replace: substantive law gap by enacting Shari'ah commercial/civil legislation and a Bangsamoro ADR framework integrating sulh.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes —the most significant Shari'ah dimension of any BOL power. The entire Shari'ah court system is a Shari'ah institution, and Parliament's Art. X, Sec. 4 legislative authority is the broadest Shari'ah law-making power in Philippine history.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant?

A. (1) 'Adl (justice/equity); (2) Sulh (mediation/settlement) —Islamic basis for ADR; (3) Ta'zir (discretionary punishment) —the only Shari'ah criminal penalty authorized; (4) Shura (consultation) —the legislative process for Shari'ah laws should involve ulama and the Darul Ifta'.

Q. What existing Shari'ah legislation applies?

A. PD 1083 (Code of Muslim Personal Laws) is the primary substantive Shari'ah legislation. Parliament may legislate on matters not provided for under PD 1083 —principally commercial law and minor criminal offenses.9

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge?

A. Moderate — substantively broad for Shari'ah legislation but structurally constrained. Parliament has express authority to enact Shari'ah substantive laws (Art. X, Sec. 4). Structural constraints from the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A): Supreme Court supervision (Art. X, Sec. 2: Shari'ah courts "shall be subject to the supervision of the Supreme Court"); Congress creates courts (Art. X, Sec. 3: "Congress shall create Shari'ah High Courts"); criminal jurisdiction capped at arresto menor (Art. X, Sec. 18(b)); regular courts under national law (Art. X, Sec. 1(d): "Regular courts in the Bangsamoro shall continue to be subject to the supervision of the Supreme Court and shall apply national laws"). Constitutional floor from the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E): due process, equal protection, Art. VIII judicial power. The BOL conformity language is primarily institutional retention (Supreme Court jurisdiction) and minimum standards (Philippine Bar requirement for Shari'ah judges — Art. X, Sec. 8).

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping jurisdictions?

A. (1) Shari'ah court administration (Supreme Court supervises; Parliament legislates; BAGO provides services); (2) prosecution (DOJ handles Shari'ah criminal cases pending prosecution service).

Q. What pending bills address gaps?

A. PB Nos. 170, 173, 174 (barangay justice); PB 304 (Shari'ah scholarship); PB 363 (Shari'ah academy). No comprehensive traditional justice code or ADR act has been filed.

H. Development Connection

Q. What development outcomes are at risk?

A. Without traditional/tribal justice legislation: IP dispute resolution lacks legal recognition. Without ADR legislation: Art. X, Sec. 20 remains unfulfilled. Without Shari'ah legislation under Art. X, Sec. 4: the broadest Shari'ah legislative authority in Philippine history goes unexercised. Without the Shari'ah prosecution service: DOJ handles cases it is not trained for.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What is the jurisdiction of each Shari'ah court level?

A. (1) Circuit Courts (Sec. 5): PD 1083 offenses; family disputes; communal property; ta'zir; civil claims up to P200,000/real property up to P400,000. (2) District Courts (Sec. 6): custody/guardianship; estate/probate; Muslim Registry; Shari'ah contracts; civil claims above Circuit thresholds; appellate jurisdiction. (3) High Court (Sec. 7): auxiliary writs; annulment of District Court judgments; appellate jurisdiction; final except questions of law to SC.

Q. Who appoints Shari'ah judges?

A. The Supreme Court (Art. X, Sec. 14). Qualifications (Sec. 8): Philippine citizen, Muslim, Philippine Bar member, 2+ years Shari'ah/Islamic Jurisprudence. Circuit: 30 years, 5 years practice. District: 35 years, 10 years. High Court: natural-born citizen, 40 years, 15 years.

Q. What is the scope of Parliament's ta'zir authority?

A. Limited to "minor offenses punishable by arresto menor or ta'zir which must be equivalent to arresto menor or fines commensurate to the offense" —1-30 days imprisonment maximum. No hudud penalties are within Parliament's authority.8

Q. What is the Bangsamoro Support Fund to Judiciary (BAA 85, Ch. XXXIV)?

A. Because Shari'ah courts are under Supreme Court supervision, the Bangsamoro Government cannot directly fund court operations. The Support Fund provides supplemental financial support without administrative encroachment on the Supreme Court's domain.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
No traditional/tribal justice framework Bangsamoro Traditional and Tribal Justice Act Legal recognition; jurisdiction; relationship with regular/Shari'ah courts; IP participation; Office for Tribal Justice System powers Art. X, Sec. 17
No ADR legislation Bangsamoro Alternative Dispute Resolution Act Conciliation/mediation; sulh integration; mediator accreditation; enforceability; ADR use by Shari'ah and traditional courts Art. X, Sec. 20
No Shari'ah prosecution service Bangsamoro Shari'ah Special Prosecution Service Act Organization, mandate, qualifications; relationship with DOJ; jurisdiction over ta'zir and PD 1083 offenses Art. X, Sec. 16
No Shari'ah academy Bangsamoro Shari'ah Judicial and Legal Academy Act Training for judges, lawyers, court personnel; curriculum; coordination with PHILJA; scholarships Art. X, Sec. 16
No Shari'ah commercial/civil legislation Bangsamoro Shari'ah Commercial and Civil Code Commercial law; civil actions not covered by PD 1083; ta'zir offenses Art. X, Sec. 4

Administrative Organization

Power 2 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(b) Administrative Organization

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(b) —"Administrative organization;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(b) —enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes —Art. VII, Sec. 13 ("The Parliament shall adopt the Revised Compensation and Position Classification System of the National Government as provided for in Republic Act No. 6758, as amended"); Art. XVI, Sec. 10 ("consistent with existing budgeting, organization, staffing, position classification and compensation policies, guidelines, and standards of the National Government")
Lead Ministry/Office Office of the Chief Minister (OCM); Bangsamoro Parliament (BTA)
Supporting Agencies MILG; BPDA; DAB; SAMLMG (BAA 85, Ch. XXXIII)

Legal Sources

BOL Provisions

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(b)"Administrative organization;"Authority to structure, create, reorganize, and manage the entire Bangsamoro bureaucracy.
Art. VII, Sec. 2"The powers of government shall be vested in the Parliament ... It shall set policies, legislate on matters within its authority, and elect a Chief Minister who shall exercise executive authority on its behalf."Parliamentary form —foundational organizational principle.
Art. VII, Sec. 4"The executive function and authority shall be exercised by the Cabinet which shall be headed by a Chief Minister."Cabinet system under the Chief Minister.
Art. VII, Sec. 38"The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to create offices and organize its own bureaucracy. It may establish other offices and institutions necessary for the exercise of its functions and delivery of social services."Core organizational power —express grant to create offices and organize the bureaucracy.
Art. VII, Sec. 13"The Parliament shall adopt the Revised Compensation and Position Classification System of the National Government as provided for in Republic Act No. 6758, as amended."National law constraint on compensation.
Art. VII, Sec. 39"The Bangsamoro Parliament shall enact a Bangsamoro civil service law which shall be in accordance with existing national civil service laws. In case of conflict ... the Constitution and existing national civil service laws ... shall prevail."Civil service framework —national law prevails in conflict.
Art. VII, Sec. 42"The Parliament may create other offices such as for the youth, women, settler communities, disaster risk reduction and management, and planning and development, including a Bangsamoro Pilgrimage Authority."Permissive authority for additional offices —non-exhaustive list.
Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a)"The Bangsamoro Transition Authority shall prepare and enact the following codes: An Administrative Code..."Administrative Code is a priority code. Fulfilled by BAA 13.
Art. XVI, Sec. 8"The interim Cabinet shall consist of ... fifteen (15) primary ministries..."Lists the 15 primary ministries by name.
Art. XVI, Sec. 10"...consistent with existing budgeting, organization, staffing, position classification and compensation policies, guidelines, and standards of the National Government."National consistency requirement for personnel transfer/placement.

Legal Briefer

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant over administrative organization?

A. Art. V, Sec. 2(b) enumerates "Administrative organization" as one of the 55 powers. Art. VII, Sec. 38 provides the core elaboration: "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to create offices and organize its own bureaucracy." This encompasses authority to structure, create, reorganize, and manage the entire Bangsamoro bureaucracy.12

Q. What provisions elaborate on this power?

A. Seven clusters: (1) Parliamentary system (Art. VII, Sec. 2-4); (2) organizational authority (Art. VII, Sec. 38); (3) compensation constraint (Art. VII, Sec. 13 —adopt RA 6758); (4) additional offices (Art. VII, Sec. 42); (5) transition structure (Art. XVI, Sec. 6-9 —15 ministries); (6) personnel transfer (Art. XVI, Sec. 10 —national consistency); (7) priority code (Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) —Administrative Code mandate, fulfilled by BAA 13).2345678

Q. Does the BOL mandate specific legislation?

A. Yes. Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a): "An Administrative Code" —the strongest mandate (named priority code). Fulfilled by BAA 13.8

Q. What limitations does the BOL place?

A. Three: (1) compensation must follow RA 6758 (Art. VII, Sec. 13); (2) personnel placement must be consistent with national standards (Art. XVI, Sec. 10); (3) LGU privileges under RA 7160 cannot be diminished (Art. VI, Sec. 10).479

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply?

A. (1) Art. X, Sec. 20 (autonomous region framework); (2) Art. IX-B (CSC jurisdiction over all government personnel); (3) Art. IX-D (COA audit authority). CSC and COA retain permanent authority over Bangsamoro Government entities.1011

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What national laws apply?

A. All apply: RA 6758 (directly, per BOL); RA 7160 (suppletory for LGUs); RA 6713 (directly, per BOL); RA 10149 (directly); RA 9184 (directly). National institutions CSC, COA, and DBM retain permanent authority.1213

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. What standalone legislation exists?

A. The most legislated power structurally: BAA 13 (Administrative Code); BAA 5 (BAGO); BAA 6 (BPDA/BEDC); BAA 11 (Appointment); BAA 37 (Seat of Government); BAA 49 (Local Governance Code). Every BAA creating a body exercises this power.

Q. What is the OCM structure under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book IV defines: OCM Proper (Chief Minister, Deputy CMs, Executive Offices including Senior Minister, Chief of Staff, Cabinet Secretary, BAGO); 15 primary ministries (Book V); Bangsamoro Management Support System (Sec. 25 —TMS, AMS, FMS, ISS, Procurement, Clinic, Satellite Offices, Manila Liaison, Library/Archives); attached offices (Internal Audit, ICO, Legal Division, Office for Bangsamoro Communities Outside the Region, and numerous councils/commissions/authorities).141516

D.2 How the National Government Implements

Q. How does the national government exercise authority?

A. CSC maintains a regional field office (Art. VII, Sec. 39). COA audits all entities. DBM sets organization/staffing standards per Art. XVI, Sec. 10. Administrative organization is substantially devolved —constraints are systemic, not requiring specific devolution actions.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished?

A. (1) BAA 13 enacted —most comprehensive autonomous region administrative code in Philippine history; (2) 15 ministries fully established; (3) 13+ attached offices operationalized; (4) BAA 37 (seat of government); (5) BAA 49 (Local Governance Code); (6) BAA 11 (appointment system); (7) annual GAAs funding the bureaucracy.

Q. What is holding BARMM back?

A. (1) Institutional capacity gaps especially in island provinces; (2) Bangsamoro Government Center still under development; (3) some ARMM-to-BARMM personnel placement incomplete.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What should BARMM replicate, adapt, or replace?

A. Replicate: RA 6758, RA 9184, COA standards. Adapt: organizational structures for parliamentary system, island geography, Shari'ah policy integration. Replace: presidential-bureaucracy model with parliamentary-cabinet model (already done through BAA 13).

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Indirect but foundational. Amanah (stewardship), shura (consultation), and maslahah (public interest) inform how the bureaucracy should be organized. BAA 13 integrates Shari'ah through the Darul Ifta' (Sec. 52) and BAGO's LROD Shari'ah policy review function (Sec. 17(3)(d)).

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge?

A. Broad but bounded authority to organize differently. Art. VII, Sec. 38 grants express organizational power; the BOL itself establishes a parliamentary system distinct from the national presidential system. Limits from the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E): CSC jurisdiction (Art. VII, Sec. 39 — "shall prevail"), COA authority (Art. XII, Sec. 2 — exclusive auditor), RA 6758 compensation constraint (Art. VII, Sec. 13 — salary standardization). Within these bounds, BARMM can create entities with no national counterpart (e.g., CSEA, Darul Ifta', Office for Bangsamoro Communities Outside the Region).

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping jurisdictions?

A. Personnel management: CSC retains jurisdiction (Constitution, Art. VII, Sec. 39) while Bangsamoro Government exercises organizational authority (Art. VII, Sec. 38). Resolution: Art. VII, Sec. 39 (national law prevails in conflict). LGU supervision: MILG under BAA 49 and DILG under RA 7160 —BAA 49 addresses most overlap.

Q. Pending bills?

A. PB Nos. 166, 195, 251, 321, 370, 387, 417 —addressing ministry creation, administrative systems, organizational restructuring.

H. Development Connection

Q. What development outcomes are at risk?

A. Without full institutional capacity in all ministries —especially island provinces —service delivery is compromised. Without the Government Center, efficiency suffers from dispersed operations.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. How does BAA 13 relate to the 15 ministries in Art. XVI, Sec. 8?

A. Art. XVI, Sec. 8 lists 15 interim ministries. BAA 13 codifies and elaborates these with detailed mandates, creating a bureaucracy of 28+ institutional entities beyond the 15 core ministries.

Q. What is the relationship between BAA 13 and BAA 49?

A. BAA 13 governs the regional government (ministries, OCM, attached offices). BAA 49 governs constituent LGUs (provinces, cities, municipalities, barangays). They are complementary: BAA 13 structures the regional level; BAA 49 structures the local level.

Q. Can Parliament create ministries beyond the 15?

A. Yes. Art. VII, Sec. 38 grants power to "create offices and organize its own bureaucracy" without limiting the number. Art. XVI, Sec. 8 lists the interim structure. Parliament has full authority to restructure through legislation (PB 166 reflects this).

Q. What is the DAB's role?

A. DAB (BAA 13, Sec. 47) is the "central human capital development training and research arm" —the mechanism for building bureaucratic capacity across all ministries and offices.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
No performance management framework Bangsamoro Performance Management Act SPMS for all ministries; targets; evaluation; performance-budget link Art. VII, Sec. 38; Res. No. 227
No gazette/administrative register Bangsamoro Gazette Act Mandatory publication; official repository; legal effectivity Art. VII, Sec. 38; PB 195, 251
Attached offices lack fiscal autonomy Organizational Restructuring Act Fiscal autonomy for key offices; separate plantilla Art. VII, Sec. 38; PB 387
No identification system Bangsamoro Identification Act Regional ID; data privacy; PhilSys coordination Art. VII, Sec. 38; PB 370

Agriculture, Livestock, and Food Security
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(c)

Power 3 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(c) Agriculture, Livestock, and Food Security

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(c) — "Agriculture, livestock, and food security;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(c) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes — Art. XIII, Sec. 23 requires agriculture and fisheries policies to be "consistent with the provisions of Republic Act No. 8550 ... as amended, and other relevant national laws"
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Agrarian Reform (MAFAR) — promotes sustainable agri-fishery growth and equitable land distribution (BAA 13, Book V, Title I, Sec. 2-15)
Supporting Agencies Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority (BPDA); Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT); Ministry of Environment, Natural Resources and Energy (MENRE); Bangsamoro Agriculture and Fisheries Training Institute (BAFTI, BAA 59); Bangsamoro Board of Investments (BBOI, BAA 13)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 12 (Environment and Natural Resources)
Priority Code None named in BOL — but the scope warrants a comprehensive Bangsamoro Agriculture and Food Security Code

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(c) "Agriculture, livestock, and food security;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over the full scope of agricultural governance — crop production, livestock management, and food security programs within the region.
Art. XIII, Sec. 23 "Consistent with the provisions of Republic Act No. 8550, otherwise known as 'The Philippine Fisheries Code of 1998,' as amended, and other relevant national laws, the policies and laws of the Bangsamoro Government on agriculture, fisheries, and aquatic resources shall advance agriculture as a key development strategy, promote productivity measures, and provide support for small farmers, landholders, and marginal fisherfolk. The Bangsamoro people, indigenous peoples, and resident marginal fisherfolk shall enjoy preferential fishing rights in the Bangsamoro regional waters, without prejudice to the fishing rights of other citizens of the Philippines, whether residents or nonresidents of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." The primary elaboration of this power. Imposes four obligations: (1) consistency with RA 8550 and other national laws — one of the BOL's most explicit national law consistency requirements; (2) advance agriculture as a key development strategy; (3) support for small farmers, landholders, and marginal fisherfolk; (4) preferential fishing rights for Bangsamoro people and IPs in regional waters. Note: this provision covers both agriculture AND fisheries, linking power (c) with power (y).
Art. VII, Sec. 5(f) "Enact a law within the competencies of the Bangsamoro Government to regulate the grant of regional franchises and concessions, and empower the Chief Minister to grant leases, permits, and licenses over agricultural lands and for forest management;" Parliament may legislate on agricultural land leases, permits, and licenses. Links the agriculture power to land allocation authority — enabling the Chief Minister to issue agricultural permits.
Art. XIII, Sec. 19 "The Joint Body created for the Zones of Joint Cooperation ... shall establish the policies therein and shall be composed of the following: (a) Secretary of Agriculture and a Bangsamoro Minister as co-Chairpersons..." The Joint Body for Zones of Joint Cooperation is co-chaired by the Secretary of Agriculture and a Bangsamoro Minister. Purposes include protection of traditional fishing grounds and equitable sharing of benefits — directly affecting agricultural-fisheries communities.
Art. XIII, Sec. 2 "The Bangsamoro Government shall promote the effective use of economic resources and endeavor to attain economic development that facilitates growth and full employment, human development, and social justice." General economic development framework. Agriculture is the primary means of effective use of economic resources in BARMM's predominantly rural economy.
Art. XIII, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Government shall establish an economic system based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution." General economic framework. The 1987 Constitution, Art. XIII, Sec. 4-8, mandates comprehensive agrarian reform and agricultural development — establishing a constitutional floor for BARMM agricultural policy.
Art. XII, Sec. 9(d) "Taxes, fees, or charges on agricultural and aquatic products when sold by marginal farmers or fisherfolk;" Fiscal protection — prohibits the Bangsamoro Government from taxing agricultural products sold by marginal farmers or fisherfolk. This protects the poorest agricultural producers from BARMM taxation.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Book V, Title I (MAFAR sections): establishes MAFAR's institutional mandate for agriculture, fisheries, agrarian reform, and food security. Defines 28 enumerated functions, 3 Directors-General (Agriculture, Fisheries, Agrarian Reform), and the Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Adjudication Board. In Force
BAA 59 — Bangsamoro Agriculture and Fisheries Training Institute (BAFTI) Creates BAFTI for specialized training of farmers and fisherfolk. The only standalone BAA directly implementing the agriculture power. In Force
BAA 85 — General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter XVI allocates budget to MAFAR, confirming its institutional existence and operational funding. In Force
No comprehensive Bangsamoro agriculture code or food security act has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 221 — Directing MAFAR to apprise Parliament on coconut industry status Signals Parliament's interest in monitoring a key agricultural subsector — coconut is a major crop in BARMM's island provinces
Res. No. 222 — Directing MAFAR to modify procurement of motorized banca Addresses procurement issues for fishery/agriculture equipment critical to fisherfolk livelihoods

National Laws

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 8550 (1998), as amended by RA 10654 (2014) Philippine Fisheries Code of 1998 BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 23 expressly requires consistency. Sets the national framework for fisheries management, conservation, and regulation. Directly applicable: BARMM agriculture and fisheries legislation must be consistent with its provisions. MAFAR implements within BARMM.
RA 8435 (1997) Agriculture and Fisheries Modernization Act (AFMA) National framework for agricultural modernization — infrastructure, credit, R&D, trade, extension services. Suppletory: applies in BARMM pending a comprehensive Bangsamoro agriculture code. Provides the modernization framework currently applicable to MAFAR programs.
RA 6657 (1988), as amended by RA 9700 (2009) Comprehensive Agrarian Reform Law (CARL/CARPER) National agrarian reform framework — land distribution, support services, beneficiary development. Suppletory: DAR retains national authority over agrarian reform. MAFAR exercises agrarian reform functions within BARMM per BAA 13, including issuance of CLOAs (Sec. 3(u)).
RA 10068 (2010), as amended by RA 11511 (2020) Organic Agriculture Act of 2010 Promotes organic agriculture; establishes National Organic Agriculture Program. Suppletory: applies in BARMM. A Bangsamoro agriculture code could adopt region-specific organic farming standards.
RA 7900 (1995) High-Value Crops Development Act Promotes high-value crop production and agribusiness development. Suppletory: provides incentives for crop diversification applicable in BARMM.
RA 10611 (2013) Food Safety Act of 2013 Establishes food safety standards and regulatory framework nationwide. Suppletory: applies in BARMM. MAFAR coordinates with FDA and DA on food safety compliance.
RA 11321 (2019) Sagip Saka Act Links farmers to institutional markets through cooperatives and associations. Suppletory: applies in BARMM for farm-to-market linkage programs.
RA 11203 (2019) Rice Tariffication Law Liberalizes rice importation; creates Rice Competitiveness Enhancement Fund (RCEF) for Filipino rice farmers. Directly applicable: rice farmers in BARMM are eligible for RCEF support. MAFAR coordinates with DA for rice programs.
RA 11901 (2022) Agriculture, Fisheries, and Rural Development Financing Act Strengthens financing system for agriculture and fisheries — cooperatives as key conduit for rural credit. Repeals RA 10000. Suppletory: applies to BARMM agricultural credit and financing programs.
RA 9168 (2002) Philippine Plant Variety Protection Act Protects new plant varieties; establishes National Plant Variety Protection Board. Suppletory: national plant variety protection framework applies in BARMM.
PD 1083 (1977) Code of Muslim Personal Laws Governs property relations including agricultural land under Islamic law — inheritance, gifts (*hibah*), endowments (*waqf*). Directly applicable to Muslim agricultural landholders. Relevant to agrarian reform interactions with Islamic property law.

Shari'ah Sources

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Muzara'ah (Sharecropping) Islamic agricultural contract where the landowner provides land and the farmer provides labor; harvest is shared according to an agreed ratio. The predominant traditional farming arrangement in BARMM's rural areas. A Bangsamoro agrarian reform act must recognize muzara'ah alongside CARP-based tenurial arrangements — many Muslim farmers hold land through traditional Islamic tenure rather than Torrens titles.
Musaqah (Irrigation Partnership) Contract where one party provides an orchard or agricultural land with existing crops and another undertakes irrigation and tending; produce is shared. Applicable to BARMM's coconut, palm oil, and fruit orchards. Provides a Shari'ah-compliant alternative to conventional farm management contracts.
Waqf (Endowment) Irrevocable dedication of property for charitable or religious purposes under Islamic law. Waqf agricultural land cannot be sold, gifted, or inherited — its usufruct serves the designated purpose perpetually. Significant portions of agricultural land in BARMM are waqf-designated. A Bangsamoro agrarian reform act must address the intersection of waqf and CARP — waqf agricultural land may be exempt from compulsory acquisition under agrarian reform because its inalienability derives from Islamic law recognized under PD 1083.
Prohibition of Riba (Interest/Usury) All forms of predetermined return on money lent are prohibited. Agricultural credit and microfinance in BARMM must provide Shari'ah-compliant alternatives — *murabaha* (cost-plus) for input procurement, *salam* (forward sale) for crop financing — so Muslim farmers can access credit without violating the riba prohibition.
Halal Standards Islamic dietary requirements governing the production, processing, and sale of food — including slaughter methods, ingredient prohibitions, and contamination prevention. Livestock production and food processing in BARMM must accommodate halal standards. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 30 establishes a halal program and accreditation of halal-certifying bodies. BAA 13 creates the Bangsamoro Halal Board with MAFAR participation (Sec. 36).
Maslahah (Public Interest / Welfare) Legislation that serves the common good is justified under Islamic jurisprudence. Food security legislation serves maslahah — ensuring adequate, accessible, and affordable food supply is a fundamental public welfare obligation in Islamic governance.

Jurisprudence

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro agricultural authority or the boundary between MAFAR jurisdiction and national DA regulatory authority. Agricultural disputes in BARMM have historically been resolved under national agrarian reform and fisheries frameworks. The leading jurisprudence on CARP coverage and exemptions (e.g., Hacienda Luisita cases) applies to BARMM agrarian reform through the suppletory application of RA 6657.

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development Agriculture identified as the backbone of the Bangsamoro economy; targets increased productivity, crop diversification, and value-chain development
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 12: Environment and Natural Resources Sustainable agriculture, climate-resilient farming, and integrated watershed management
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience Food security and agricultural productivity as foundations of economic resilience; reducing dependence on imported food

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over agriculture?

A. Article V, Section 2(c) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "agriculture, livestock, and food security" as one of the 55 powers. This is a broad grant covering crop production, livestock management, food security programs, and related agricultural governance within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Four provisions elaborate:

(1) Art. XIII, Sec. 23 is the primary elaboration. It directs the Bangsamoro Government's agriculture and fisheries policies to "advance agriculture as a key development strategy, promote productivity measures, and provide support for small farmers, landholders, and marginal fisherfolk." It also establishes preferential fishing rights for Bangsamoro people, indigenous peoples, and resident marginal fisherfolk in regional waters. Critically, it imposes a national law consistency requirement: "Consistent with the provisions of Republic Act No. 8550 ... as amended, and other relevant national laws."2

(2) Art. VII, Sec. 5(f) authorizes Parliament to "enact a law ... to regulate the grant of regional franchises and concessions, and empower the Chief Minister to grant leases, permits, and licenses over agricultural lands." This links the agriculture power to land allocation authority.3

(3) Art. XIII, Sec. 19 establishes the Joint Body for Zones of Joint Cooperation co-chaired by the Secretary of Agriculture and a Bangsamoro Minister — addressing fishing grounds and resource-sharing in the Sulu Sea and Moro Gulf.4

(4) Art. XII, Sec. 9(d) provides fiscal protection by excluding from the Bangsamoro taxing power: "Taxes, fees, or charges on agricultural and aquatic products when sold by marginal farmers or fisherfolk."5

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation?

A. The BOL does not name a specific "agriculture code" by title. However, Art. XIII, Sec. 23 contemplates "policies and laws of the Bangsamoro Government on agriculture, fisheries, and aquatic resources" — implying legislative action. Art. VII, Sec. 5(f) explicitly mandates Parliament to "enact a law" on agricultural land leases and permits. The combined effect is a strong legislative obligation — though no named code is mandated.23

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body for this power?

A. The BOL creates the Joint Body for Zones of Joint Cooperation (Art. XIII, Sec. 19) co-chaired by the Secretary of Agriculture and a Bangsamoro Minister. MAFAR itself was established through BAA 13 (Administrative Code) as the lead ministry, receiving budget allocation under BAA 85, Chapter XVI. BAFTI (BAA 59) is the dedicated training institution.467

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Three express limitations:

(1) National law consistency: Art. XIII, Sec. 23 requires consistency with "Republic Act No. 8550 ... as amended, and other relevant national laws" — one of the BOL's most explicit consistency requirements.2

(2) Constitutional floor: Art. XIII, Sec. 1 requires the economic system to be "based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution" — including the constitutional mandate for agrarian reform (Art. XIII, Sec. 4-8 of the Constitution).8

(3) Fiscal limitation: Art. XII, Sec. 9(d) prohibits taxing agricultural products sold by marginal farmers or fisherfolk.5

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to agriculture?

A. Four categories:

(1) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): The universal constitutional boundary.10

(2) Agrarian Reform (Art. XIII, Sec. 4-8): The Constitution mandates "a comprehensive agrarian reform program" to promote social justice. Art. XIII, Sec. 4 states: "The State shall, by law, undertake an agrarian reform program founded on the right of farmers and regular farmworkers ... to own directly or collectively the lands they till." This is a constitutional mandate that applies in BARMM — any Bangsamoro agrarian reform must meet this floor.11

(3) Social Justice (Art. XIII, Sec. 1): "The Congress shall give highest priority to the enactment of measures that protect and enhance the right of all the people to human dignity, reduce social, economic, and political inequalities." Agriculture is a primary vehicle for social justice in BARMM.12

(4) Right to Food and Basic Services: While the Constitution does not expressly guarantee a "right to food," Art. II, Sec. 9 (social justice), Art. XIII, Sec. 1-2 (social justice and human rights), and Art. II, Sec. 18 (labor and employment) collectively support food security as a state obligation.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for agriculture?

A. The national framework is comprehensive: RA 8550 (Fisheries Code) governs fisheries management; RA 8435 (AFMA) provides the agricultural modernization framework — infrastructure, credit, R&D, trade, and extension services; RA 6657/9700 (CARL/CARPER) governs agrarian reform; RA 10068/11511 (Organic Agriculture Act) promotes organic farming; RA 10611 (Food Safety Act) establishes food safety standards; RA 11321 (Sagip Saka Act) links farmers to institutional markets; RA 11203 (Rice Tariffication Law) liberalizes rice trade and funds the Rice Competitiveness Enhancement Fund; RA 11901 strengthens agricultural financing. Together, these provide comprehensive regulatory standards, institutional frameworks, financing mechanisms, and market linkage systems.1314151617181920

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. All national agricultural laws apply in the absence of a comprehensive Bangsamoro agriculture code:

(1) RA 8550 — applies directly by express BOL mandate (Art. XIII, Sec. 23).13 (2) RA 8435 — suppletory: agricultural modernization framework.14 (3) RA 6657/9700 — suppletory: agrarian reform. DAR retains national authority; MAFAR exercises delegated functions within BARMM.15 (4) RA 10068/11511 — suppletory: organic agriculture framework.16 (5) RA 10611 — suppletory: food safety standards.17 (6) RA 11203 — directly applicable: rice farmers eligible for RCEF.19 (7) RA 11901 — suppletory: agricultural financing.20

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM?

A. (1) Department of Agriculture (DA) — retains regulatory authority over quarantine, plant health, and animal disease control; co-chairs the Joint Body for Zones of Joint Cooperation with MAFAR; implements nationally-funded agricultural programs. (2) Department of Agrarian Reform (DAR) — retains authority over agrarian reform case adjudication and national policy; MAFAR exercises delegated functions per BAA 13. (3) Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources (BFAR) — national fisheries regulatory functions apply under RA 8550 consistency requirement.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry is primarily responsible?

A. MAFAR is the lead agency. BAA 13, Book V, Title I, Chapter 1, Sec. 2 defines its mandate: "The Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Agrarian Reform and its attached agencies and bureaus shall promote sustainable Agri-fishery growth and development, and equitable land distribution and tenurial security for the benefit of the landless farmers in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." It further mandates MAFAR to "ensure the availability, adequacy, accessibility, and affordability of food supplies at all times in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." BAA 85, Chapter XVI allocates its operational budget.67

Q. What standalone legislation has Parliament enacted?

A. BAA 59 (BAFTI) is the only standalone BAA directly implementing the agriculture power. It creates the Bangsamoro Agriculture and Fisheries Training Institute for specialized farmer and fisherfolk training. No comprehensive agriculture code has been enacted.

Q. What are MAFAR's mandate and functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book V, Title I, Chapter 1, Sec. 3 enumerates 28 functions organized across agriculture, fisheries, and agrarian reform:

Agriculture functions (Sec. 3(a)-(h)): - (a) "Provide integrated services to farmers, fishermen, and other food producers on production, utilization, conservation, and disposition of agricultural livestock and fishery resources;" - (b) "Plan, formulate, execute, regulate and monitor programs and activities relating to agriculture, food production, and supply;" - (c) "Promulgate and enforce all laws, rules and regulations governing the conservation and proper utilization of agricultural, livestock, and fishery resources;" - (d) "Establish central and regional information systems to serve the production, marketing, and financing data requirements of the farmers;" - (e) "Provide comprehensive and effective extension services and training to farmers;" - (f) "Conduct, coordinate, and disseminate research studies on appropriate technologies;" - (g) "Provide the mechanisms for the participation of farmers, fishermen and agricultural entrepreneurs at all levels of policy-making;" - (h) "Coordinate with and enlist other public and private agencies for cooperation and assistance"6

Fisheries functions (Sec. 3(i)-(n)): - (i) "Issue clearances and permits for the importation and exportation of agricultural and fishery commodities;" - (j) "Approve charter contracts, issue permits for Special Commercial Fishing Boats and Licenses for Commercial Fishing Vessels;" - (k) "Determine and designate fish landing points;" - (l) "Assist LGUs in the management and conservation of their municipal waters;" - (m) "Regulate and supervise the production, capture, and gathering of fish and fishery/aquatic products;" - (n) "Designate areas in the BARMM as fishery reserves"6

Agrarian reform functions (Sec. 3(o)-(ab)): - (o) "Implement all agrarian laws" with subpoena and contempt authority; - (p)-(q) Advise on agrarian reform policy and establish operational rules; - (r)-(t) Coordinate with LBP; acquire, administer, distribute agricultural lands; conduct surveys; - (u) "Issue Certificates of Land Ownership Awards (CLOA) and emancipation patents;" - (v) "Provide free legal services to agrarian reform beneficiaries;" - (w)-(y) Promote cooperatives; conduct continuing education; institutionalize farmer participation; - (z) "Exercise exclusive authority to approve or disapprove conversion of agricultural lands;"6

Organizational structure (Sec. 7-15): Three Directors-General (Agriculture, Fisheries, Agrarian Reform), each with specialized services. The Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Adjudication Board (Sec. 12) adjudicates agrarian reform cases. Provincial and Municipal offices extend MAFAR's reach to the field level (Sec. 13-14).6

Q. What can MAFAR NOT do without a standalone agriculture code?

A. Without a comprehensive code, MAFAR cannot: (1) establish Bangsamoro-specific agricultural regulatory standards — pesticide/fertilizer regulations, agricultural product quality standards, livestock health codes with legal enforcement mechanisms; (2) create a Bangsamoro agrarian reform framework addressing the unique intersection of CARP, ancestral domain, and Islamic land tenure (waqf, muzara'ah); (3) establish crop insurance and Shari'ah-compliant agricultural credit programs with statutory authority; (4) create food security institutional mechanisms with enforceable targets and accountability; (5) define the jurisdictional boundary with DA and DAR — particularly on regulatory functions and agrarian reform adjudication.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is MAFAR the correct lead institution?

A. Yes. MAFAR's mandate is comprehensive — covering agriculture, fisheries, AND agrarian reform in a single ministry. This integrated structure is an advantage over the national system where DA, DAR, and BFAR are separate agencies. However, the scope is enormous — three Directors-General, provincial and municipal offices, an adjudication board — making institutional capacity a significant challenge.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist?

A. (1) MAFAR — lead ministry (BAA 13, BAA 85 Ch. XVI); (2) BAFTI — training institute (BAA 59); (3) Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Adjudication Board — adjudicates agrarian cases (BAA 13, Sec. 12); (4) Bangsamoro Halal Board — MAFAR participates in halal certification (BAA 13); (5) Bangsamoro Board of Investments (BBOI) — MAFAR Minister is an ex officio member (BAA 13, Sec. 28); (6) Joint Body for Zones of Joint Cooperation — intergovernmental body co-chaired by DA Secretary and MAFAR Minister (Art. XIII, Sec. 19).

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. Three national agencies maintain significant presence: (1) DA — implements nationally-funded agricultural programs (RCEF under RA 11203, AFMA infrastructure projects); regulatory authority over plant quarantine, animal disease control, and veterinary drug regulation; co-chairs the Zones of Joint Cooperation Joint Body; (2) DAR — retains national agrarian reform policy authority; MAFAR exercises delegated functions but DAR maintains oversight of CLOA issuance and land valuation; (3) BFAR — national fisheries regulation under RA 8550, which BARMM must comply with.

Q. What is the status of devolution?

A. Devolution is partially complete. MAFAR exercises agricultural extension, fisheries management, and agrarian reform functions within BARMM. However, DA regulatory functions (quarantine, plant health, animal disease control) have not been fully devolved. DAR retains policy authority over agrarian reform nationally. The devolution of specific functions — particularly regulatory and adjudicatory — depends on BARMM enacting comprehensive legislation that can replace the suppletory application of national laws.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished?

A. (1) MAFAR operational with three Directors-General, provincial offices across all BARMM provinces, and municipal offices; (2) BAFTI established (BAA 59) for farmer and fisherfolk training; (3) Agricultural extension services delivered — input distribution, livestock development, crop production programs; (4) Agrarian reform functions exercised — CLOA issuance, land surveys, beneficiary development; (5) Fisheries regulation — licensing of commercial fishing vessels, management of municipal waters; (6) Parliament has signaled legislative intent through 7 filed bills (PB 184, 221, 244, 249, 260, 289, 291) and 2 resolutions (Res. 221, 222).

Q. Where is BARMM stronger than the national government?

A. BARMM has two unique advantages: (1) Integrated agriculture-fisheries-agrarian reform ministry — MAFAR combines functions that are split among DA, DAR, and BFAR nationally, enabling coordinated policy; (2) Conflict-sensitive agricultural programming — MAFAR can design programs for IDP farming communities, ex-combatant livelihood cooperatives, and disaster-recovery agriculture that the national system cannot.

Q. What is holding BARMM back?

A. The primary constraint is the absence of a comprehensive Bangsamoro Agriculture and Food Security Code. Without it, MAFAR cannot independently regulate agricultural standards, establish Shari'ah-compliant agricultural credit, address the waqf-CARP tension, or create enforceable food security targets. The secondary constraint is incomplete devolution — DA regulatory functions and DAR policy authority have not been fully transferred.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built that BARMM does not yet have?

A. (1) Legislative depth — RA 8435 (AFMA), RA 8550 (Fisheries Code), RA 6657/9700 (CARP/CARPER), and 10+ supporting laws provide a comprehensive regulatory framework with decades of implementing rules and jurisprudence; (2) Institutional infrastructure — DA has regional offices, research centers (PhilRice, PCAARRD), and field stations nationwide; DAR has provincial agrarian reform officers in every province; (3) Regulatory systems — quarantine protocols, plant variety protection database, livestock disease surveillance, food safety inspection; (4) Financing mechanisms — RCEF (RA 11203), agricultural credit guarantee (RA 11901), crop insurance (PCIC).

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) No Shari'ah-compliant agricultural financing — all national agricultural credit programs use conventional interest-bearing instruments; (2) No recognition of Islamic land tenure — CARP does not account for waqf, muzara'ah, or Islamic inheritance patterns; (3) No conflict-sensitive agricultural framework — national programs are designed for stable agricultural communities, not post-conflict environments; (4) Language and cultural barriers — DA extension materials are primarily in Filipino and English, not in Maguindanaon, Maranao, Tausug, Sama, or Yakan.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes. Agriculture in BARMM has a significant Shari'ah dimension in three areas: (1) Islamic land tenure — muzara'ah (sharecropping), musaqah (irrigation partnership), and waqf (endowment) are traditional agricultural arrangements that coexist with and sometimes conflict with the national agrarian reform framework; (2) Agricultural finance — the prohibition of riba (interest) means conventional agricultural credit is not Shari'ah-compliant; alternatives such as salam (forward sale) and murabaha (cost-plus) financing are needed; (3) Halal standards — BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 30 establishes a halal program; BAA 13 creates the Bangsamoro Halal Board with MAFAR participation; livestock production must accommodate halal slaughter requirements.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area?

A. Five principles directly apply:

(1) Muzara'ah — the predominant traditional farming arrangement. A Bangsamoro agrarian reform act must recognize this alongside CARP-based tenure.

(2) Waqf — agricultural land dedicated as waqf cannot be sold or redistributed under agrarian reform. This creates a direct tension with CARP compulsory acquisition.

(3) Prohibition of riba — agricultural credit must include Shari'ah-compliant alternatives (salam, murabaha).

(4) Halal standards — livestock production, food processing, and the food supply chain must accommodate Islamic dietary requirements.

(5) Maslahah — food security is a fundamental public welfare obligation.

Q. What existing Shari'ah legislation applies?

A. PD 1083 (Code of Muslim Personal Laws) governs property relations for Muslims — including inheritance of agricultural land, waqf endowments, and gifts (hibah). Its property provisions directly affect how agricultural land is held, transferred, and distributed among Muslim landholders. Shari'ah courts have jurisdiction over property disputes among Muslims — agricultural land disputes between Muslim parties could fall within this jurisdiction. No specific fatawa on agricultural contracts have been identified from the Bangsamoro Darul Ifta', though the principles of muzara'ah and musaqah are well-established in classical Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh).9

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge from national agricultural law?

A. BARMM's divergence is moderately constrained by the express consistency requirement. As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), Art. XIII, Sec. 23 requires consistency with "Republic Act No. 8550 ... as amended, and other relevant national laws." This is broader than most BOL consistency requirements — it references "other relevant national laws" generally, not just RA 8550. As established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), the constitutional agrarian reform mandate (Art. XIII, Sec. 4-8) sets the floor. BARMM cannot: enact fisheries regulations that contradict RA 8550; exempt agricultural lands from agrarian reform coverage (constitutional mandate); or lower food safety standards below national minimums. BARMM can: add Shari'ah-compliant agricultural credit as an alternative; recognize Islamic land tenure alongside CARP; establish region-specific crop insurance; create halal-specific livestock and food processing standards; and design agricultural programs for conflict-affected communities.

Q. If BARMM enacts different standards, what are the implications?

A. Agricultural products from BARMM must meet national food safety standards (RA 10611) to access national markets. BARMM halal standards would be additional requirements, not replacements for national standards. Farmers holding CLOAs under national CARP continue to be protected by RA 6657 regardless of any Bangsamoro agrarian reform act. Cross-boundary implications are significant for fisheries — the Zones of Joint Cooperation (Art. XIII, Sec. 18-21) require coordinated regulation between BARMM and the national government.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions?

A. Yes. Three overlaps: (1) MAFAR vs. DA — regulatory authority over quarantine, plant health, and animal disease control within BARMM is unclear; (2) MAFAR vs. DAR — agrarian reform adjudication authority is shared between the Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Adjudication Board (BAA 13, Sec. 12) and DAR's national adjudication system; (3) Fisheries regulation — MAFAR regulates fishing within BARMM waters, but the RA 8550 consistency requirement means BFAR standards apply.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. Seven bills address parts of the gap: PB 260 (agricultural modernization), PB 184 (agricultural microfinance), PB 289 (agri-fishery worker social protection), PB 249 (veterinary services), PB 291 (agricultural education), PB 244 (fruit-bearing trees), PB 221 (agricultural engineering bureau). However, no comprehensive agriculture and food security code has been filed — these are sectoral bills that address fragments of the gap.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP goals and Matatag pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 7 (Economic Development) identifies agriculture as the backbone of the Bangsamoro economy — the sector employing the majority of the region's workforce. Targets include increased agricultural productivity, crop diversification, and value-chain development. Chapter 12 (Environment and Natural Resources) addresses sustainable agriculture, climate-resilient farming, and integrated watershed management. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028), Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience), prioritizes food security and agricultural productivity as foundations of economic resilience — reducing dependence on imported food.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if gaps remain?

A. Without a comprehensive agriculture code: MAFAR lacks the regulatory authority to enforce agricultural standards, regulate markets, or create institutional architecture that transforms subsistence farming into a productive sector. Agricultural cooperatives — critical for food security in conflict-affected areas — operate without a region-specific support framework. Shari'ah-compliant agricultural credit remains unavailable, excluding observant Muslim farmers from formal financing. The waqf-CARP intersection remains unresolved, creating legal uncertainty for Muslim landholders. The BDP targets for agricultural productivity depend on institutional infrastructure that BAA 13's general mandate alone cannot provide.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. How does this power interact with power (y) Fishery, marine, and aquatic resources?

A. The BOL treats agriculture and fisheries together in Art. XIII, Sec. 23. MAFAR has jurisdiction over both (BAA 13 includes a Bangsamoro Director-General for Fisheries). The consistency requirement with RA 8550 technically applies to fisheries, but Art. XIII, Sec. 23 also covers agricultural policy. The Zones of Joint Cooperation (Art. XIII, Sec. 18-21) primarily affect fisheries but indirectly impact agricultural fisherfolk communities.

Q. How does the waqf-CARP conflict affect agrarian reform in BARMM?

A. Waqf agricultural land is inalienable under Islamic law — it cannot be sold, gifted, or inherited as personal property. CARP (RA 6657) authorizes compulsory acquisition of agricultural land for redistribution. The conflict arises when waqf agricultural land falls within CARP coverage — can the government compel redistribution of waqf? PD 1083 recognizes waqf under Muslim personal law. The BOL does not expressly resolve this conflict. A Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Act should address this by: (a) recognizing waqf as an exemption or special category under BARMM agrarian reform; (b) providing alternative mechanisms for waqf beneficiaries (e.g., stewardship leases rather than ownership transfer); (c) coordinating with DAR on cases where waqf designation is contested.9

Q. What is the significance of halal standards for this power?

A. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 30 establishes a halal program and accreditation of halal-certifying bodies. BAA 13 creates the Bangsamoro Halal Board (Sec. 35-36) with MAFAR as a key participant — the MAFAR Minister serves as Vice Chairperson (Sec. 35(b)). Halal standards directly affect livestock production (slaughter methods), food processing (ingredient compliance), and the food supply chain. A comprehensive agriculture code should integrate halal standards into the livestock and food safety regulatory framework, rather than treating halal as a separate program.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap Type Gap What Law / Institution Should Be Created What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No comprehensive agriculture code despite agriculture being the primary livelihood sector Bangsamoro Agriculture and Food Security Act Agricultural modernization strategies; crop insurance and Shari'ah-compliant agricultural credit (salam, murabaha); livestock development and halal standards; food security programs with enforceable targets; market linkage and value chain development; MAFAR regulatory authority; agricultural extension framework; pesticide and fertilizer regulation Art. V, Sec. 2(c); Art. XIII, Sec. 23
Legislative No Bangsamoro-specific agrarian reform framework addressing Islamic land tenure Bangsamoro Agrarian Reform Act Land distribution adapted to waqf, muzara'ah, and Islamic inheritance patterns; waqf exemption/special category; support services for agrarian reform beneficiaries; resolution of overlapping land claims (ancestral domain, Torrens title, Islamic property); agricultural leasehold regulation per Art. VII, Sec. 5(f) Art. V, Sec. 2(c); Art. VII, Sec. 5(f)
Legislative No agricultural credit and insurance legislation with Shari'ah-compliant options Bangsamoro Agricultural Credit and Insurance Act Salam-based crop financing; murabaha-based input procurement credit; crop and livestock insurance; microfinance for small farmers; linkage with Islamic banking system (Art. V, Sec. 2(ii)) Art. V, Sec. 2(c); Art. XIII, Sec. 23
Legislative No livestock and veterinary code with halal integration Bangsamoro Livestock and Animal Health Act Animal health standards; halal slaughter regulation integrated with Art. XIII, Sec. 30 halal program; veterinary services framework; livestock registration; disease control and quarantine protocols Art. V, Sec. 2(c); Art. XIII, Sec. 30
