(d)
Ancestral Domain and Natural Resources
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(d)

Power 4 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(d) Ancestral Domain and Natural Resources

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(d) — "Ancestral domain and natural resources;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(d) — enumerated power; Art. X, Sec. 20(3) of the 1987 Constitution expressly lists "Ancestral domain and natural resources" as one of the legislative powers the organic act may provide for autonomous regions
National Law Consistency Required Yes — Art. XIII, Sec. 8 ("Subject to the provisions of the Constitution"); Art. XIII, Sec. 10 (fossil fuels and uranium require "joint exercise" with National Government, "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws"); Art. XIII, Sec. 13 (mines and minerals "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws")
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Environment, Natural Resources and Energy (MENRE) — BAA 13, Book VI, Title III, Ch. 1-4
Supporting Agencies Ministry of Indigenous Peoples' Affairs (MIAP) — ancestral domain, native titles, CADT issuance; Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Agrarian Reform (MAFAR) — agricultural land and fishery natural resources; Bangsamoro Sustainable Development Board (Art. VI, Sec. 8) — natural resource oversight
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 12 (Environment and Natural Resources); Chapter 7 (Economic Development — mining revenue); Chapter 10 (Peace and Security — IP rights and ancestral domain)
Priority Code —

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(d) "Ancestral domain and natural resources;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over both ancestral domain and natural resources as a single enumerated power — one of the broadest powers in the BOL, touching land, minerals, forests, waters, and indigenous heritage.
Art. XIII, Sec. 8 "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution, the Bangsamoro Government shall have the power, authority, and right to explore, develop, and utilize the natural resources, including surface and subsurface rights, inland waters, coastal waters, and renewable and nonrenewable resources in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." Core natural resources grant — subject to the Constitution (Regalian doctrine, state ownership of natural resources under Art. XII, Sec. 2 of the 1987 Constitution). Also mandates priority for protection, conservation, rehabilitation and development of forests, coastal and marine resources and power to declare nature reserves and protected areas.
Art. XIII, Sec. 9 "The management and protection of nature reserves and aquatic parks, forests, watershed reservations, and other protected areas in the territorial jurisdiction of the Bangsamoro that have already been defined by and under the authority of the National Government shall be transferred to the Bangsamoro Government." Transfer mandate — requires DENR to transfer management of existing protected areas to BARMM within two years of BTA establishment.
Art. XIII, Sec. 10 "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws, the Bangsamoro Government and the National Government shall jointly exercise the power to grant rights, privileges, and concessions over the exploration, development, and utilization of uranium and fossil fuels such as petroleum, natural gas, and coal in the territorial jurisdiction of the Bangsamoro." Joint exercise requirement for fossil fuels and uranium — DOE and Bangsamoro Government must jointly identify contract areas, evaluate proponents (with preferential rating for bona fide residents), and jointly award service contracts.
Art. XIII, Sec. 11 "Qualified citizens who are bona fide residents of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, all other conditions being equal, shall have preferential rights over the exploration, development, and utilization of natural resources" Preferential rights of bona fide residents in natural resource access. Existing rights (leases, permits, concessions) are respected until expiration.
Art. XIII, Sec. 12 "The Parliament shall enact a law recognizing the rights of indigenous peoples in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in relation to natural resources within the areas covered by a native title, including their share in revenues as provided in this Organic Law, and priority rights in the exploration, development, and utilization of such natural resources within their area." Express legislative mandate for IP natural resource rights — now addressed through BAA 64. Requires free, prior and informed consent (FPIC) for development within ancestral domains covered by CADTs.
Art. XIII, Sec. 13 "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws, the Bangsamoro Government shall have the authority and jurisdiction over the exploration, development, and utilization of mines and minerals in its territorial jurisdiction, taking into consideration environmental protection and ecological balance." Authority over mines and minerals — power to grant permits, licenses, and contracts. Subject to both Constitution and national laws.
Art. XIII, Sec. 14 "The Bangsamoro Government may endorse to the President financial and technical agreements covering mineral resources in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, in accordance with the mining policy that shall be adopted by the Parliament." FTAA endorsement power — the Bangsamoro Government endorses FTAAs to the President, not enters them directly. Requires prior enactment of a Bangsamoro mining policy.
Art. XIII, Sec. 15 "Small-scale mining shall be regulated by the Bangsamoro Government to the end that the ecological balance, safety and health, and the interests of the affected communities, the miners, the indigenous peoples, and the local government units of the place where such operations are conducted are duly protected and safeguarded." Small-scale mining regulation — Bangsamoro Government regulates directly. All gold produced must be sold to BSP.
Art. XIII, Sec. 16 "Policies on mining and other extractive industries shall be established by the Parliament in accordance with a comprehensive sustainable development plan and overall medium-term and long-term Bangsamoro Development Plan." Parliament must adopt a Bangsamoro Mining Policy aligned with BDP. This is a prerequisite for FTAA endorsement under Sec. 14.
Art. IX, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Parliament, taking into account the report of the Transitional Justice and Reconciliation Commission, shall enact a transitional justice mechanism to address the legitimate grievances of the Bangsamoro people and the indigenous peoples, such as historical injustices, human rights violations, and marginalization through unjust dispossession of territorial and proprietary rights and customary land tenure." Ancestral domain restitution — transitional justice must address unjust dispossession of territorial rights. Now addressed through BAA 89 (Transitional Justice and Reconciliation).
Art. IX, Sec. 3 "The Bangsamoro Government recognizes the rights of the indigenous peoples and shall adopt measures for the promotion and protection of the following rights: (a) Native titles or fusaka inged; ... (d) Equitable share in revenues from the utilization of resources in their ancestral lands; ..." Eight enumerated IP rights including native titles (fusaka inged), revenue share from natural resources, political participation, and cultural protection. MIAP is the primary ministry. IPRA (RA 8371) rights shall not be diminished.
Art. XII, Sec. 34-36 "Government revenues generated from the exploration, development, and utilization of all natural resources in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, including mines and minerals, shall pertain fully to the Bangsamoro Government." (Sec. 34) Fossil fuels/uranium revenues shared equally. Constituent LGU shares: 30% BARMM, 20% provinces, 15% cities, 20% municipalities, 15% barangays (Sec. 35). Indigenous communities receive equitable share per law to be enacted by Parliament (Sec. 36). Revenue sharing framework — natural resource revenues pertain fully to BARMM (except fossil fuels/uranium, shared equally with National Government). Internal distribution formula in Sec. 35. IP share in Sec. 36 requires implementing legislation.
Art. VI, Sec. 8 "The Parliament shall create a Bangsamoro Sustainable Development Board composed of representatives from the National Government and the Bangsamoro Government." Intergovernmental body for sustainable natural resource management — created through BAA 31.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Establishes MENRE (Book VI, Title III) with 19 enumerated powers and functions including natural resource exploration, management, conservation, land surveys, mining regulation, and energy development. Establishes MIAP (Book VI, Title VII) with powers over ancestral domain, CADT issuance, and IP well-being. In Force
BAA 31 — Sustainable Development Board Creates the Bangsamoro Sustainable Development Board per Art. VI, Sec. 8, integrating environmental, natural resources, and sustainable development governance In Force
BAA 64 PB No. 273 Indigenous Peoples' Act Comprehensive IP rights law addressing ancestral domain, native titles, CADT issuance, FPIC requirements, and natural resource rights per Art. IX, Sec. 3 and Art. XIII, Sec. 12. Fulfills the express legislative mandate of Art. XIII, Sec. 12. In Force
BAA 89 — Transitional Justice and Reconciliation Creates the BTJRC addressing historical injustices including unjust dispossession of territorial and proprietary rights per Art. IX, Sec. 1 In Force
No standalone Bangsamoro Mining Code, Environmental Code, or Natural Resources Code has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 38 — Protesting delineation process in Maguindanao, urging NCIP to cease CADT proceedings Ancestral domain delineation dispute — demonstrates jurisdictional tension between NCIP and Bangsamoro authorities over CADT issuance in BARMM
Res. No. 298 — Condemning killing of Jocelyn Palao, Chief of Ancestral Domain Division of MIPA Ancestral domain governance — highlights security risks facing ancestral domain officers

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 8371 (1997) Indigenous Peoples' Rights Act (IPRA) National framework for ancestral domain recognition, CADT/CALT issuance, FPIC, and NCIP authority. BOL Art. IX, Sec. 3 explicitly provides: "This Organic Law shall not in any manner diminish the rights and benefits of the non-Moro indigenous peoples in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region under the Constitution, national laws, particularly Republic Act No. 8371." Directly applicable as constitutional floor — IPRA rights cannot be diminished. BAA 64 operationalizes IPRA principles within BARMM. MIAP exercises NCIP-equivalent functions for CADT issuance (BAA 13, Sec. 3(c)).
RA 7942 (1995) Philippine Mining Act National mining framework — mineral agreements, FTAAs, environmental compliance, small-scale mining. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 13 subjects Bangsamoro mining authority to ***"the provisions of the Constitution and national laws."*** Applies suppletorily pending enactment of Bangsamoro Mining Code. FTAA endorsement process under Art. XIII, Sec. 14 follows national law framework.
RA 7076 (1991) People's Small-Scale Mining Act Regulates small-scale mining; community-based mining; requires Minahang Bayan declarations BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 15 grants Bangsamoro Government direct regulatory power over small-scale mining. RA 7076 applies suppletorily.
RA 7586 (1992) NIPAS Act (National Integrated Protected Areas System) Establishes protected areas framework. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 8 authorizes Bangsamoro Government to declare protected areas. Existing protected areas within BARMM transferred to Bangsamoro Government management per Art. XIII, Sec. 9. Suppletory pending Bangsamoro protected areas law.
RA 11038 (2018) Expanded NIPAS Act Declares additional protected areas and amends RA 7586. Strengthens Protected Area Management Boards. New protected areas declared within BARMM territory subject to Bangsamoro management transfer. Suppletory.
EO 79 (2012) Institutionalizing and Implementing Reforms in the Philippine Mining Sector National mining reform — moratorium on new mineral agreements, revenue sharing, enhanced environmental standards Suppletory — mining reform policies apply to BARMM pending Bangsamoro Mining Code.
EO 192 (1987) Reorganization of DENR Establishes DENR as lead national agency for environment, natural resources, and land management including Land Management Bureau Coordination — MENRE coordinates with DENR for natural resource management transfer and survey data sharing.
1987 Constitution, Art. XII, Sec. 2 Regalian Doctrine / National Economy and Patrimony ***"All lands of the public domain, waters, minerals, coal, petroleum, and other mineral oils, all forces of potential energy, fisheries, forests or timber, wildlife, flora and fauna, and other natural resources are owned by the State."*** Exploration, development, and utilization under full control and supervision of the State. The constitutional ceiling — Bangsamoro natural resource authority operates within state ownership principles. The BOL grants authority to explore, develop, and utilize but does not transfer ownership.
1987 Constitution, Art. XII, Sec. 5 IP Ancestral Lands ***"The State ... shall protect the rights of indigenous cultural communities to their ancestral lands to ensure their economic, social, and cultural well-being."*** Constitutional mandate for ancestral domain protection — underpins both IPRA and BAA 64.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Khalifah (Stewardship) Humans are stewards (khalifah) of the earth, entrusted with the responsibility to manage natural resources justly and sustainably. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Provides Islamic justification for environmental conservation and sustainable development mandates in the BOL. Natural resources are a trust (amanah) to be preserved for present and future generations — aligns with Art. XIII, Sec. 2 (equitable and sustainable development).
Ihya al-mawat (Revival of Dead Land) Islamic principle allowing individuals to claim ownership of uncultivated land by bringing it into productive use. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Potentially relevant to ancestral domain claims based on long-standing cultivation and occupation. However, this principle must be reconciled with the Regalian doctrine (state ownership of public lands) and IPRA's native title framework (fusaka inged).
Hima (Protected Reserve) Islamic concept of setting aside land or natural resources for communal benefit and conservation, managed by the governing authority. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Provides Islamic precedent for protected areas and nature reserves under Art. XIII, Sec. 8. A Bangsamoro Environmental Code could incorporate hima as a culturally resonant model for community-managed protected areas.
Prohibition of Israf (Waste/Excess) Quran (7:31, 6:141): wasteful exploitation of resources is prohibited. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Supports the BOL's emphasis on ecological balance in mining (Art. XIII, Sec. 13) and sustainable development planning (Art. XIII, Sec. 16). A Bangsamoro Mining Code should incorporate sustainability principles consistent with the israf prohibition.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro ancestral domain or natural resource authority boundaries. The landmark IPRA case — Cruz v. DENR Secretary (G.R. No. 135385, Dec. 6, 2000) — addressed constitutionality of IPRA's native title provisions but did not resolve the issue definitively (equally divided vote). Bangsamoro-specific ancestral domain jurisdiction remains largely unlitigated.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
PB No. 310 Legal Framework for Conservation of Liguasan Marsh, Creating LMMC Natural resources management — conservation of a major wetland ecosystem within BARMM Filed
PB No. 352 Sustainable Management of Lake Lanao and Its Watershed Natural resources — protection and sustainable management of Lake Lanao, a critical inland water body Filed

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 12: Environment and Natural Resources Sustainable management of natural resources; biodiversity conservation; protected area management; land administration
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development Mining revenue generation; resource-based economic diversification under Art. XII, Sec. 34-36
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 10: Peace and Security IP rights and ancestral domain recognition; conflict resolution over ancestral domains
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 4: Environmental Sustainability Natural resource governance, protected area management, and climate resilience

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis in the style of Philippine . Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over ancestral domain and natural resources?

A. Article V, Section 2(d) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "ancestral domain and natural resources" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This is one of the broadest powers in the BOL, encompassing: natural resource exploration, development, and utilization (Art. XIII, Sec. 8); protected area management (Art. XIII, Sec. 8-9); fossil fuel and uranium co-management (Art. XIII, Sec. 10); mining regulation (Art. XIII, Sec. 13-16); ancestral domain recognition and native titles (Art. IX, Sec. 1-3); and natural resource revenue (Art. XII, Sec. 34-36).1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Eleven provisions elaborate this power:

(1) Art. XIII, Sec. 8 — the core natural resources grant, subject to the Constitution. Includes surface and subsurface rights, inland and coastal waters, renewable and nonrenewable resources. Also mandates protected area declarations.2

(2) Art. XIII, Sec. 9 — transfer of existing protected area management from DENR to the Bangsamoro Government within two years.2

(3) Art. XIII, Sec. 10 — joint exercise with National Government (DOE) for fossil fuels and uranium. Competitive and transparent process required; bona fide residents receive preferential rating.2

(4) Art. XIII, Sec. 11 — preferential rights of bona fide residents for natural resource access; existing rights respected until expiration.2

(5) Art. XIII, Sec. 12 — express legislative mandate for IP natural resource rights, including revenue share and FPIC. Fulfilled through BAA 64.2

(6) Art. XIII, Sec. 13-16 — mines and minerals authority, FTAA endorsement, small-scale mining regulation, and Bangsamoro mining policy.2

(7) Art. IX, Sec. 1 — transitional justice mechanism addressing unjust dispossession of territorial rights. Addressed through BAA 89.3

(8) Art. IX, Sec. 3 — eight enumerated IP rights including native titles (fusaka inged), revenue share, and MIAP creation mandate.3

(9) Art. XII, Sec. 34-36 — revenue sharing from natural resources: full pertinence to BARMM for non-fossil-fuel resources; equal sharing for fossil fuels/uranium; LGU and IP community shares.4

(10) Art. VI, Sec. 8 — Bangsamoro Sustainable Development Board creation mandate. Fulfilled through BAA 31.5

(11) Art. XIII, Sec. 1 — general economic framework requiring the Bangsamoro economic system to be "based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution."6

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. Yes. The BOL contains multiple legislative mandates:

(1) Art. XIII, Sec. 8 — Parliament "shall pass a law to establish protected areas" — not yet fulfilled.

(2) Art. XIII, Sec. 12 — Parliament "shall enact a law recognizing the rights of indigenous peoples ... in relation to natural resources" — fulfilled through BAA 64.

(3) Art. XIII, Sec. 16 — mining policies "shall be established by the Parliament" — not yet fulfilled (no Bangsamoro Mining Code enacted).

(4) Art. XII, Sec. 36 — IP share in natural resource revenues to be detailed "by a law to be passed by the Parliament" — not yet fulfilled as a standalone law (BAA 64 addresses IP rights generally but does not detail the revenue sharing mechanism and percentages).

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Five categories of limitations:

(1) Constitutional limitation (Art. XIII, Sec. 8): "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution" — the Regalian doctrine (1987 Constitution, Art. XII, Sec. 2) remains supreme; natural resources are owned by the State.

(2) Joint exercise requirement (Art. XIII, Sec. 10): fossil fuels and uranium require joint exercise with the National Government through DOE. Neither government acts alone.

(3) Constitutional and national law limitation (Art. XIII, Sec. 13): mines and minerals authority is "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws."

(4) FTAA endorsement (Art. XIII, Sec. 14): the Bangsamoro Government "may endorse to the President" FTAAs — Presidential approval required.

(5) IPRA floor (Art. IX, Sec. 3, last paragraph): "This Organic Law shall not in any manner diminish the rights and benefits of the non-Moro indigenous peoples in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region under the Constitution, national laws, particularly Republic Act No. 8371."

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to ancestral domain and natural resources?

A. Five constitutional provisions define the boundaries:

(1) Regalian Doctrine (Art. XII, Sec. 2): "All lands of the public domain, waters, minerals, coal, petroleum, and other mineral oils, all forces of potential energy, fisheries, forests or timber, wildlife, flora and fauna, and other natural resources are owned by the State." The State retains ownership; the BOL grants authority to explore, develop, and utilize — not to transfer ownership.

(2) Land Classification (Art. XII, Sec. 3): "Lands of the public domain are classified into agricultural, forest or timber, mineral lands, and national parks." Only Congress can classify public lands — the BOL's Sec. 17 allows Bangsamoro to "recommend" classification to the President (see power (j)).

(3) IP Ancestral Lands (Art. XII, Sec. 5): "The State ... shall protect the rights of indigenous cultural communities to their ancestral lands."

(4) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): Expressly includes "Ancestral domain and natural resources" as a legislative power for autonomous regions.

(5) Presidential Prerogative (Art. XII, Sec. 2): The President may enter FTAAs with foreign corporations — Bangsamoro endorsement power under Art. XIII, Sec. 14 operates within this presidential authority.

Q. What is the constitutional floor for this power?

A. The constitutional floor comprises: (a) state ownership of natural resources — the Bangsamoro Government exercises management and utilization authority but does not acquire ownership; (b) IPRA protection — indigenous rights cannot be diminished; (c) 60-40 Filipino ownership rule for natural resource utilization corporations; (d) Congressional prerogative over land classification and FTAA framework. Any Bangsamoro legislation must operate above this floor.

Q. What constitutional mandates exist for this area?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 5 mandates the State to protect IP ancestral lands. Art. XII, Sec. 4 mandates Congress to determine forest land and national park limits. Art. X, Sec. 20(3) expressly authorizes the organic act to provide legislative powers over "Ancestral domain and natural resources."

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide?

A. The national framework establishes comprehensive systems: - RA 8371 (IPRA) — ancestral domain recognition, CADT/CALT issuance, FPIC, NCIP authority. - RA 7942 (Mining Act) — mineral agreements (MPSAs, FTAAs, EPSAs), environmental compliance, Mines and Geosciences Bureau (MGB). - RA 7076 (Small-Scale Mining Act) — community-based small-scale mining, Minahang Bayan. - RA 7586/RA 11038 (NIPAS/Expanded NIPAS) — protected area management, PAMBs. - EO 79 (2012) — mining sector reforms, new mineral agreement moratorium. - EO 192 (1987) — DENR as lead national agency for environment, natural resources, and land management.

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM?

A. Three national agencies operate: - NCIP — retains functions over ancestral domain delineation and CADT processes, creating jurisdictional tension with MIAP (see Res. No. 38). - DENR/MGB — manages mining permits and environmental compliance pending Bangsamoro Mining Code. - DOE — joint exercise partner for fossil fuels and uranium per Art. XIII, Sec. 10.

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM for this power?

A. All national laws listed above apply suppletorily pending standalone Bangsamoro legislation, except: (a) IPRA applies directly as a constitutional-level floor that cannot be diminished; (b) RA 7942 applies for mining subject to "the provisions of the Constitution and national laws" qualifier in Art. XIII, Sec. 13.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible?

A. MENRE is the lead ministry for natural resources. See the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A) for the BOL basis. BAA 13, Book VI, Title III defines MENRE's mandate, powers, and functions.7

Q. What are MENRE's functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book VI, Title III, Sec. 3 enumerates 19 powers and functions including: - (a) "Recommend the enactment of regional laws relative to the development, utilization, regulation and conservation of the natural resources and energy" - (d) "Exercise supervision and control over forest lands, agricultural lands, mineral resources, and national parks" - (f) "Undertake exploration, assessment, classification and inventory" of natural resources - (h) "Undertake geological surveys of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region including its territorial waters" - (j)(1) "Accelerate cadastral surveys, land use planning and public land titling" - (j)(3) "Support forest-based industries" - (j)(4) "Expedite mineral resources surveys, promote the production of metallic and non-metallic minerals" - (k) "Regulate the development, disposition, exploration and utilization of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region's forests, land, mineral and energy resources" - (l) "Exercise administration and supervision of all lands of the public domain" - (n) "Recommend policies governing the grant of rights, privileges, and concessions over the exploration, development and utilization of fossil fuels ... and uranium"7

MENRE's organizational structure includes: Land Management Services (Surveys Division; Land Utilization Development Division); Mines and Geosciences Services (Geosciences Division; Mines and Technology Management Division; Mines Safety Environment and Social Development Division); Forest Management Services; Environment Management Services; Biodiversity Ecosystems Research and Development Services; and Energy Management and Development Services.7

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Which office within MENRE handles ancestral domain specifically?

A. The Land Management Services through its Surveys Division handles land surveys relevant to ancestral domain delineation. However, ancestral domain recognition and CADT issuance are primarily handled by MIAP — specifically the Bureau of Ancestral Domains/Native Title (BAA 13, Book VI, Title VII, Sec. 10). BAA 13 gives MIAP the power to "Issue Certificates of Ancestral Domain Title (CADTs) to IPs in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region and register such CADTs in the appropriate Register of Deeds, as may be provided by law passed by the Bangsamoro Parliament" (Sec. 3(c)).8

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. Three national agencies maintain operational presence: - NCIP — continues to process ancestral domain claims and CADT applications within BARMM, creating overlap with MIAP's mandate. Resolution No. 38 protested NCIP's delineation process in Maguindanao. - DENR (through MGB) — manages mining permits, mineral agreements, and environmental compliance certificates (ECCs) within BARMM pending Bangsamoro Mining Code. - DOE — exercises joint authority over fossil fuel and uranium exploration per Art. XIII, Sec. 10.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished?

A. BARMM has: (a) institutionally established MENRE and MIAP under BAA 13; (b) created the Bangsamoro Sustainable Development Board through BAA 31; (c) enacted BAA 64 (IP Act) fulfilling the Art. XIII, Sec. 12 mandate; (d) enacted BAA 89 (Transitional Justice) addressing ancestral domain restitution; (e) MENRE operates field offices (PENREOs and CENREOs) in each province. However, no standalone Mining Code, Environmental Code, or Protected Areas Act has been enacted.

Q. What is holding BARMM back?

A. Three constraints: (1) No Bangsamoro Mining Code — mining regulation depends on national law (RA 7942) and MENRE's general mandate; (2) No Protected Areas Act — the BOL mandates this legislation but it has not been enacted; (3) NCIP-MIAP jurisdictional overlap on ancestral domain — no formal protocol established for transferring NCIP functions to MIAP. Additionally, the joint exercise protocol with DOE for fossil fuels has not been formalized.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built that BARMM does not yet have?

A. The national system has: (a) MGB — a specialized bureau with regional offices, geological survey capacity, and mining permit processing infrastructure; (b) NCIP — institutional infrastructure for ancestral domain delineation including survey teams and CADT processing systems; (c) DENR's NIPAS system — established protected area management boards (PAMBs) with operational budgets; (d) DOE's petroleum service contract system — a mature framework for fossil fuel exploration.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) NCIP's Bangsamoro disconnect — NCIP was designed for non-Moro indigenous communities; its processes do not account for Bangsamoro Moro ancestral domain claims, fusaka inged (native titles under customary Moro law), or the political dimensions of land tenure in formerly conflict-affected areas. (2) Mining permit bottleneck — MGB processing does not account for Bangsamoro Government input or FPIC requirements specific to BARMM IPs. (3) Protected area management — existing PAMBs do not integrate Bangsamoro sustainable development priorities.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes. Islamic law contains well-developed principles on natural resource stewardship and land tenure, though none are enacted in current BARMM legislation. The concept of khalifah (stewardship) positions humans as trustees of natural resources — a framework that aligns with the BOL's sustainable development emphasis. The hima (protected reserve) concept provides Islamic precedent for community-managed conservation areas. The prohibition of israf (waste/excess) supports environmental protection mandates. These remain fiqh principles applicable as persuasive authority or policy guidance, not enacted law.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area?

A. Four fiqh principles are directly relevant:

(1) Khalifah (stewardship) — natural resources are an amanah (trust) from God, to be managed for the benefit of all, including future generations.

(2) Hima (protected reserve) — land or resources set aside by governing authority for communal benefit. Provides an Islamic model for protected areas.

(3) Ihya al-mawat (revival of dead land) — potential basis for ancestral domain claims through long-standing cultivation. Must be reconciled with the Regalian doctrine and IPRA native title framework.

(4) Prohibition of israf (waste/excess) — supports ecological balance requirements in mining and resource extraction.

Q. What existing Shari'ah legislation applies?

A. PD 1083 (Code of Muslim Personal Laws) does not address natural resources or ancestral domain. BOL Art. X, Sec. 4 authorizes Shari'ah-based commercial legislation, which could encompass natural resource contracts, but no such law has been enacted. The Shari'ah dimension of this power remains at the fiqh principle level — relevant for policy design but not for current legal obligations.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge from national natural resource law?

A. The power to diverge varies by sub-area: - General natural resources (Art. XIII, Sec. 8): "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution" only — no "national laws" qualifier. BARMM has broad authority to create distinct frameworks for forest management, non-mineral natural resources, and protected areas, constrained only by the constitutional floor (Regalian doctrine, Art. XII, Sec. 2-5). - Mines and minerals (Art. XIII, Sec. 13): "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws" — narrower authority. A Bangsamoro Mining Code must be consistent with the national Mining Act framework, though it may add additional protections. - Fossil fuels and uranium (Art. XIII, Sec. 10): "Subject to the provisions of the Constitution and national laws" plus joint exercise — the most constrained sub-area. BARMM cannot act unilaterally. - IP rights (Art. IX, Sec. 3): the IPRA floor "shall not ... be diminished." BAA 64 can go above IPRA protections but not below.

Q. If BARMM enacts different standards, what are the implications?

A. (1) For a Bangsamoro Mining Code: it must meet the constitutional and national law consistency requirement (Art. XIII, Sec. 13) but can impose additional environmental protections, community benefit requirements, and Shari'ah-compliant resource-sharing mechanisms. (2) For ancestral domain: BAA 64 can provide stronger protections than IPRA — broader FPIC requirements, more generous revenue sharing, faster CADT processing — because the constitutional floor only prevents diminishment.

Q. What cross-references from other themes constrain divergence?

A. The constitutional limitations established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E) (Regalian doctrine, IPRA floor, Presidential FTAA authority) set the outer boundary. The national law consistency requirement established in the National Law Considerations section (Section C) (RA 7942 for mining, RA 8371 for IP rights) narrows BARMM's legislative space for mining specifically. The joint exercise requirement in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A) (Art. XIII, Sec. 10) completely constrains fossil fuel authority.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions?

A. Yes. Three overlaps: (1) NCIP vs. MIAP on ancestral domain — NCIP (national) and MIAP (BARMM) both claim authority over CADT issuance within BARMM. Resolution No. 38 demonstrates active conflict. No formal jurisdictional protocol has been established. (2) MGB vs. MENRE on mining — DENR-MGB retains mining permit authority pending Bangsamoro Mining Code. (3) DOE joint exercise — Art. XIII, Sec. 10 requires joint exercise for fossil fuels but no implementing protocol has been agreed upon.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. PB No. 310 (Liguasan Marsh Conservation) and PB No. 352 (Lake Lanao Sustainable Management) address specific natural resource conservation needs. No pending PB addresses a comprehensive Bangsamoro Mining Code, Environmental Code, or Protected Areas Act.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals depend on this power?

A. BDP Chapter 12 (Environment and Natural Resources) depends directly on this power for sustainable management, biodiversity conservation, and protected area management. BDP Chapter 7 (Economic Development) depends on mining revenue generation under Art. XII, Sec. 34-36 and resource-based economic diversification. BDP Chapter 10 (Peace and Security) depends on ancestral domain recognition and conflict resolution.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without standalone legislation: (1) Mining revenue cannot be maximized — BARMM lacks its own mining policy framework to attract investment under Art. XII, Sec. 34-36. (2) Protected areas lack a Bangsamoro-specific management framework, undermining environmental conservation targets. (3) IP revenue sharing under Art. XII, Sec. 36 cannot be operationalized without an implementing law detailing percentages and mechanisms. (4) NCIP-MIAP overlap creates administrative confusion that delays CADT processing, undermining BDP Chapter 10 peace and security goals.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. How does the "joint exercise" requirement for fossil fuels work in practice?

A. Art. XIII, Sec. 10 creates a co-management model: DOE and the Bangsamoro Government must jointly identify prospective contract areas, jointly evaluate proponents (with bona fide resident preferential rating), and jointly award service contracts. The BOL requires a "competitive and transparent process." In practice, this means neither government can unilaterally grant fossil fuel exploration rights within BARMM territory — an arrangement without precedent in Philippine governance. No implementing protocol has been agreed upon, leaving this provision unoperationalized.

Q. What is the relationship between this power and power (ff) Indigenous Peoples' Rights?

A. Power (d) — ancestral domain and natural resources — and power (ff) — indigenous peoples' rights — are intertwined but distinct. Power (d) covers the resource dimension (exploration, development, utilization, revenue) while power (ff) covers the rights dimension (native titles, self-determination, cultural protection, political participation). BAA 64 bridges both powers, addressing IP rights in relation to natural resources. MENRE leads on the resource dimension; MIAP leads on the rights dimension.

Q. What is the significance of the Art. XII, Sec. 34 revenue provision?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 34 provides that natural resource revenues "shall pertain fully to the Bangsamoro Government" — this is one of the strongest fiscal provisions in the BOL. For non-fossil-fuel resources (minerals, forests, quarry resources), 100% of government revenues stay in BARMM. For fossil fuels and uranium, revenues are shared equally with the National Government. This creates a powerful incentive for BARMM to develop a mining policy framework — the revenue potential is substantial but cannot be realized without the institutional capacity to attract and regulate investment.

Q. Why does the BOL require "authority of the President" for cadastral surveys but not for natural resource management?

A. The distinction reflects the different constitutional bases. Natural resource management falls under Art. X, Sec. 20(3) of the Constitution, which expressly lists "Ancestral domain and natural resources" as an autonomous region legislative power. Cadastral surveys relate to land classification, which under the Regalian doctrine remains a Presidential prerogative (see power (h)). The BOL carefully separates what the Bangsamoro Government can do autonomously (manage, explore, develop, utilize natural resources) from what requires Presidential participation (classify public lands, conduct cadastral surveys).

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
No comprehensive mining regulatory framework Bangsamoro Mining Code Mining policy; permits, licenses, and contracts; FTAAs endorsement procedures; small-scale mining regulation; environmental compliance; community benefit requirements; revenue distribution; MENRE institutional authority for mining Art. XIII, Sec. 13-16 — "the Bangsamoro Government shall have the authority and jurisdiction over the exploration, development, and utilization of mines and minerals"; "Policies on mining ... shall be established by the Parliament"
No protected areas legislation despite express mandate Bangsamoro Protected Areas Act Protected area declaration procedures; management framework; PAMB equivalent; biodiversity conservation; ancestral domain FPIC for protected area declarations; community-managed conservation (hima model) Art. XIII, Sec. 8 — "The Parliament shall pass a law to establish protected areas"
IP share in natural resource revenues not detailed IP Natural Resource Revenue Sharing Act (or chapter within Mining Code) Sharing mechanism and percentages for IP communities per Art. XII, Sec. 36; coordination with BAA 64 Art. XII, Sec. 36 — "shall be provided by a law to be passed by the Parliament detailing the sharing mechanism and percentages"
No NCIP-MIAP jurisdictional protocol MIAP-NCIP Transition Protocol (executive agreement or BAA amendment) Transfer of NCIP functions within BARMM to MIAP; CADT processing standardization; data sharing; transitional arrangements for pending applications Art. IX, Sec. 3; BAA 13, Sec. 3(c) (MIAP CADT issuance power); BAA 64
No joint exercise protocol for fossil fuels Bangsamoro-DOE Joint Exercise Protocol (executive agreement) Implementing rules for Art. XIII, Sec. 10; service contract award procedures; revenue sharing mechanisms; dispute resolution Art. XIII, Sec. 10 — joint exercise mandate

(e)
Barter Trade and Countertrade
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(e)

Power 5 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(e) Barter Trade and Countertrade

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(e) — "Barter trade and countertrade;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(e) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes — Art. XIII, Sec. 26: goods traded "shall not be sold elsewhere in the country without the payment of appropriate customs or import duties in accordance with existing national laws"; countertrade "shall be in accordance with the spirit, intent, goals, processes, modalities, and arrangements in the ASEAN economic integration agreements"
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT) — BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV (Trade, Investments, and Tourism)
Supporting Agencies Bureau of Customs (national — customs duty enforcement); BIMP-EAGA National Secretariat (framework for barter trade partners); Department of Trade and Industry (national trade coordination)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy)
Priority Code —

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(e) "Barter trade and countertrade;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over two distinct forms of cross-border trade: barter trade (traditional exchange of goods without monetary payment) and countertrade (trade arrangements where payment is made partly or wholly in goods rather than currency).
Art. XIII, Sec. 26 "The Bangsamoro Government shall regulate traditional barter trade with the Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East ASEAN Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA) Member States and countertrade with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) Member States. The goods or items that are traded with the said countries shall not be sold elsewhere in the country without the payment of appropriate customs or import duties in accordance with existing national laws."

"Countertrade with ASEAN Member States shall be in accordance with the spirit, intent, goals, processes, modalities, and arrangements in the ASEAN economic integration agreements."		 The primary implementing provision. Defines three elements: (1) scope — barter trade with BIMP-EAGA states, countertrade with ASEAN states; (2) customs constraint — goods cannot be sold domestically without payment of customs/import duties per national law; (3) ASEAN compliance — countertrade must follow ASEAN economic integration agreements. This is a unique BARMM power — no other Philippine autonomous region or LGU has authority to regulate cross-border trade.
Art. XIII, Sec. 24 "The Bangsamoro Government recognizes the private sector as a mover of trade, commerce, and industry." General trade and industry framework within which barter trade operates. Positions private sector participants — including traditional barter traders — as the primary economic actors.
Art. XIII, Sec. 28 "Through the intergovernmental relations mechanism, the Bangsamoro Government and the National Government shall cooperate on customs, immigration, and quarantine services including the attendant international commitments thereto, in order to implement and make fully operational such economic zones, industrial estates, and free ports..." Customs cooperation for economic zones — this provision is specific to economic zones (Sec. 28's subject), not a general customs cooperation mandate. However, the principle of intergovernmental customs cooperation is relevant by analogy to barter trade enforcement. A standalone barter trade act should establish its own customs cooperation mechanism.
Art. XII, Sec. 28 "The Bangsamoro Government may establish linkages for cultural exchange, economic and technical cooperation with countries having diplomatic relations with the Philippines upon review and approval of the National Government." International economic linkages — potentially supports formalization of barter trade relationships with BIMP-EAGA states, subject to National Government review and approval.
Art. XIII, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Government shall establish an economic system based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution." General economic framework requiring constitutional consistency. Relevant because barter trade regulation must not violate constitutional provisions on trade, customs, and foreign affairs.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Establishes MTIT (Book VI, Title XIV) with 26 enumerated powers and functions covering trade, investments, and tourism. Barter trade regulation falls within MTIT's general trade mandate but is not specifically enumerated among its functions. In Force
No standalone BAA has been enacted to implement the barter trade and countertrade power as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
No enacted resolution directly addresses barter trade and countertrade as of the current index.

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
EO 313 (1996) Legalizing and Regulating the Operations of Barter Trade in Mindanao The primary national framework for barter trade in southern Philippines. Legalized traditional barter trading in designated ports, established registration and permit requirements, designated goods eligible for barter, and required customs duty payment for goods entering the domestic market. Suppletory — provides the baseline regulatory framework pending a Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act. The BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 26 effectively grants BARMM the authority to create its own regulatory framework within the customs duty constraint.
EO 64 (2011) Reviving Barter in Mindanao, Promoting Its Growth and Development Revived and modernized the barter trade framework, expanded authorized trading posts, and constituted the Mindanao Barter Trade Council Suppletory — the institutional framework (Mindanao Barter Trade Council) operates pending BARMM's own regulatory mechanism.
EO 427 (1990) Placing the Control and Supervision of Barter Trade in Jolo, Sulu with the Regional Government of ARMM Historical precedent — placed barter trade supervision in Jolo under ARMM control. Demonstrates that barter trade devolution to the autonomous region has been national policy since 1990. Historical — superseded by EO 313 (1996) and the BOL. Note: Sulu is no longer within BARMM territory per Province of Sulu v. Medialdea (2024) and EO 91, s. 2025.
RA 10863 (2016) Customs Modernization and Tariff Act (CMTA) National customs law governing import duties on traded goods. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 26 explicitly requires payment of "customs or import duties in accordance with existing national laws" — the CMTA is the operative law. Directly applicable — barter-traded goods entering the domestic Philippine market must comply with CMTA customs duties. BARMM cannot override this requirement.
RA 3524 (1963) An Act to Prohibit Barter in International Trade Historically prohibited barter trade. Effectively superseded for Mindanao by EO 313 (1996) and EO 64 (2011), which re-legalized barter trade in specified areas. Superseded within BARMM by the BOL's express grant of barter trade authority (Art. V, Sec. 2(e); Art. XIII, Sec. 26). The BOL as a congressional act prevails over RA 3524 for BARMM territory.
RA 10845 (2016) Anti-Agricultural Smuggling Act Declared large-scale agricultural smuggling as economic sabotage. Repealed by RA 12022 (2024). Repealed — but the replacement law (RA 12022) maintains anti-smuggling enforcement relevant to barter trade abuse.
RA 12022 (2024) Agricultural Economic Sabotage Act Defines crimes of agricultural economic sabotage including smuggling; vests jurisdiction with the Court of Tax Appeals Directly applicable — illegal diversion of barter-traded agricultural goods to the domestic market without customs duties constitutes smuggling under this law.
ASEAN Trade in Goods Agreement (ATIGA) ASEAN Trade in Goods Agreement and related instruments BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 26 requires countertrade to conform with "the spirit, intent, goals, processes, modalities, and arrangements in the ASEAN economic integration agreements." Directly applicable — countertrade with ASEAN states must follow ATIGA and related ASEAN instruments. These are international commitments of the Philippines.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Bay' al-muqayadah (Barter Sale) Exchange of goods for goods without money as a medium. Permitted in Islamic law provided the goods exchanged are specified, the exchange is completed on the spot (to avoid gharar — uncertainty), and neither party is coerced. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Traditional barter trade in Bangsamoro communities has deep roots in Islamic commerce. A Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act should recognize bay' al-muqayadah as the Shari'ah-compliant basis for barter, ensuring that regulatory requirements do not inadvertently prohibit lawful Islamic exchange.
Prohibition of Riba (Interest/Usury) Interest-bearing financing prohibited. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Relevant to countertrade financing — if BARMM develops countertrade financial instruments, they should offer Shari'ah-compliant alternatives to interest-based trade financing (e.g., murabaha trade credit).
Prohibition of Gharar (Excessive Uncertainty) Contracts with excessive ambiguity regarding price, quantity, or quality of goods are prohibited. *Fiqh* principle — not enacted in BARMM law. Barter trade regulation should require clear specification of goods, quantities, and quality standards to avoid gharar. This aligns with good trade regulation practice generally.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified addressing Bangsamoro barter trade authority or the relationship between autonomous region trade regulation and national customs law. Barter trade disputes have historically been resolved administratively through customs enforcement rather than judicial proceedings.

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
No pending Parliament Bill directly addresses barter trade and countertrade as of the current index.

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development Identifies barter trade as a traditional economic activity to be regularized and expanded within the BIMP-EAGA framework; promotes cross-border trade facilitation
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 13: Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy International economic cooperation including trade linkages as revenue and livelihood sources
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience Cross-border trade formalization as part of regional economic resilience; trade facilitation with BIMP-EAGA partners

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis in the style of Philippine . Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over barter trade and countertrade?

A. Article V, Section 2(e) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "barter trade and countertrade" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This is a unique power — it grants the Bangsamoro Government regulatory authority over two distinct forms of cross-border trade: traditional barter trade with BIMP-EAGA states and countertrade with ASEAN states. No other Philippine LGU or autonomous region has been granted this authority.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power?

A. Four provisions elaborate:

(1) Art. XIII, Sec. 26 — the primary implementing provision. Defines scope (barter with BIMP-EAGA, countertrade with ASEAN), imposes the customs duty constraint, and requires ASEAN compliance for countertrade.2

(2) Art. XIII, Sec. 28 — customs cooperation between the Bangsamoro Government and National Government for economic zones; relevant by analogy to barter trade customs enforcement, though not a direct barter trade mandate.3

(3) Art. XII, Sec. 28 — international economic linkages with countries having diplomatic relations, subject to National Government review.4

(4) Art. XIII, Sec. 24 — general trade and industry framework positioning the private sector as the mover of trade.5

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation?

A. No — unlike other powers (e.g., cooperative code, mining policy), the BOL does not expressly name a specific piece of legislation for barter trade. Art. XIII, Sec. 26 uses "shall regulate" — granting regulatory authority but not mandating a specific code by name. However, effective exercise of this power requires legislation to establish registration systems, permitted goods lists, trading post designations, and enforcement mechanisms.

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Three express limitations:

(1) Geographic scope — barter trade is limited to "BIMP-EAGA Member States" (Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines); countertrade is limited to "ASEAN Member States." The Bangsamoro Government cannot regulate barter or countertrade with non-BIMP-EAGA or non-ASEAN countries under this provision.2

(2) Customs duty constraint — goods traded "shall not be sold elsewhere in the country without the payment of appropriate customs or import duties in accordance with existing national laws." This means barter-traded goods entering the domestic Philippine market must clear customs — the Bangsamoro Government cannot waive national customs duties.2

(3) ASEAN compliance — countertrade "shall be in accordance with the spirit, intent, goals, processes, modalities, and arrangements in the ASEAN economic integration agreements." BARMM countertrade must conform to the Philippines' international trade commitments.2

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body for this power?

A. No. The BOL does not create a specific barter trade authority. MTIT is the institutional home by virtue of its general trade mandate under BAA 13. A standalone Barter Trade Act could create a dedicated Barter Trade Authority or division within MTIT.

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply?

A. Three categories:

(1) Foreign affairs prerogative (Art. VII, Sec. 21): Treaty-making is a national prerogative. Barter trade arrangements touching international commitments (BIMP-EAGA framework, ASEAN agreements) must operate within the foreign affairs power of the National Government.

(2) Customs authority (Art. VI, Sec. 28(2)): Congress has the power to set tariff rates and customs duties. The BOL's customs duty constraint (Art. XIII, Sec. 26) reflects this constitutional distribution — BARMM regulates the trade but cannot waive nationally-set customs duties.

(3) Autonomous region framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): The organic act may provide legislative powers over "Economic, social, and tourism development" — barter trade regulation falls within this constitutional authorization.

Q. What is the constitutional floor?

A. The constitutional floor is: (a) national customs authority — cannot be overridden by BARMM legislation; (b) foreign affairs prerogative — barter trade regulation cannot create independent foreign trade relationships outside the Philippines' diplomatic framework; (c) equal protection (Art. III, Sec. 1) — barter trade regulation must not discriminate unreasonably against traders.

Q. What constitutional mandates exist?

A. No specific constitutional mandate exists for barter trade. The authority derives from the organic act provision for economic development powers (Art. X, Sec. 20) as implemented through the BOL (Art. V, Sec. 2(e); Art. XIII, Sec. 26).

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the national framework provide?

A. The national framework has an unusual history: RA 3524 (1963) prohibited barter in international trade. This prohibition was effectively lifted for Mindanao through executive orders — EO 427 (1990) placed barter trade supervision under ARMM for Jolo; EO 313 (1996) legalized and regulated barter trade across Mindanao; EO 64 (2011) revived and modernized the framework. The BOL now grants BARMM express authority that supersedes this executive order framework.

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. Three national instruments apply: - RA 10863 (CMTA) — applies directly for customs duty enforcement on barter-traded goods entering the domestic market. - EO 313 (1996) and EO 64 (2011) — apply suppletorily as the baseline regulatory framework pending BARMM legislation. - RA 12022 (2024) — anti-smuggling enforcement for illegal diversion of barter-traded goods.

Q. What national institutions exercise authority?

A. The Bureau of Customs exercises customs duty collection and anti-smuggling enforcement at designated barter trading posts. The DTI provides general trade policy coordination. The Mindanao Barter Trade Council (EO 64) provides an inter-agency coordination mechanism. Upon enactment of a Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act, MTIT would assume regulatory functions, but customs enforcement would remain a joint responsibility (by analogy with Art. XIII, Sec. 28's economic zones customs cooperation framework).

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry is primarily responsible?

A. MTIT is the lead ministry by virtue of its general trade mandate. BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Sec. 3 enumerates 26 powers and functions covering trade, investments, and tourism — but barter trade is not specifically listed among these functions. MTIT's authority over barter trade derives from the general trade mandate and the BOL's Art. XIII, Sec. 26 grant.6

Q. What are MTIT's relevant functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Sec. 3 provides general trade functions relevant to barter trade: - (a) "Formulate policies, plans, programs and projects for the development of the trade, investments, and tourism in the region" - (v) "Promote domestic trade, marketing and distributions to ensure the rational, economical and steady flow of commodities from producing and/or marketing centers to areas where there is a shortage of supply" - (z) "Exercise such other powers and functions as necessary, proper, or incidental to the attainment of its mandate"6

No specific barter trade regulatory functions — registration, permitted goods lists, trading post designation, or enforcement — are enumerated.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Does MTIT have a dedicated division for barter trade?

A. No. BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Sec. 10 lists MTIT's organizational structure but does not include a dedicated barter trade division. A Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act should create either a Barter Trade Division within MTIT or a dedicated Bangsamoro Barter Trade Authority attached to MTIT.

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority?

A. The national government exercises authority through: (a) Bureau of Customs — collects customs duties on barter-traded goods and enforces anti-smuggling laws at designated barter trading posts; (b) Mindanao Barter Trade Council (EO 64) — inter-agency coordination body; (c) Philippine Coast Guard and Philippine National Police Maritime Group — enforcement against illegal barter trade and smuggling. The barter trading posts historically designated in Sulu (Jolo) are no longer within BARMM territory; BARMM's barter trade infrastructure must be developed in its remaining island provinces (Tawi-Tawi, Basilan) and mainland coastal areas.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished?

A. BARMM has institutionally established MTIT under BAA 13 but has not enacted any standalone legislation for barter trade. No Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act, no designated barter trading posts, no registration system, and no permitted goods list have been established under Bangsamoro law. The power remains entirely unexercised through dedicated legislation.

Q. What is holding BARMM back?

A. Three factors: (1) No enabling legislation — Art. XIII, Sec. 26 grants the power but MTIT has no specific regulatory authority codified in Bangsamoro law; (2) Sulu's exclusion — the traditional center of barter trade (Jolo) is no longer in BARMM, reducing the immediate commercial pressure to legislate; (3) Customs coordination complexity — effective barter trade regulation requires a formal agreement with the Bureau of Customs under Art. XIII, Sec. 28, which has not been established.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built that BARMM does not yet have?

A. The national government through EO 313 and EO 64 established: designated barter trading posts; a registration system for barter traders; a list of permitted goods; the Mindanao Barter Trade Council for inter-agency coordination; and customs duty processing protocols. BARMM has none of these under its own authority.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) The existing framework is designed for Mindanao-wide barter trade centered on Zamboanga and Jolo — not on BARMM's specific trading communities. (2) The framework does not account for the Islamic commercial traditions (bay' al-muqayadah) that underpin Bangsamoro barter practice. (3) The institutional structure (Mindanao Barter Trade Council) does not give BARMM governance representation proportional to its role in traditional barter trade.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes. Traditional barter trade in the Bangsamoro homeland — particularly in the Sulu Archipelago, Tawi-Tawi, and Basilan — has been practiced for centuries by Muslim traders as bay' al-muqayadah (goods-for-goods exchange). This is a Shari'ah-compliant form of commerce with deep historical and cultural roots in the Bangsamoro identity. The Tausug, Sama, and Yakan peoples have maintained barter relationships with Sabah (Malaysia), Kalimantan (Indonesia), and Brunei long before modern national boundaries existed.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant?

A. Three fiqh principles are relevant:

(1) Bay' al-muqayadah (barter sale) — permissible provided goods are specified, exchange is completed, and parties are not coerced.

(2) Prohibition of gharar (excessive uncertainty) — barter contracts must have clear terms regarding goods, quantities, and quality.

(3) Prohibition of riba — relevant to countertrade financing instruments that should avoid interest-based mechanisms.

Q. How should Shari'ah compliance be integrated?

A. A Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act should: (a) recognize the historical legitimacy of bay' al-muqayadah in the preamble; (b) ensure that registration requirements do not criminalize traditional Islamic commerce; (c) provide Shari'ah-compliant countertrade financing options (e.g., murabaha trade credit) for BARMM traders engaging in countertrade with ASEAN states.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge from national barter trade regulation?

A. BARMM has moderate authority to diverge. The BOL grants express regulatory authority ("shall regulate"), but imposes two hard constraints: (a) customs duties per "existing national laws" cannot be waived — this is a non-negotiable national law consistency requirement; (b) ASEAN compliance for countertrade — BARMM cannot create countertrade arrangements inconsistent with the Philippines' ASEAN commitments.

Within these constraints, BARMM can: establish its own barter trading posts; create a distinct registration and permit system; define its own permitted goods list; impose additional quality and safety standards; and establish Shari'ah-compliant barter trade frameworks — all without needing to mirror the EO 313/EO 64 framework.

Q. If BARMM enacts different standards, what are the implications?

A. (1) For traders: a BARMM barter trade system could offer simpler registration, culturally appropriate processes, and proximity to traditional trading communities — potentially encouraging formalization of currently informal trade. (2) For customs enforcement: dual compliance is inevitable — BARMM registration for trade regulation, national customs clearance for goods entering the domestic market. A well-designed system would integrate both into a single process at designated barter trading posts.

Q. What cross-references from other themes constrain divergence?

A. The customs constraint from the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A) and the constitutional foreign affairs prerogative from the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E) are the binding limits. BARMM cannot create an independent international trade regime — only regulate traditional barter and countertrade within the BIMP-EAGA/ASEAN framework specified by the BOL.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions?

A. Yes. The primary overlap is between the Bureau of Customs (national customs enforcement) and MTIT (trade regulation). The BOL's Art. XIII, Sec. 28 establishes customs cooperation for economic zones (not directly for barter trade), but no barter-trade-specific customs protocol has been established. Additionally, the Mindanao Barter Trade Council (EO 64) operates region-wide, potentially overlapping with MTIT's regulatory authority within BARMM.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. No pending Parliament Bill addresses barter trade and countertrade. This is one of the powers with the least legislative attention — no filed PB, no resolution, no legislative committee action identified. The power has been completely dormant legislatively.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals depend on this power?

A. BDP Chapter 7 (Economic Development) identifies cross-border trade facilitation within the BIMP-EAGA framework as a strategy for economic growth. BDP Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy) identifies international economic cooperation, including trade linkages, as a source of revenue and livelihood. Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience) of the Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda depends on formalizing traditional trade channels to build economic resilience.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without legislation: (1) Informal barter trade continues unregulated — traditional Bangsamoro traders operate outside the formal economy, missing revenue capture opportunities and creating smuggling risks. (2) BIMP-EAGA integration stalls — BARMM cannot formally participate in BIMP-EAGA trade facilitation programs without a regulatory framework. (3) Countertrade potential unrealized — ASEAN countertrade opportunities (e.g., exchange of Bangsamoro agricultural products for manufactured goods) cannot be structured without a legal framework. (4) Revenue leakage — without a formal barter trade registration system, the Bangsamoro Government cannot capture fees, permits, or data on cross-border trade volumes.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What makes this power unique among the 55 BOL powers?

A. Barter trade and countertrade is the only power that grants the Bangsamoro Government direct regulatory authority over a form of international trade. While the power is constrained by customs and ASEAN compliance requirements, it effectively gives BARMM a role in cross-border commerce that no other Philippine LGU or autonomous region possesses. This reflects the historical reality of Bangsamoro maritime trade networks — the Sulu Sultanate and Maranao traders maintained commerce with Southeast Asian neighbors for centuries before the Philippines existed as a nation-state.

Q. How does the loss of Sulu affect this power?

A. Sulu's exclusion from BARMM territory (per Province of Sulu v. Medialdea, G.R. Nos. 242255, 243246, 243693 (Sept. 9, 2024; finalized Nov. 26, 2024); EO 91, s. 2025, realigning Sulu to Region IX) significantly reduces the traditional barter trade infrastructure available to BARMM. Jolo was the primary barter trading center under EO 313 and EO 427. BARMM must now develop barter trade infrastructure in Tawi-Tawi (closest to Sabah), Basilan (closest to Zamboanga trade routes), and potentially coastal Maguindanao and Cotabato City for BIMP-EAGA land-based trade.

Q. What is the relationship between barter trade and the customs cooperation requirement?

A. Art. XIII, Sec. 26 grants BARMM the power to "regulate" barter trade, while Art. XIII, Sec. 28 requires "cooperation on customs, immigration, and quarantine services." This creates a bifurcated authority model: BARMM regulates who can trade, what can be traded, where trading posts are located, and how trade is conducted; the National Government (through the Bureau of Customs) enforces customs duties on goods entering the domestic market. A Bangsamoro Barter Trade Act should establish a joint customs processing mechanism at designated barter trading posts where BARMM trade permits and national customs clearance are processed simultaneously.

Q. How does this power interact with power (u) Economic zones and free ports?

A. Power (u) — "economic zones, industrial centers, and free ports" (Art. V, Sec. 2(u)) — and power (e) are complementary. Art. XIII, Sec. 28 establishes customs cooperation for economic zones — a similar framework would need to be established for barter trade through standalone legislation. A barter trade zone could be established within a Bangsamoro economic zone, combining the barter trade regulatory authority of power (e) with the fiscal incentives of power (u).

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
No BAA regulates barter trade despite detailed BOL mandate Bangsamoro Barter Trade and Countertrade Act Barter trade registration system; permitted goods list; designated barter trading posts (Tawi-Tawi, Basilan, coastal Maguindanao); MTIT regulatory authority; quality and safety standards; Shari'ah-compliant trade framework; enforcement and penalties; countertrade procedures with ASEAN states; coordination with Bureau of Customs Art. XIII, Sec. 26 — "The Bangsamoro Government shall regulate traditional barter trade"
No customs cooperation protocol for barter trade Barter Trade Customs Cooperation Agreement (executive agreement with Bureau of Customs) Joint customs processing at barter trading posts; data sharing; anti-smuggling coordination; revenue accounting Art. XIII, Sec. 26 (customs duty constraint); Art. XIII, Sec. 28 (economic zones customs cooperation — by analogy)
MTIT lacks specific barter trade regulatory functions MTIT Barter Trade Division (through standalone act or BAA 13 amendment) Dedicated institutional capacity for barter trade regulation, trader registration, and monitoring BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV (MTIT mandate); Art. XIII, Sec. 26
