Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(f) Budgeting

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(f) —"Budgeting;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(f) —enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes —Art. VII, Sec. 28 ("consistent with existing laws, rules and regulations of the National Government"); Art. XII, Sec. 2 (COA as auditor); Art. XII, Sec. 19 ("the national laws and the budgeting rules and regulations implemented by the Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local Government applicable to local government units shall apply")
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Finance, and Budget and Management (MFBM) —BAA 13, Book V, Title IV
Supporting Agencies Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority (BPDA); Bangsamoro Treasury Office (BAA 13, Ch. 4); Bangsamoro Budget Office (BAA 13, Ch. 6); Bangsamoro Finance Office (BAA 13, Ch. 5); Bangsamoro Revenue Office (BAA 13, Ch. 7); Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board (Art. VI, Sec. 4); Contingent Fund (BAA 85, Ch. XXXI)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 5 —Governance and Institutional Development; Chapter 13 —Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy
Priority Code Bangsamoro Budget System Act (BAA 84) —enacted as the comprehensive budget system framework. The Revenue Code is one of the 7 priority codes in Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) —not yet enacted.

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data —BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power —verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(f) "Budgeting;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over its own budgeting processes —preparation, allocation, enactment, and execution of the annual budget.
Art. XII, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Government shall enjoy fiscal autonomy with the end in view of attaining economic self-sufficiency and genuine development. ... It shall prepare its budget and allocate funds in accordance with an annual appropriations law passed by the Parliament." Fiscal autonomy mandate —the Bangsamoro Government prepares its own budget and allocates funds through its own appropriations law. Spending must be programmatic, transparent, performance-based, and phased.
Art. XII, Sec. 2 "Pursuant to the Constitution, the Commission on Audit shall be the exclusive auditor of the Bangsamoro Government and its constituent local government units." COA as exclusive auditor —a constitutional constraint. The Bangsamoro Government must also establish an internal auditing body per RA 3456.
Art. VII, Sec. 27 "No public money, including the block grant and other national government subsidies and support funds given in lump sum, shall be spent without an appropriations law clearly defining the purpose for which it is intended." Appropriations law requirement —every peso of public money requires a law defining its purpose. This prohibits off-budget spending.
Art. VII, Sec. 28 "The form, content, and manner of preparation of the Bangsamoro budget shall be prescribed by law enacted by the Parliament, consistent with existing laws, rules and regulations of the National Government." Budget system legislation —Parliament must enact a budget system law. The national law consistency requirement means the Bangsamoro budget process must align with DBM rules. Fulfilled by BAA 84.
Art. VII, Sec. 29 "If, by the end of a fiscal year, the Parliament shall have failed to pass the Bangsamoro appropriations bill for the ensuing fiscal year, the Bangsamoro appropriations law for the preceding fiscal year shall be deemed reenacted." Reenacted budget provision —prevents government shutdown. Only salaries, statutory/contractual obligations, and operating expenses are deemed reenacted.
Art. XII, Sec. 6 "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to create its own sources of revenues and to levy taxes, fees, and charges..." Enumerates 12 revenue sources feeding the budget: taxes; fees and charges; annual block grant; natural resource revenues; national tax shares; GOCC dividends; economic agreement grants; donations/foreign assistance; loans/ODA; public utility revenues; national budget allocations; quarry resource tax.
Art. XII, Sec. 15-18 "The National Government shall provide an annual block grant which shall be the share of the Bangsamoro Government in the national internal revenue tax collections..." Block grant —the primary revenue source. Amount = 5% of net national internal revenue tax + Bureau of Customs net collections (from 3rd preceding fiscal year). Automatically appropriated in the national GAA (Sec. 17). Released without lien or holdback (Sec. 18).
Art. XII, Sec. 19 "The Parliament shall pass an annual appropriations law allocating the block grant to various agencies and programs ... The Parliament shall assign the highest budgetary priority to education, health, and social services... In the allocation of the block grant, the national laws and the budgeting rules and regulations implemented by the Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local Government applicable to local government units shall apply." Allocation rules: (1) highest priority to education, health, social services; (2) no firearms/ammunition procurement from block grant; (3) development plans furnished to DILG; (4) DBM/DILG rules apply to block grant allocation; (5) performance standards and targets required; (6) unspent amounts revert to Bangsamoro Treasury Special Fund.
Art. XII, Sec. 20 "The total appropriations ... for Personal Services of the Bangsamoro Government for one (1) fiscal year shall not exceed forty-five percent (45%) of the total revenue sources." 45% PS cap —one of the most important fiscal constraints. Also: no salary higher than maximum for the position; no appropriations for national government salaries; creation/abolition of positions per Art. XVI, Sec. 10; permanent positions must have adequate appropriations; no retroactive salary adjustments.
Art. VI, Sec. 4 "There is hereby created an Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board that shall address revenue imbalances and fluctuations in regional financial needs and revenue-raising capacity of the Bangsamoro Government." Creates the IFPB —the intergovernmental body for fiscal policy coordination between national and Bangsamoro governments. Functions detailed in Art. XII, Sec. 37-41.
Art. XII, Sec. 37-41 "The Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board ... shall have the following functions..." IFPB functions: resolve tax disputes; determine GOCC shares; promulgate rules on taxable elements; review block grant formula every 5 years; annual report to Congress and Parliament; full disclosure of fiscal data.

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 84 PB No. 325 Budget System Act Key standalone code —comprehensive budget system framework fulfilling Art. VII, Sec. 28 mandate In Force
BAA 3 -- FY 2020 GAA First Bangsamoro annual appropriations act Amended by BAA 14, 24, 38
BAA 14 -- Extension of FY 2020 Appropriations Extends validity of FY 2020 budget In Force
BAA 15 -- FY 2021 GAA Annual appropriations act Amended by BAA 24, 38
BAA 22 -- Special Development Fund (2020-2021) Allocates SDF for conflict-affected communities per Art. XIV, Sec. 2 Amended by BAA 34, 51
BAA 23 -- FY 2022 GAA Annual appropriations act Amended by BAA 33, 38

BAA 32 -- FY 2023 GAA Annual appropriations act Amended by BAA 52
BAA 33 -- Extension of FY 2022 GAA Budget validity extension In Force

BAA 36 -- Revolving Fund for Nationally-Funded Programs Ensures timely salary payment through a revolving fund mechanism In Force
BAA 38 -- Continued Use of FY 2020-2022 Funds Budget validity extension for unpaid obligations In Force
BAA 51 -- Extension of SDF 2020-2023 SDF validity extension In Force
BAA 52 -- Extension of FY 2023 GAA Budget validity extension In Force
BAA 56 PB No. 262 FY 2024 GAA Annual appropriations act In Force
BAA 63 -- FY 2024 Supplemental Appropriation Supplemental budget —additional funding for FY 2024 In Force
BAA 65 PB No. 3 FY 2025 GAA Annual appropriations act In Force
BAA 75 PB No. 349 FY 2025 Supplemental Appropriation Supplemental budget In Force
BAA 85 -- FY 2026 GAA Current annual appropriations act —36 chapters funding entire bureaucracy plus special purpose funds In Force

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 3 -- Authorizing Chief Minister to request release of transitional fund Initial budget mobilization for the BTA transition
Res. No. 4 -- Authorizing transfer of ARMM appropriation funds to BTA ARMM-to-BARMM fund transition
Res. No. 45 -- Strengthening 2020 fiscal framework through equitable allocation Budget equity and fiscal discipline
Res. No. 100 -- Approving BTA Parliament budget for 2021 Parliamentary budget allocation
Res. No. 137 -- Requesting DBM on Mandanas ruling effect on LGU IRA Fiscal impact of the Supreme Court Mandanas ruling on BARMM LGU revenue shares
Res. No. 493 -- Adhering to EO 64 on salary schedule updates Compensation and budgeting alignment with national salary standardization

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 992 (1954) Budget System for the National Government National budget system framework —provides the conceptual basis for government budgeting in the Philippines Suppletory for general budgeting principles. BAA 84 now provides the Bangsamoro-specific budget system.
RA 9184 (2003) Government Procurement Reform Act National procurement framework —governs how appropriated funds are spent on goods, services, and infrastructure Directly applicable. All procurement by Bangsamoro Government entities follows RA 9184 and its IRR.
RA 6758 (1989) Compensation and Position Classification Act BOL Art. VII, Sec. 13 expressly requires adoption —directly affects the Personal Services component of the budget Directly applicable. Determines salary grades and compensation limits that constrain budget allocation for personal services.
RA 7160 (1991) Local Government Code BOL Art. XII, Sec. 19 references DBM and DILG standards applicable to LGUs for block grant allocation —RA 7160 provides the LGU fiscal framework Directly applicable to BARMM LGU budgeting. LGU IRA shares and fiscal procedures follow RA 7160 as supplemented by BAA 49.
RA 6713 (1989) Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards BOL Art. VII, Sec. 16(b) applies its prohibitions —relevant to officials handling budget matters Directly applicable.
RA 3456 (1962) Internal Auditing Act BOL Art. XII, Sec. 2 requires the Bangsamoro Government to establish an internal auditing body "in accordance with Republic Act No. 3456" Directly applicable. The Bangsamoro internal audit function must conform to this national framework.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Amanah (Trust / Stewardship) Public funds are a trust (amanah) held on behalf of the people. Those who manage public money are trustees obligated to use it faithfully, transparently, and for the public good. The foundational Islamic principle for public financial management. Budget preparation, allocation, and execution must reflect faithful stewardship of the people's resources. Misappropriation or wasteful spending violates amanah.
Maslahah (Public Interest / Welfare) Public spending should prioritize the common good —meeting essential needs before discretionary expenditures. Aligns with Art. XII, Sec. 19's mandate to assign highest budgetary priority to education, health, and social services. Islamic public finance prioritizes necessities (daruriyyat), then needs (hajiyyat), then enhancements (tahsiniyyat).
Prohibition of Israf (Extravagance/Waste) Quran (7:31, 17:26-27): prohibition against excess and waste in spending. Public expenditure must be proportionate to need. Reinforces the 45% PS cap (Art. XII, Sec. 20) and the requirement for performance-based spending (Art. XII, Sec. 1). Government budgets must avoid extravagance and ensure value for money.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions have been identified that specifically adjudicate Bangsamoro budgetary authority under the BOL. The Supreme Court's Mandanas ruling (Mandanas v. Ochoa, G.R. No. 199802, July 3, 2018) on the IRA/NTA formula significantly impacts BARMM LGU revenue shares but does not directly address the Bangsamoro Government's block grant or budgeting authority. Res. No. 137 reflects Parliament's awareness of the Mandanas ruling's fiscal impact.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
PB No. 240 Instituting schedule of submission of proposed Bangsamoro Budget Expenditure Program Budget transparency and submission timeline regulation Filed
PB No. 286 Providing for the Revenue Code of the BARMM Fulfills Art. XII, Sec. 14 and Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) mandate for a Bangsamoro Revenue Code —a priority code not yet enacted. While technically a revenue power (n), the revenue code directly feeds the budget by defining revenue sources. Filed
PB No. 400 Institutionalizing the Bangsamoro Trust Fund for Development Continuity Creates a trust fund mechanism for development continuity across fiscal years Filed

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 5: Governance and Institutional Development Budgeting is the fiscal expression of governance —institutional development depends on adequate, well-allocated budgets
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 13: Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy Directly addresses budgeting —fiscal strategy, resource mobilization, block grant utilization, revenue generation targets
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 1: Good Governance Transparent, performance-based budgeting is a core governance deliverable
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience Budget allocation to economic programs drives resilience outcomes

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over budgeting?

A. Article V, Section 2(f) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "Budgeting" as one of the 55 powers. This power is elaborated across the entire Article XII (Fiscal Autonomy —41 sections) plus Art. VII, Sec. 27-29. The grant encompasses budget preparation, revenue mobilization, appropriation, allocation, execution, and reporting. Art. XII, Sec. 1 states: "The Bangsamoro Government shall enjoy fiscal autonomy with the end in view of attaining economic self-sufficiency and genuine development." This is the broadest fiscal authority granted to any autonomous region in Philippine history.12

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. The budgeting power is the most elaborately detailed power in the BOL. Key provision clusters:

(1) Fiscal autonomy framework (Art. XII, Sec. 1-5): fiscal autonomy mandate, COA as exclusive auditor, local government finance office, Bangsamoro Treasury Office, national assistance in tax administration.23

(2) Revenue sources (Art. XII, Sec. 6-14): 12 categories of revenue, taxation principles, tax incentives, taxing power limitations, sharing of national taxes, revenue office establishment, corporate tax allocation, LGU shares, and the Revenue Code mandate.4

(3) Block grant (Art. XII, Sec. 15-23): annual block grant formula (5% of net NIRTA + Customs), automatic appropriation, unconditional release, allocation rules (priority to education/health/social services, DBM/DILG rules apply), 45% PS cap, general limitations, block grant deductions after 20 years, formula review every 5 years, development programs.567

(4) Budget process (Art. VII, Sec. 27-29): appropriations law requirement, budget form/content per Parliament-enacted law (consistent with national rules), reenacted budget provision.89

(5) Intergovernmental fiscal mechanism (Art. VI, Sec. 4; Art. XII, Sec. 37-41): IFPB creation, functions, composition, annual reporting, full disclosure.10

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. Yes. Three express mandates:

(1) Art. VII, Sec. 28: Parliament shall enact a law prescribing "the form, content, and manner of preparation of the Bangsamoro budget." Fulfilled by BAA 84 (Budget System Act).9

(2) Art. XII, Sec. 14: "The Parliament shall enact a Bangsamoro tax and revenue code." This is one of the 7 priority codes under Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a). Not yet fulfilled as of BAA 89.11

(3) Art. XII, Sec. 11: Parliament shall establish by law the Bangsamoro Revenue Office for tax assessment and collection. Fulfilled through BAA 13, Book V, Title IV, Ch. 7.12

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power?

A. Yes. The BOL directly creates:

(1) Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board (IFPB) (Art. VI, Sec. 4) —addresses revenue imbalances and fiscal policy coordination between national and Bangsamoro governments.10

(2) Bangsamoro Treasury Office (Art. XII, Sec. 4) —receives and safeguards all revenues; headed by a Regional Treasurer appointed by the Chief Minister for a 6-year term.3

(3) Bangsamoro Regional Office of the Bureau of Local Government Finance (Art. XII, Sec. 3) —under the Department of Finance, coordinating LGU fiscal operations.3

BAA 13 further establishes the Bangsamoro Budget Office, Bangsamoro Finance Office, and Bangsamoro Revenue Office within MFBM.

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Among the most constrained of all 55 powers. Seven major limitations:

(1) National law consistency: Art. VII, Sec. 28 —budget system "consistent with existing laws, rules and regulations of the National Government."9

(2) DBM/DILG rules apply: Art. XII, Sec. 19 —block grant allocation follows "the national laws and the budgeting rules and regulations implemented by the Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local Government applicable to local government units."6

(3) COA as exclusive auditor: Art. XII, Sec. 2 —constitutional constraint.3

(4) 45% PS cap: Art. XII, Sec. 20 —personal services cannot exceed 45% of total revenue sources.7

(5) Education/health/social services priority: Art. XII, Sec. 19 —Parliament shall assign "the highest budgetary priority to education, health, and social services."6

(6) No firearms from block grant: Art. XII, Sec. 19 —"The Parliament shall not include the procurement of firearms, ammunition, armaments, and explosives in its annual appropriations law from the block grant."6

(7) Salary ceiling: Art. XII, Sec. 20(b) —"No official or employee shall be entitled to a salary rate higher than the maximum fixed for the position."7

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to budgeting?

A. Four categories:

(1) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): Budgeting operates "within the framework of this Constitution."13

(2) Commission on Audit (Art. IX-D): COA has constitutional authority as "the exclusive auditor" of all government entities, including the Bangsamoro Government. This cannot be superseded by any BAA.14

(3) Public Financial Management (Art. VI, Sec. 25): "All money collected on any tax levied for a special purpose shall be treated as a special fund and paid out for such purpose only." This constitutional earmarking principle applies to BARMM special purpose funds.15

(4) No-Increase Rule (Art. VI, Sec. 25(1)): Congress may not increase appropriations recommended by the President. While this specifically governs the national GAA process, the principle of executive budget recommendation and legislative appropriation is reflected in the Bangsamoro's own budget process.

Q. What constitutional mandates for budgeting institutions exist?

A. COA (Art. IX-D) is the constitutionally mandated auditing institution with exclusive authority over all government accounts. No Bangsamoro legislation can diminish COA's authority or create a parallel external audit body.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for this subject area?

A. The national framework provides: RA 992 (national budget system); RA 9184 (procurement reform); RA 6758 (compensation classification); RA 7160 (LGU budgeting framework); RA 3456 (internal auditing). DBM circulars, budget call documents, and COA rules supplement the statutory framework.16

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. All apply:

(1) RA 9184 —directly applicable for procurement.16 (2) RA 6758 —directly applicable for compensation.16 (3) RA 7160 —directly applicable for LGU fiscal matters.16 (4) RA 3456 —directly applicable for internal auditing per Art. XII, Sec. 2.3 (5) DBM circulars and budget rules —directly applicable per Art. XII, Sec. 19.6

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. COA audits all Bangsamoro Government entities (constitutional authority). DBM sets budgeting rules and regulations that apply to the block grant (Art. XII, Sec. 19). DILG standards apply to block grant allocation for LGUs. Bureau of the Treasury processes block grant releases. Bureau of Local Government Finance coordinates LGU fiscal operations through the BARMM regional office (Art. XII, Sec. 3). The IFPB serves as the intergovernmental coordination mechanism. These institutions retain permanent authority —the BOL does not contemplate their replacement.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power?

A. The Ministry of Finance, and Budget and Management (MFBM) is the lead ministry. BAA 13, Book V, Title IV (Sec. 1-20) defines its mandate, powers, functions, and organizational structure. BAA 85, Chapter III allocates its operational budget.17

Q. What standalone legislation (BAAs) has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. 19 BAAs —the most of any power: BAA 84 (Budget System Act); 7 annual GAAs (BAA 3, 15, 23, 32, 56, 65, 85); 2 supplemental appropriations (BAA 63, 75); 1 SDF act (BAA 22); 6 extension acts (BAA 14, 24, 33, 34, 38, 51, 52); 1 revolving fund (BAA 36). Additionally, BAA 13 establishes MFBM and its component offices.

Q. What are MFBM's mandate and functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book V, Title IV defines MFBM comprehensively:

Mandate (Sec. 2): "The Ministry of Finance, and Budget and Management is primarily mandated to evolve sound, efficient and responsive fiscal management and utilization of resources. It shall be responsible for the formulation, institutionalization and administration of fiscal policies."17

Powers and functions (Sec. 3) —15 enumerated functions: - (a) "Formulate medium-term expenditure and annual plans covering the Bangsamoro Government's resources mobilization efforts"; - (b) "Undertake the formulation of the annual budget"; - (c) "Formulate, institutionalize and administer fiscal and tax policies"; - (d) "Supervise, direct, manage, and control the collection of Bangsamoro Government revenues"; - (e) "Act as custodian of, and manage all financial resources"; - (f) "Manage public debt"; - (g) Review and coordinate policies of financial institutions; - (h) Review and coordinate policies of GOCCs; - (i) "Ensure the implementation of policies and measures on local revenue administration"; - (j) Coordinate on fiscal/monetary policies, credit, international finance, trade; - (k) "Develop and administer standard accounting system"; - (l) "Conduct a continuing study of the bureaucracy and assess as well as make policy recommendation on its role, size, composition, structure and functions"; - (m) "Establish rules and procedures for the management of government organization resources"; - (n) "Conceptualize and administer the Bangsamoro Government's compensation and position classification consistent with national standards"; - (o) Other functions assigned by the Minister.18

Component offices: - Bangsamoro Treasury Office (Ch. 4, Sec. 10-14) —receives/safeguards revenues, custodian of financial assets, investment strategy, cash flow analysis; - Bangsamoro Finance Office (Ch. 5, Sec. 15-16) —fiscal/tax policy formulation, revenue collection supervision, GOCC coordination, expenditure planning; - Bangsamoro Budget Office (Ch. 6, Sec. 17-18) —annual budget formulation, accounting system, bureaucracy assessment, organization/systems improvement, compensation/position classification; - Bangsamoro Revenue Office (Ch. 7, Sec. 19-20) —tax assessment/collection, preventive measures for tax evasion, field unit supervision.19202122

Q. What can MFBM NOT do without additional legislation?

A. BAA 13 provides the institutional framework and BAA 84 provides the budget system. What still requires legislation: (a) the Bangsamoro Revenue Code (Art. XII, Sec. 14; Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a)) —the most critical unfulfilled mandate, which would define taxing powers, rates, exemptions, and enforcement mechanisms; (b) detailed appropriation procedures for specific sectors beyond what BAA 84 covers; (c) a comprehensive public financial management code integrating accounting, auditing, and fiscal reporting standards specific to BARMM.

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. DBM applies national budgeting rules to the block grant per Art. XII, Sec. 19. COA audits all Bangsamoro Government accounts. Bureau of the Treasury processes and releases the block grant. BIR collects national taxes within BARMM (Art. XII, Sec. 11 —until the Bangsamoro Revenue Office is fully operational). The IFPB coordinates fiscal policy between national and Bangsamoro governments.

Q. What is the status of devolution for this power?

A. Budgeting is substantially devolved —the Bangsamoro Government prepares its own budget, enacts its own GAA, and manages its own fiscal resources. The constraints are structural: COA audit authority (constitutional), DBM budgeting rules (Art. XII, Sec. 19), and the block grant mechanism itself (dependent on national government release). The Bangsamoro Revenue Office has been established but revenue collection functions are still transitioning from BIR.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power?

A. Extensive accomplishments: (1) 7 annual GAAs enacted (FY 2020-2026) —unbroken appropriation cycle; (2) BAA 84 (Budget System Act) enacted —fulfilling Art. VII, Sec. 28; (3) MFBM fully operationalized with Treasury, Finance, Budget, and Revenue offices; (4) 2 supplemental appropriations passed; (5) SDF mechanism established (BAA 22) and extended; (6) Revolving fund (BAA 36) for nationally-funded salary payments; (7) Multiple extension acts demonstrating fiscal flexibility; (8) Budget transparency through published GAAs and performance targets.

Q. Where is the BARMM stronger than the national government in this area?

A. Two areas: (1) Block grant unconditional release —Art. XII, Sec. 18 provides that the block grant "shall not be subject to any lien or holdback" —giving the Bangsamoro Government more fiscal predictability than national agencies subject to DBM allotment; (2) Fiscal autonomy design —the BOL's comprehensive fiscal framework (12 revenue sources, block grant formula, IFPB mechanism) represents the most advanced fiscal autonomy arrangement for any autonomous region in the Philippines.

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. Three constraints: (1) No Revenue Code —the most critical gap. Without it, the Bangsamoro Government cannot fully exercise its taxing powers under Art. XII, Sec. 6-9, and the Bangsamoro Revenue Office cannot independently administer regional taxes; (2) Revenue collection transition —BIR still collects most taxes in BARMM; the transition to Bangsamoro Revenue Office collection is incomplete; (3) Absorption capacity —the ability to spend appropriated funds efficiently and completely is constrained by institutional capacity, especially in infrastructure and capital projects.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built for this area that BARMM does not yet have?

A. The national government has: (1) BIR with decades of tax administration infrastructure, IT systems, and trained personnel; (2) DBM with comprehensive budget preparation, execution, and monitoring systems (BESF, NEP, UACS); (3) COA with established audit methodologies, training programs, and enforcement mechanisms; (4) PhilGEPS for electronic procurement; (5) Government Integrated Financial Management Information System (GIFMIS) for financial reporting.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. Two areas: (1) BIR's BARMM presence has historically been one of the weakest regional offices, with low collection efficiency and limited enforcement capacity —contributing to the revenue gap; (2) National budgeting rules designed for the presidential system do not always fit the parliamentary model —some DBM procedures are bureaucratically heavy for a smaller, more agile government.

Q. What should BARMM replicate, adapt, or replace from the national system?

A. Replicate: COA audit standards, government accounting rules (NGAFS), and procurement procedures (RA 9184). Adapt: DBM budget preparation procedures to fit the parliamentary model; the BIR tax administration framework for the Bangsamoro Revenue Office, incorporating regional context. Replace: the national tax collection presence with the Bangsamoro Revenue Office once the Revenue Code is enacted, while maintaining coordination with BIR for national taxes.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes. While budgeting is primarily a technical governance function, Islamic public finance principles provide an ethical framework. The principle of amanah (trust/stewardship) is foundational —public funds are a sacred trust. The prohibition of israf (extravagance/waste) reinforces fiscal discipline. The principle of maslahah (public interest) aligns with Art. XII, Sec. 19's priority for education, health, and social services —corresponding to the Islamic hierarchy of needs (daruriyyat, hajiyyat, tahsiniyyat).

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area?

A. Three principles: (1) Amanah —budget officials are trustees of public money; (2) Prohibition of israf —government spending must be proportionate, not extravagant; (3) Maslahah —budget allocation should prioritize essential services and public welfare. No specific Shari'ah rules on taxation or budgeting procedure override the BOL's fiscal framework, but these principles should guide the exercise of budgetary discretion.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national budgeting rules?

A. BARMM's divergence authority is narrower for budgeting than for most powers. As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), Art. VII, Sec. 28 requires budget system consistency with national rules, and Art. XII, Sec. 19 applies DBM/DILG rules to block grant allocation. As established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), COA's constitutional audit authority cannot be superseded. Within these constraints, BARMM can: (a) set its own budget priorities within the required education/health/social services preference; (b) create its own special purpose funds (SDF, Contingent Fund, IMPACT, etc.); (c) enact its own Revenue Code defining regional tax rates and exemptions; (d) establish its own fiscal calendar and submission schedule; (e) develop Shari'ah-informed fiscal principles (e.g., amanah-based accountability standards).

Q. If BARMM enacts different fiscal standards, what are the implications?

A. (1) Revenue Code differences: a Bangsamoro Revenue Code can set different tax rates and exemptions within the Art. XII, Sec. 9 limitations (12 categories of taxes the Bangsamoro Government cannot impose). (2) Budget process differences: BAA 84 establishes the Bangsamoro budget system, but must remain consistent with national rules per Art. VII, Sec. 28. (3) Cross-boundary fiscal operations: corporations operating in both BARMM and non-BARMM areas face dual tax compliance —the IFPB resolves such disputes.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority?

A. Two primary overlaps: (1) Tax collection: BIR and the Bangsamoro Revenue Office both have potential collection authority —Art. XII, Sec. 11 provides for transition, and Sec. 12 addresses corporate tax allocation, but the operational handover is incomplete. The IFPB is the designated dispute resolution mechanism (Art. XII, Sec. 9). (2) Block grant allocation rules: Art. XII, Sec. 19 applies DBM/DILG rules, but the specific interaction between Bangsamoro budget priorities and national budgeting procedures can create friction.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. Three bills: PB No. 286 (Bangsamoro Revenue Code —the most critical pending legislation), PB No. 240 (budget submission schedule), and PB No. 400 (Bangsamoro Trust Fund for development continuity). Enactment of the Revenue Code would be the most significant legislative achievement for this power after BAA 84.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power?

A. All development goals depend on budgeting —it is the fiscal infrastructure for all government action. BDP Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy) is the dedicated fiscal chapter, targeting revenue mobilization, block grant optimization, and development financing. Matatag Pillar 1 (Good Governance) depends on transparent, performance-based budgeting. Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience) depends on adequate budget allocation to economic programs and infrastructure.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if the legislative gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without a Revenue Code: the Bangsamoro Government remains dependent on the block grant and BIR-collected taxes, unable to independently mobilize regional revenue —undermining fiscal autonomy. Without full operationalization of the Bangsamoro Revenue Office: revenue leakage continues and the government cannot exercise the taxing powers Art. XII, Sec. 6 grants. Without strengthened absorption capacity: appropriated funds go unspent, and development projects are delayed.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What is the block grant formula and how is it calculated?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 16: for the budget year immediately following the BOL's effectivity, the block grant = 5% of net NIRTA of BIR + net collections of Bureau of Customs from the 3rd fiscal year preceding the current year. "Net" NIRTA = total BIR internal revenue tax collections minus LGU IRA, tax refunds, informers' rewards, and earmarked amounts. The formula is reviewed by the IFPB every 5 years (Art. XII, Sec. 22). After 20 years, certain revenues are deducted from the block grant (Art. XII, Sec. 21).

Q. What taxes can the Bangsamoro Government NOT impose?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 9 enumerates 12 categories the Bangsamoro Government's taxing power cannot extend to: (a) income tax (except on banks); (b) customs duties; (c) transit taxes; (d) taxes on marginal farmers'/fisherfolk's products; (e) taxes on BOI-certified pioneer enterprises; (f) excise taxes; (g) VAT; (h) transportation gross receipts taxes; (i) reinsurance premiums; (j) taxes on Philippine exports; (k) taxes on cooperatives under RA 6810/RA 6938; (l) taxes on national government entities.

However, where all taxable elements are within BARMM, Parliament may impose 4 taxes to the exclusion of BIR: Capital Gains Tax, Documentary Stamp Tax, Donor's Tax, and Estate Tax.

Q. What is the 45% PS cap and what does it include?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 20(a): "The total appropriations ... for Personal Services of the Bangsamoro Government for one (1) fiscal year shall not exceed forty-five percent (45%) of the total revenue sources." This covers salaries, wages, and allowances of all Bangsamoro Government personnel. Excluded: salaries of public utility/economic enterprise employees (charged against their own budgets). The 45% is calculated against total revenue sources (Art. XII, Sec. 6), not just the block grant.

Q. What happens when the block grant formula is reviewed?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 22: the IFPB reviews the formula 5 years after effectivity and every 5 years thereafter, considering fiscal needs, actual revenues generated, and ensuring transparent/performance-based expenditure. If the National Government no longer has representatives in the IFPB, the review is undertaken by the Intergovernmental Relations Body (Art. VI, Sec. 2).

Q. What is the Special Development Fund (SDF)?

A. Art. XIV, Sec. 2 provides for a P50 billion SDF over 10 years for rehabilitation and development of conflict-affected areas. BAA 22 implements this provision, with validity extended by BAA 34, 51. The SDF is separate from the block grant and annual GAA —it is a peace dividend for communities most affected by decades of armed conflict.

Q. How does BAA 84 (Budget System Act) relate to the BOL's fiscal framework?

A. BAA 84 fulfills the express mandate of Art. VII, Sec. 28 to enact a law prescribing "the form, content, and manner of preparation of the Bangsamoro budget." It codifies: the budget cycle, preparation procedures, submission requirements, appropriation processes, execution rules, and accountability mechanisms. BAA 84 operates within the constraints of Art. XII (fiscal autonomy framework) and must be "consistent with existing laws, rules and regulations of the National Government."

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
No Revenue Code despite priority code mandate Bangsamoro Tax and Revenue Code Taxing powers, tax rates, exemptions, incentives; capital gains/documentary stamp/donor's/estate tax administration; Bangsamoro Revenue Office authority and enforcement; tax dispute resolution; coordination with BIR Art. XII, Sec. 14 —"The Parliament shall enact a Bangsamoro tax and revenue code"; Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) (priority code)
No comprehensive public financial management code Bangsamoro Public Financial Management Act Integrated accounting standards; financial reporting; internal audit framework per RA 3456; fiscal transparency and disclosure; performance-based budgeting standards beyond BAA 84; COA coordination Art. XII, Sec. 1 (fiscal autonomy); Art. XII, Sec. 2 (auditing); Art. VII, Sec. 28
No trust fund for development continuity Bangsamoro Trust Fund Act Mechanism for multi-year development funding continuity; investment strategy for unspent funds; governance structure; relationship with SDF and block grant Art. XII, Sec. 1 (fiscal autonomy); PB No. 400

Back to Table of Contents
(g)
Business Name Registration
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(g)

Power 7 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(g) Business Name Registration

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(g) — "Business name registration;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(g) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required No express consistency requirement in the enumerated power. However, Art. XIII, Sec. 25 requires business names registered in BARMM to be "listed in the Philippine Business Registry" — establishing an interoperability requirement with the national system without subordinating Bangsamoro registration authority to national law.
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT) — Bureau of Trade and Industry (BAA 13, Book VII, Title V, Ch. 4, Sec. 11)
Supporting Agencies Bangsamoro Economic Zone Authority (BEZA); Bangsamoro Board of Investments (BBOI); Bangsamoro Halal Board
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 4 (Development Framework)
Priority Code None. Business name registration is not covered by a named priority code. A Bangsamoro Trade and Industry Code or Business Registration Act would be a standalone legislative initiative.

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(g) "Business name registration;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority to register business names within the region. This is a discrete regulatory function — the power to issue, maintain, and regulate business name registrations.
Art. XIII, Sec. 25 "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to register business names which shall be listed in the Philippine Business Registry." The sole elaborating provision. Two elements: (1) grants registration power to the Bangsamoro Government; (2) requires interoperability — registered names must be listed in the Philippine Business Registry (PBR), the national database administered by DTI under EO 587. This is a data interoperability mandate, not a subordination of Bangsamoro registration authority to DTI.
Art. XIII, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Government shall establish an economic system based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution." General economic framework. The constitutional basis for trade and industry regulation — Art. XII (National Economy and Patrimony) provides the floor.
Art. XIII, Sec. 2 "The Bangsamoro Government shall promote the effective use of economic resources and endeavor to attain economic development that facilitates growth and full employment, human development, and social justice." Positions business registration as an instrument of economic development — streamlined registration promotes enterprise formation and employment.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Book VII, Title V: establishes MTIT and the Bureau of Trade and Industry with functions including business name registration (Sec. 11(c)); creates the one-stop-shop Business Development Support Center (Sec. 3(x)) In Force
BAA 85 — General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter V (MTIT) allocates operational budget, confirming institutional existence and operational funding In Force
No standalone business name registration act or trade and industry code has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
No adopted resolutions directly addressing business name registration have been identified as of the current legislative record.

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
Act No. 3883 (1931) Business Name Law Original law requiring registration of business names, trade names, and trade styles with the Bureau of Commerce (later DTI). Requires all sole proprietorships and partnerships using trade names to register. Suppletory: applies in BARMM pending enactment of a Bangsamoro business registration law. DTI continues to process registrations under this Act.
RA 11232 (2018) Revised Corporation Code of the Philippines Governs the registration and regulation of corporations and partnerships with the SEC. Distinct from business name registration (DTI) but overlaps for entities using trade names. Applies directly: SEC retains jurisdiction over corporate registrations, including those in BARMM. Not affected by Art. V, Sec. 2(g), which covers business names, not corporate registrations.
RA 11032 (2018) Ease of Doing Business and Efficient Government Service Delivery Act Mandates streamlined government processes including business registration; establishes Anti-Red Tape Authority (ARTA); sets processing time standards Applies directly: ARTA standards apply to all government agencies including those in BARMM. A Bangsamoro business registration system must comply with ARTA timelines.
RA 9501 (2008) Magna Carta for MSMEs Comprehensive MSME framework — simplifies business registration, provides incentives Suppletory: MSME classification and incentive framework applies to BARMM enterprises.
RA 9178 (2002) Barangay Micro Business Enterprises (BMBEs) Act Simplified registration for micro enterprises at the barangay level; provides tax exemptions and incentives Suppletory: BMBE registration applies in BARMM barangays pending regional legislation.
RA 10644 (2014) Go Negosyo Act Creates Negosyo Centers in LGUs as one-stop shops for business registration, permits, and enterprise development Suppletory: Negosyo Centers operate in BARMM LGUs. A Bangsamoro system could integrate these centers.
EO 587 Directing DTI to Establish and Administer the Philippine Business Registry (PBR) Creates the PBR — the integrated national database of registered business entities. BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 25 expressly requires BARMM-registered business names to be listed in the PBR. Directly relevant: establishes the national system with which BARMM must interoperate per BOL mandate.
RA 7394 (1992) Consumer Act of the Philippines Establishes consumer protection standards including trade name protection and unfair competition Suppletory: consumer protection provisions apply to BARMM businesses. BAA 13 Sec. 11(c) references the Fair Trade Laws and consumer protection.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Maslahah (Public Interest / Welfare) Legislation serving the common good is justified under Islamic jurisprudence, even where no specific textual authority exists. Business registration serves maslahah by enabling orderly commerce, protecting consumers, and preventing fraud.
Amanah (Trust / Stewardship) Persons conducting business under registered names bear a duty of transparency and accountability to customers and the public. Business name registration enforces amanah by requiring disclosure of the person behind a trade name — preventing anonymous commercial fraud.
Halal Certification The Bangsamoro Halal Board (BAA 13, attached to MTIT) oversees halal industry development, accreditation of certifying bodies, and halal auditors. Business name registration intersects with halal certification — enterprises using halal designations in their business names should be verified through the Halal Board's accreditation system. A Bangsamoro business registration law could integrate halal compliance into the registration process.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area — defining boundaries of autonomy, resolving jurisdiction questions, or interpreting related provisions.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro business name registration authority or the jurisdictional boundary between DTI and BARMM business registration.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs. Shows legislative intent and pending proposals.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
No pending Parliament Bill specifically addressing business name registration has been identified. The power remains exercised solely through MTIT's BAA 13 general mandate.

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development Streamlined business registration is a prerequisite for enterprise formation and private sector growth
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 4: Development Framework Business registration efficiency enables the trade and investment environment needed for inclusive growth
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience Simplified business registration reduces barriers to enterprise creation and strengthens the local business ecosystem

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over business name registration?

A. Article V, Section 2(g) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "business name registration" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This is a specific, discrete regulatory function — the authority to register, maintain records of, and regulate business names used by enterprises within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. One provision elaborates on this power:

Art. XIII, Sec. 25 states: "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to register business names which shall be listed in the Philippine Business Registry." This provision: (1) restates the registration authority in more specific terms; and (2) creates an interoperability requirement — Bangsamoro-registered business names must be listed in the Philippine Business Registry (PBR), the integrated national database established by DTI under EO 587. This is not a "consistent with national law" requirement — it is a data-sharing mandate ensuring that businesses registered in BARMM are visible in the national commercial database.2

Additionally, Art. XIII, Sec. 1 and Sec. 2 provide the general economic framework within which business registration operates — requiring the Bangsamoro economic system to be "based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution" and to promote "economic development that facilitates growth and full employment."34

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. No. The BOL does not expressly mandate the Parliament to enact a business registration law by name. Art. XIII, Sec. 25 grants the power but does not use the mandatory language "the Parliament shall enact" or name a specific piece of legislation (unlike Art. XIII, Sec. 24 for cooperatives or Art. XII, Sec. 14 for the Revenue Code). The power is therefore exercised through the general legislative authority of Parliament and the institutional mandate of MTIT under BAA 13.

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power?

A. No. The BOL does not create a specific registration office for business names. The function is institutionally assigned to MTIT through BAA 13, specifically the Bureau of Trade and Industry (Sec. 11(c)), which is tasked with the "Implementation of the Fair Trade Laws, Price Act and related policies on consumer protection and business name registration."5

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. One condition:

PBR listing requirement: Art. XIII, Sec. 25 requires that BARMM-registered business names "shall be listed in the Philippine Business Registry." This is a mandatory interoperability condition — not a subordination of BARMM registration to DTI authority, but a requirement that the BARMM registration system feed data into the national database. This protects against duplicate business name registrations across jurisdictions and ensures that BARMM-registered businesses are discoverable nationally.2

No "consistent with national law" or "in accordance with" qualifier is attached to this power. The constitutional floor (Art. X, Sec. 15) applies as to all 55 powers.

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to business name registration?

A. Three categories of constitutional provisions apply:

(1) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): The universal constitutional boundary — all autonomous region powers operate "within the framework of this Constitution."8

(2) National Economy and Patrimony (Art. XII): Art. XII, Sec. 1 mandates the State to promote "a more equitable distribution of opportunities, income, and wealth" and to promote "industrialization and full employment." Business name registration facilitates enterprise formation in furtherance of this mandate.

(3) Equal Protection and Due Process (Art. III, Sec. 1): "No person shall be deprived of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, nor shall any person be denied the equal protection of the laws." Business registration decisions (approval, denial, cancellation) must satisfy procedural due process. Business owners in BARMM must not face more burdensome registration requirements than those outside BARMM without rational basis.9

Q. What rights and privileges does the Constitution guarantee in this area?

A. (1) Right to engage in trade and livelihood — Art. XII provisions promote private enterprise and the right to engage in lawful business. Business name registration cannot be used as a barrier to this right. (2) Equal protection — differential treatment of BARMM-registered businesses must rest on substantial distinctions. (3) Due process — denial or cancellation of business name registration must provide notice, hearing, and appeal.

Q. What constitutional mandates exist for legislation or institutions in this area?

A. No specific constitutional mandate for business name registration exists. The general mandate to promote trade and enterprise (Art. XII) applies.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for business name registration?

A. The national framework provides a simple, low-cost registration system administered by the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI). Act No. 3883 (Business Name Law, 1931) requires persons using business names, trade names, or trade styles to register with DTI. EO 587 established the Philippine Business Registry (PBR) — an integrated online database linking DTI business name registrations with SEC corporate registrations and other government databases. RA 11032 (Ease of Doing Business Act) mandates processing time standards for registration. RA 9501 (Magna Carta for MSMEs) and RA 9178 (BMBEs Act) provide simplified registration for micro and small enterprises.

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. All national business registration laws apply in the absence of a Bangsamoro business registration law:

(1) Act No. 3883 — DTI registers all business names in BARMM. Applies suppletorily.10 (2) EO 587 — PBR operates in BARMM; BOL Art. XIII, Sec. 25 expressly requires interoperability.11 (3) RA 11032 — ARTA standards apply to all government agencies including BARMM. Applies directly. (4) RA 11232 — SEC retains corporate registration jurisdiction. Applies directly. (5) RA 9501, RA 9178, RA 10644 — suppletory application for MSME, BMBE, and Negosyo Center frameworks.

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. The Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) exercises full business name registration authority in BARMM through its regional and provincial offices. DTI processes all business name registrations, issues Certificates of Registration, and maintains the PBR database entries for BARMM businesses. When a Bangsamoro business registration law is enacted, the question is whether MTIT replaces DTI within BARMM for business name registration or whether DTI retains a coordination role for PBR interoperability.11

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power?

A. The Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT) is the lead ministry, specifically the Bureau of Trade and Industry. BAA 13, Book VII, Title V, Chapter 4, Sec. 11(c) assigns business name registration to this bureau. BAA 85, Chapter V (MTIT) allocates its operational budget.512

Q. What standalone legislation (BAAs) has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. No standalone BAA has been enacted for business name registration as of BAA 89. This power is exercised entirely through MTIT's BAA 13 general mandate.

Q. What are MTIT's mandate and functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book VII, Title V defines MTIT comprehensively:

Mandate (Sec. 2): MTIT is "the primary government agency mandated to implement laws, programs and projects on trade, investments, and tourism" through: (a) Comprehensive industrial growth strategy; (b) Progressive and socially responsible liberalization program; and (c) Policies designed for the expansion and diversification of trade.13

Powers and functions (Sec. 3) — 26 enumerated functions including: - (a) "Formulate policies, plans, programs and projects for the development of the trade, investments, and tourism in the region;" - (n) "Promote, develop, and regulate Halal Industry development, and accredit Halal certifying bodies and Halal auditors;" - (x) "Create, in coordination with related agencies of the Bangsamoro Government, a one-stop-shop Business Development Support Center to promote the growth of micro, small and medium-scale businesses and enterprises;" - (y) "Administratively adjudicate and impose reasonable fines and penalties for violation of existing trade, investments, tourism, and cooperative development laws;"14

Bureau of Trade and Industry (Sec. 11) — headed by a Director II, with functions including: - (c) "Assist the Minister in the Implementation of the Fair Trade Laws, Price Act and related policies on consumer protection and business name registration;"5

Divisions under the Bureau of Trade and Industry (Sec. 12): (a) Trade Regulation and Consumer Protection Division; (b) Enterprise and Trade Development Division; (c) Industry Development and Investment Promotion Division.

Attached agencies (Sec. 5): (1) Bangsamoro Economic Zone Authority (BEZA); (2) Bangsamoro Halal Board.

Q. What can MTIT NOT do without a standalone business registration act?

A. While BAA 13 assigns business name registration to the Bureau of Trade and Industry, it does not provide: a comprehensive registration framework with procedures, requirements, forms, and timelines; penalty provisions for operating under an unregistered business name; a dispute resolution mechanism for business name conflicts; fee schedules for registration; a digital registration system with PBR interoperability (as required by Art. XIII, Sec. 25); or a clear jurisdictional boundary with DTI for the transition of registration authority. BAA 13 assigns the function but does not create the regulatory framework for its full exercise.

Q. Have any parliamentary resolutions signaled intent to legislate in this area?

A. No parliamentary resolutions specifically addressing business name registration have been identified.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is the assigned lead MOA the correct one for this power?

A. Yes. MTIT is the correct lead ministry. BAA 13 Sec. 11(c) expressly assigns business name registration to the Bureau of Trade and Industry within MTIT. The national counterpart — DTI — performs the same function nationally. There is no mismatch between the power's scope and the MOA's mandate.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power?

A. - Lead ministry: MTIT (BAA 13, Book VII, Title V; BAA 85, Chapter V) - Bureau of Trade and Industry: Division responsible for business name registration (BAA 13, Sec. 11(c)) - Bangsamoro Halal Board: Attached to MTIT (BAA 13, Sec. 5) — relevant for verifying halal designations in business names - BEZA: Attached to MTIT (BAA 13, Sec. 5; Sec. 22-23) — economic zone enterprises have separate registration - MTIT Provincial Offices: 7 field offices (BAA 13, Sec. 19) — potential registration access points - Negosyo Centers: Operating in BARMM LGUs under RA 10644 (national program)

Q. What institutions does the BOL mandate that have NOT been created yet?

A. The BOL does not mandate a specific institution for business name registration beyond MTIT. However, Art. XIII, Sec. 25's requirement that registrations be "listed in the Philippine Business Registry" implies a technical integration mechanism — either a BARMM registration database linked to the PBR, or a data-sharing agreement with DTI — that has not been formally established.

Q. Does this power require creating NEW institutions that the BOL does not specifically name?

A. No new institutions are required. A Bangsamoro Business Registration Unit within the Bureau of Trade and Industry could be formalized by legislation to handle the function, with a digital registration system interoperable with the PBR. This would be an organizational unit, not a new institution.

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. DTI registers all business names within BARMM through its regional and provincial offices. DTI issues Certificates of Business Name Registration, maintains records in the PBR, and processes renewals (business name registrations are valid for 5 years). All sole proprietorships and partnerships using trade names in BARMM must register with DTI.

Q. What national government programs and services operate in BARMM for this area?

A. (1) DTI business name registration — routine processing of new registrations and renewals; (2) Negosyo Centers — one-stop shops in BARMM LGUs for business registration, permits, and enterprise development (RA 10644); (3) Philippine Business Registry (PBR) — online database listing all registered businesses including those in BARMM; (4) ARTA compliance monitoring — processing time standards for government transactions including business registration.

Q. What is the status of devolution for this power?

A. Devolution has not commenced. DTI retains full business name registration authority within BARMM. MTIT's Bureau of Trade and Industry has an assigned function under BAA 13 Sec. 11(c) but has not operationalized an independent registration system. No memorandum of agreement, executive order, or transition plan governs the transfer of the registration function from DTI to MTIT.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power?

A. MTIT has been institutionally established under BAA 13 with the Bureau of Trade and Industry, provincial offices, and attached agencies. It has begun exercising its programmatic functions — trade promotion, investment facilitation, halal industry development, and coordination with LGUs on economic development. However, the business name registration function itself has not been independently operationalized — registrations continue to be processed through DTI.

Q. Where is the BARMM stronger than the national government in this area?

A. BARMM has two potential advantages, though neither has been fully realized:

(1) Halal integration — MTIT, through the Halal Board, can integrate halal certification verification into the business registration process. DTI does not have this capability. Enterprises using halal designations could be simultaneously registered and halal-verified.

(2) Conflict-sensitive accessibility — MTIT's 7 provincial offices and the one-stop-shop Business Development Support Center (BAA 13 Sec. 3(x)) can be designed for BARMM's context — delivering services in local languages, accommodating post-conflict enterprise formation, and integrating with community-based economic development programs.

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. The single biggest constraint is the absence of a standalone business registration act. Without it: MTIT cannot independently register business names — the regulatory framework (standards, forms, fees, timelines, penalties, appeals) does not exist in Bangsamoro law; the PBR interoperability required by Art. XIII, Sec. 25 has no implementing mechanism; and the jurisdictional boundary with DTI remains undefined.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built for this area that BARMM does not yet have?

A. - Legislative depth: Act No. 3883 has been operational since 1931, supplemented by RA 11032, RA 9501, and multiple DTI administrative orders. - Institutional infrastructure: DTI has 17 regional offices, provincial offices in every province, and the Bureau of Trade Regulation and Consumer Protection at the national level. - Digital systems: The Philippine Business Registry (PBR) is an integrated online platform for business name searches, new registrations, and renewals. DTI's eBNRS (electronic Business Name Registration System) enables online processing. - Negosyo Centers: 960+ centers in LGUs nationwide, serving as one-stop shops for business registration.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) No halal integration: DTI registration does not verify halal claims in business names — a significant gap in a region where halal certification is central to consumer trust. (2) Language barriers: DTI services are primarily in Filipino and English; registration assistance in Maguindanaon, Maranao, Tausug, and Sama is limited. (3) No conflict-sensitive enterprise support: The national system does not account for enterprise formation in post-conflict, IDP, or ex-combatant livelihood contexts. (4) Accessibility: DTI provincial offices in island provinces (Basilan, Tawi-Tawi) are underresourced.

Q. What should BARMM replicate, adapt, or replace from the national system?

A. - Replicate: registration standards (business name search, uniqueness verification, validity periods), digital registration (online system), and processing timelines (ARTA-compliant). - Adapt: one-stop-shop model — integrate business name registration with LGU business permits, halal certification, BIR/BRO tax registration, and MTIT trade facilitation services. Adapt for local languages and conflict-affected enterprise formation. - Replace: institutional framework — MTIT (Bureau of Trade and Industry) replaces DTI for business name registration within BARMM. The Bangsamoro system must maintain PBR interoperability per Art. XIII, Sec. 25 but operates independently.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Minimal direct Shari'ah dimension. Business name registration is a regulatory-administrative function. However, two indirect connections exist: (1) the halal certification system — enterprises using halal designations in their business names should be verified for compliance, creating an intersection between business name registration and the Bangsamoro Halal Board's accreditation mandate under BAA 13; and (2) the general Shari'ah principles of maslahah (public interest) and amanah (trust/stewardship) support orderly business registration as a mechanism for consumer protection and commercial transparency.

Q. What does the BOL say about Shari'ah in relation to this power?

A. The BOL does not mention Shari'ah specifically in Art. XIII, Sec. 25 (business name registration). The halal connection arises from BAA 13, which attaches the Bangsamoro Halal Board* to MTIT (Sec. 5) and assigns MTIT the function of promoting, developing, and regulating "Halal Industry development, and accredit Halal certifying bodies and Halal auditors"*** (Sec. 3(n)).6

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area?

A. No specific Shari'ah principles beyond the general values of maslahah (public interest), amanah (trust), and 'adl (justice) directly govern business name registration. A Bangsamoro business registration law should, however, include provisions for verifying halal claims in business names — preventing fraudulent use of halal designations, which would violate consumer trust and Islamic commercial ethics.

Q. What existing Shari'ah legislation or jurisprudence applies?

A. PD 1083 (Code of Muslim Personal Laws) does not address business name registration. No Shari'ah court jurisdiction over business name disputes exists under current law. Commercial disputes arising from business name conflicts between Muslim parties could potentially fall within Shari'ah District Court jurisdiction under BOL Art. X, Sec. 6(d) — "All actions arising from customary and Shari'ah compliant contracts in which the parties are Muslims."7

Q. How should Shari'ah compliance be integrated into Bangsamoro legislation for this power?

A. The recommended approach is principled — legislation guided by Islamic values without mandating specific Shari'ah compliance for business name registration itself. However, a Bangsamoro business registration law should include: (a) a prohibition on fraudulent halal designations in business names, enforceable through the Halal Board; and (b) a dispute resolution option through Shari'ah courts for business name disputes between Muslim parties.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national business name registration law?

A. BARMM has broad legislative authority to diverge. The BOL expressly grants the power to register business names (Art. V, Sec. 2(g); Art. XIII, Sec. 25) — this is congressional authorization for a distinct regional registration system. No consistency requirement with national business name law is imposed. The sole constraint is the PBR listing requirement (Art. XIII, Sec. 25), which mandates interoperability but not conformity with DTI procedures.

As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), the limits are: (1) the PBR interoperability mandate — BARMM cannot operate a closed system; (2) the constitutional floor established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E) — equal protection and due process in registration decisions. Within these limits, BARMM can establish its own registration procedures, fee schedules, validity periods, penalty provisions, and dispute resolution mechanisms — and integrate halal verification as a distinct feature.

Q. If BARMM enacts different standards, what are the implications for rights holders and cross-boundary operations?

A. (1) For business owners within BARMM: They would register with MTIT instead of DTI — potentially gaining faster processing through a local one-stop-shop and halal integration. The PBR listing ensures their registration is nationally visible.

(2) For businesses operating across BARMM and non-BARMM areas: The PBR listing requirement prevents confusion. However, a mutual recognition provision may be needed — should a DTI-registered business operating in BARMM also register with MTIT? A Bangsamoro business registration law should address cross-jurisdictional recognition.

Q. What happens to national law when the Parliament enacts a BAA on this subject?

A. The BAA governs business name registration within BARMM. DTI ceases to exercise registration authority within BARMM (unless the BAA provides otherwise for transition). National law (Act No. 3883, RA 11032) applies suppletorily where the BAA is silent. Constitutional provisions (equal protection, due process) always apply.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority?

A. Yes. The primary overlap is between MTIT (Bureau of Trade and Industry) and DTI. Currently, DTI registers all business names in BARMM. MTIT has an assigned function under BAA 13 but no operational registration system. The jurisdictional boundary is entirely undefined — no MOA, EO, or transition plan governs the transfer. A Bangsamoro business registration act must clarify: (a) when MTIT assumes registration authority; (b) what happens to existing DTI-registered businesses in BARMM; (c) how PBR interoperability is achieved; and (d) whether DTI retains any coordination role.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. No pending Parliament Bills specifically addressing business name registration have been identified. This is a legislative vacuum — no bills filed, no resolutions adopted, and no legislative intent signaled for this power area.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 7 (Economic Development) depends on an efficient business registration system to achieve private sector growth targets. Enterprise formation — the starting point for job creation and economic activity — begins with business registration. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028), Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience), requires streamlined business registration to reduce barriers to enterprise creation in BARMM's post-conflict economic context.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if the legislative gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without a Bangsamoro business registration act: MTIT cannot independently register business names — all registrations flow through DTI's national system. The one-stop-shop vision (BAA 13 Sec. 3(x)) cannot fully integrate business name registration with LGU permits and MTIT trade services. The halal verification integration cannot be formalized. The PBR interoperability required by Art. XIII, Sec. 25 lacks an implementing mechanism on the BARMM side. Enterprise formation in conflict-affected areas continues through a national system not designed for BARMM's context.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What is the significance of the PBR listing requirement in Art. XIII, Sec. 25?

A. Art. XIII, Sec. 25's requirement that BARMM-registered business names "shall be listed in the Philippine Business Registry" serves three purposes: (1) prevents duplicate registration — a business name registered in BARMM cannot conflict with a name already registered elsewhere in the Philippines, and vice versa; (2) ensures national commercial visibility — BARMM-registered businesses are discoverable by potential customers, partners, and regulators nationwide; (3) maintains commercial order — a single national database prevents the fragmentation that could occur if BARMM maintained a completely separate system. This is a data interoperability requirement, not a subordination of BARMM registration authority to DTI. MTIT can register business names under its own procedures and standards, but must feed the data into the PBR.2

Q. How does this power differ from corporate registration (SEC) and business permits (LGUs)?

A. Three distinct registration systems exist:

(1) Business name registration (Art. V, Sec. 2(g)) — the registration of sole proprietorships and partnerships operating under trade names. This is the power granted to BARMM. Nationally administered by DTI.

(2) Corporate registration — the registration of corporations and partnerships with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) under RA 11232. This is not part of Art. V, Sec. 2(g) — SEC retains jurisdiction over corporate entities in BARMM.

(3) Business permits — the licensing of businesses to operate in specific LGUs, administered by municipal or city governments under the Local Government Code (RA 7160) and BAA 49. This is a local government function, not a regional government function.

A Bangsamoro business registration law should integrate with all three — ensuring that MTIT business name registration, SEC corporate registration, and LGU business permits form a coherent process.

Q. How does this power interact with power (zz) Trade and Industry?

A. Power (zz) — "trade and industry" (Art. V, Sec. 2(zz)) — is the broader economic regulatory power. Business name registration (power (g)) is a specific component of the trade and industry framework. Art. XIII, Sec. 25 appears within the "Trade and Industry and Tourism" subsection of the BOL. MTIT is the lead for both powers. A comprehensive Bangsamoro Trade and Industry Code could address business name registration as one chapter within a broader framework — or it could be enacted as a standalone Bangsamoro Business Registration Act. Either approach fulfills the BOL mandate.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

What laws should the Bangsamoro Parliament pass to fully exercise this power? Note: most powers are already exercised by ministries/offices/agencies through their BAA 13 (Administrative Code) mandates, but standalone legislation codifies standards, creates regulatory frameworks, and establishes institutional structures that a general mandate cannot.

Gap Type Gap What Law / Institution Should Be Created What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No standalone business registration law despite express BOL power grant Bangsamoro Business Registration Act (or chapter within a Trade and Industry Code) Registration procedures, requirements, and forms; fee schedules; validity periods; renewal process; penalty provisions for operating under unregistered business names; halal designation verification; dispute resolution; PBR interoperability mechanism; jurisdictional boundary with DTI; digital registration system; one-stop-shop integration with LGU permits Art. V, Sec. 2(g); Art. XIII, Sec. 25
Institutional No operational Bangsamoro business name registration system — MTIT has the mandate but not the implementing framework Bangsamoro Business Registration System (unit within MTIT Bureau of Trade and Industry) Digital registration platform interoperable with PBR; provincial registration access points; halal compliance integration; data-sharing agreement with DTI Art. XIII, Sec. 25 — "listed in the Philippine Business Registry"
