Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 1: BARMM Extended Transition Complete territorial and institutional setup of BARMM; integrate SGA communities into the formal governance framework through municipality creation
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 5: Governance and Institutional Development Strengthen LGU governance and decentralize service delivery; new municipalities expand governance reach to underserved communities
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 1: Good Governance Effective local governance through properly constituted LGUs; municipality creation ensures responsive government closest to the people

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over municipality and barangay creation?

A. Article V, Section 2(l) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "creation, division, merger, abolition or alteration of boundaries of municipalities and barangays" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This covers the full range of LGU restructuring at the municipal and barangay levels — creating new units, splitting existing ones, combining them, eliminating them, or redrawing their boundaries.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Art. VI, Sec. 10 provides the detailed operational framework:

(1) "The Parliament may create, divide, merge, abolish, or substantially alter boundaries of municipalities or barangays in accordance with a law enacted by the Parliament." This vests the power in the Parliament as a legislative function — each action requires a separate enacted law.2

(2) Two mandatory requirements: "the criteria laid down in Republic Act No. 7160, as amended, and other national laws shall be satisfied" and "it shall be approved by a majority of the votes cast in a plebiscite in the political units directly affected." These are the RA 7160 criteria and the plebiscite requirement — neither of which can be waived by Parliament.2

(3) Congressional coordination for legislative districts: "When such acts require the creation of a legislative district, the Bangsamoro Government shall cooperate and coordinate with the National Government through the Philippine Congress-Bangsamoro Parliament Forum."2

(4) Express prohibition: "Nothing in this Organic Law shall be construed to allow the Bangsamoro Government to create legislative districts."2

(5) LGU privilege protection: "The privileges already enjoyed by local government units under Republic Act No. 7160 ... shall not be diminished."2

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. Yes. Art. VI, Sec. 10 (para. 1) mandates "a Bangsamoro local government code to be enacted by the Parliament." This is one of the 7 priority codes listed in Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a). This mandate has been fulfilled by BAA 49 (Local Governance Code).2

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power?

A. The BOL does not create a specific body solely for municipality/barangay creation. However, it mandates the Bangsamoro local government code which provides the institutional framework. BAA 13 establishes MILG as the ministry exercising general supervision over LGUs, and BAA 13, Sec. 14-28 creates the Special Geographic Area Development Authority to manage the 63 SGA barangays — which eventually became the 8 new SGA municipalities (BAA 41-48).34

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Four express limitations:

(1) RA 7160 criteria: income, population, and land area thresholds must be satisfied — this is among the BOL's most explicit national law consistency requirements.2

(2) Plebiscite requirement: majority vote approval in directly affected political units.2

(3) Legislative district prohibition: BARMM cannot create legislative districts under any circumstance.2

(4) Non-diminution of LGU privileges: existing LGU privileges under RA 7160 cannot be reduced.2

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to municipality and barangay creation?

A. Three categories:

(1) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): All autonomous region powers operate "within the framework of this Constitution." The BOL chapeau (Art. V, Sec. 2) reiterates this. This is the universal constitutional boundary.5

(2) LGU Creation (Art. X, Sec. 10): "No province, city, municipality, or barangay may be created, divided, merged, abolished, or its boundary substantially altered, except in accordance with the criteria established in the local government code and subject to approval by a majority of the votes cast in a plebiscite in the political units directly affected." The BOL's requirements in Art. VI, Sec. 10 directly mirror this constitutional mandate.6

(3) Congressional District Creation (Art. VI, Sec. 5): Legislative district apportionment is a congressional power. The BOL's prohibition on BARMM creating legislative districts directly implements this constitutional allocation.7

Q. What constitutional mandates exist for this area?

A. Art. X, Sec. 10 establishes the constitutional mandate that all LGU creation requires (a) compliance with local government code criteria and (b) plebiscite approval. Both are non-waivable. The BOL faithfully reproduces these requirements in Art. VI, Sec. 10.6

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for LGU creation?

A. RA 7160 (Local Government Code, 1991) sets the criteria: for municipality creation — at least PhP 2.5 million average annual income for the last two consecutive years (Sec. 442), population of at least 25,000, and a contiguous land area of at least 50 square kilometers (with exceptions for islands and cities); for barangay creation — at least 2,000 inhabitants for a metropolitan city/city barangay or 5,000 for a municipal barangay (Sec. 386). RA 7160 also governs mergers, divisions, and boundary alterations, requiring compliance with these thresholds and plebiscite approval.8

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. (1) RA 7160 — applies directly by express BOL mandate (Art. VI, Sec. 10). Its criteria are the binding standard for all BARMM municipality and barangay creation. (2) RA 11550 — the national law dividing Maguindanao into two provinces. Applies directly as a congressional act. Demonstrates that province-level restructuring within BARMM requires congressional action, not just Parliament legislation.89

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. The Commission on Elections (COMELEC) — through its Bangsamoro Electoral Office — conducts the required plebiscites for municipality and barangay creation. COMELEC's authority over plebiscites is constitutionally vested and cannot be transferred to BARMM. The Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) provides technical assistance on RA 7160 compliance verification, though MILG performs the primary supervisory function within BARMM.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power?

A. The Ministry of the Interior and Local Government (MILG) is the lead agency. BAA 13, Book V, Title I, Chapter 1, Sec. 2 defines its mandate: "The Ministry of the Interior and Local Government shall exercise general supervision over the constituent local governments units of the Bangsamoro Government, and ensure public safety and disaster preparedness, local autonomy, decentralization, and community empowerment." BAA 85, Chapter XII allocates its operational budget.310

Q. What standalone legislation has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. This is one of the most actively exercised enumerated powers. Parliament has enacted 13 municipality creation/amendment BAAs (BAA 41-48, 53-55, 70-71) plus the comprehensive BAA 49 (Local Governance Code). Additional enacted bills include PB 247 (Bonggo), PB 271 (Pilintangan), PB 272 (Bayantol), and renaming bills (PB 284, 328, 380).

Q. What are MILG's mandate and functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Book V, Title I, Chapter 1, Sec. 3 enumerates 8 functions:

  • (a) "Advise the Chief Minister on the Government of the Day's policies, rules, regulations, and other issuances relative to the general supervision of local government units;"
  • (b) "Establish and prescribe rules, regulations, and other issuances implementing laws on general supervision of local government units and on the promotion of local autonomy, and monitor compliance thereof;"
  • (c) "Provide technical assistance in the preparation of regional legislation affecting local governments units;"
  • (d) "Establish and prescribe plans, policies, programs, and projects to strengthen the administrative, technical, and fiscal capabilities of local government offices and personnel;"
  • (e) "Implement plans, policies, programs, and project to promote public order and safety and disaster preparedness;"
  • (f) "Formulate plans, policies, and programs which will meet regional and local emergencies arising from natural and human-made calamities;"
  • (g) "Enforce its disciplinary authority over elective officials in accordance with the Bangsamoro Local Governance Code;"
  • (h) "Perform other functions as maybe provided by law."3

MILG's organizational structure (Sec. 9) includes: Operation Management Services (with Local Government Development Division, Local Government Supervision Division, Barangay and Community Affairs Division); Interior Affairs Services (Public Safety Division, DRRM Division); and Provincial/City field offices (Sec. 10-12).3

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is MILG the correct lead institution for this power?

A. Yes, but with a qualification. MILG exercises general supervision over LGUs and provides technical assistance on LGU creation matters. However, the actual creation power is legislative — it is exercised by the Bangsamoro Parliament through enactment of specific creation laws. MILG's role is pre-legislative (technical assistance, compliance verification with RA 7160 criteria) and post-legislative (supervision of newly created municipalities). The SGA Development Authority (BAA 13, Sec. 14-28), placed under MILG, manages the transition of the 63 SGA barangays.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power?

A. (1) MILG — lead ministry (BAA 13, BAA 85 Ch. XII); (2) Bangsamoro Parliament — exercises the legislative creation power; (3) SGA Development Authority — manages SGA barangay transition under MILG supervision (BAA 13, Sec. 15); (4) SAMLMG (Sustainable Assistance Mechanism for Local Moral Governance) — BAA 85, Ch. XXXIII, provides transition support funding for LGUs; (5) Regional Chapter of Leagues of Municipalities and Barangays — BAA 13, Sec. 13, coordinates LGU interests.

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. The national government retains three functions: (1) COMELEC conducts plebiscites for all municipality and barangay creation — this is a constitutionally-vested function that cannot be devolved; (2) Congress retains the power to create provinces, cities, and legislative districts — the BOL expressly prohibits BARMM from creating legislative districts, and province creation requires congressional action (as demonstrated by RA 11550); (3) DILG provides technical standards and certification on RA 7160 compliance criteria.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power?

A. BARMM has been highly active in this area: (1) Created 8 new municipalities from SGA barangays (BAA 41-48), converting the 63 barangays that voted for inclusion in the 2019 plebiscite into formally constituted municipalities; (2) Created 3 additional municipalities from existing BARMM territories (BAA 53-55); (3) Successfully conducted plebiscites for the SGA municipalities (commended by Res. 462); (4) Enacted BAA 49 (Local Governance Code), fulfilling the Art. XVI, Sec. 4(a) priority code mandate; (5) Renamed municipalities (Sultan Mastura to Subpangen, Datu Blah T. Sinsuat to West Upi, South Upi to Teduray Municipality); (6) Amended creation laws where needed (BAA 70-71); (7) Signaled intent to coordinate province creation through Congress (Res. 499 — Kutawato Province).

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. The primary constraints are: (1) RA 7160 criteria — the income, population, and land area thresholds may not reflect BARMM's unique circumstances (island barangays, conflict-affected communities with undercounted populations, LGUs with limited income due to delayed IRA transfers); (2) No barangay creation framework — while municipality creation has been active, no BAA or specific provisions address barangay creation or boundary alteration procedures; (3) IRR development — the implementing rules for BAA 49's LGU creation provisions are still being developed; (4) Capacity building — newly created municipalities need institutional support, staffing, and infrastructure during their transition period.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built that BARMM uses?

A. The national system provides: RA 7160's LGU creation criteria — which BARMM must comply with and which serve as the objective standard; COMELEC's plebiscite machinery — which BARMM depends on for all creation/alteration votes; and the DILG's LGU capacity-building programs which complement MILG's work.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. The RA 7160 criteria apply uniformly nationwide — they do not account for (1) island and remote barangays where populations are dispersed across maritime territories; (2) conflict-affected communities with undercounted populations due to displacement; (3) IRA/ICA dependency — newly created municipalities in BARMM depend heavily on block grant shares rather than locally generated income, making the income threshold harder to meet. The national framework also provides no guidance on how the SGA — a unique BARMM institutional arrangement — should transition into permanent LGUs.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. The Shari'ah dimension is limited but relevant. Municipality and barangay creation is primarily an administrative-territorial function. However, the maslahah (public interest/welfare) principle supports creating new LGUs when doing so brings government services closer to communities. The shura (consultation) principle aligns with the plebiscite requirement — the community's voice determines the outcome. No specific Shari'ah rules govern territorial subdivision. The BOL does not reference Shari'ah in Art. VI, Sec. 10.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area?

A. Two general principles apply: (1) Maslahah — creating new municipalities serves the common good by decentralizing governance and improving public service access. (2) Shura — the plebiscite requirement ensures the directly affected population is consulted, consistent with Islamic governance principles of participatory decision-making. No subject-specific Shari'ah rules exist for territorial subdivision.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national law in this area?

A. BARMM's divergence is significantly constrained — this power is tightly bounded by express national criteria requirements. As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), the BOL expressly requires compliance with "the criteria laid down in Republic Act No. 7160" and plebiscite approval. As established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), Art. X, Sec. 10 of the Constitution independently requires the same. BARMM cannot: lower RA 7160 income, population, or land area thresholds; waive the plebiscite; or create legislative districts. BARMM can: determine the process for applying the criteria (through BAA 49 and its IRR), establish additional requirements beyond RA 7160's minimums, create the SGA institutional framework (no national equivalent), and adopt its own organizational framework for newly created LGUs.

Q. If BARMM enacts different procedural standards, what are the implications?

A. BARMM may establish its own procedural framework for processing municipality/barangay creation proposals (how applications are filed, who conducts the compliance review, what documentation is required) — but the substantive criteria (income, population, land area) must match or exceed RA 7160. Any municipality created without meeting RA 7160 criteria could face a legal challenge. Cross-boundary implications are limited since municipalities are entirely within BARMM territory — but municipalities near the SGA boundary may affect non-BARMM LGUs, requiring coordination.

Q. What happens to national law when Parliament enacts a creation BAA?

A. The specific creation BAA governs that municipality's establishment. RA 7160 remains the binding standard for the criteria. BAA 49 provides the governance framework for the new municipality. National laws on LGU operations (budgeting, procurement, civil service) apply suppletorily where BAA 49 is silent.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority?

A. Yes. Three overlaps: (1) Province and city creation — the BOL grants power over municipalities and barangays but is silent on provinces and cities. RA 11550 (Maguindanao division) was enacted by Congress, suggesting province creation is congressional. But BAA 49 may address provincial governance within BARMM. (2) Legislative district implications — when municipality creation triggers new district needs, coordination with Congress is required but no formal protocol exists beyond the Congress-Parliament Forum. (3) RA 7160 certification — who certifies compliance with RA 7160 criteria (MILG or DILG?) is procedurally unclear.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. No major gaps remain in the municipality creation pipeline — this power is actively exercised. The primary gap is in barangay creation procedures and IRR development for BAA 49's LGU creation provisions.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 1 (BARMM Extended Transition) depends on municipality creation to complete the territorial and institutional setup of BARMM — particularly the integration of SGA communities into the formal governance framework. Chapter 5 (Governance and Institutional Development) targets strengthened LGU governance and decentralized service delivery. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028), Pillar 1 (Good Governance), depends on properly constituted LGUs to deliver responsive governance at the community level.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if remaining gaps are unaddressed?

A. Without IRR completion for BAA 49's LGU creation provisions, future municipality and barangay creation proposals lack a clear procedural pathway. Without a barangay creation framework, communities that have outgrown their current barangay structures cannot be reorganized. Without transition support legislation for newly created municipalities, the 11+ new municipalities risk being under-resourced during their formative years — undermining the governance gains that motivated their creation.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. Why is this power limited to municipalities and barangays — not provinces and cities?

A. Art. V, Sec. 2(l) expressly names only "municipalities and barangays." Province and city creation requires congressional action because it directly affects legislative district apportionment (Art. VI, Sec. 5 of the Constitution) and the IRA/ICA formula (national tax distribution). RA 11550 — Congress dividing Maguindanao — confirms this limitation. The BOL's express prohibition on creating legislative districts reinforces that BARMM cannot take actions that would automatically trigger new congressional districts.2

Q. How do the 8 SGA municipalities relate to this power?

A. The 63 barangays in the Special Geographic Area (from Pikit, Pigkawayan, Carmen, Kabacan, Midsayap, and Aleosan) voted for inclusion in BARMM during the 2019 plebiscite. BAA 13, Sec. 14 constituted them as a Special Geographic Area under MILG supervision. The SGA Authority (BAA 13, Sec. 15) managed their transition governance. Parliament then exercised Art. V, Sec. 2(l) to create 8 permanent municipalities (BAA 41-48: Pahamuddin, Kadayangan, Nabalawag, Old Kaabakan, Kapalawan, Malidegao, Tugunan, Ligawasan). This was the largest single exercise of the municipality creation power, converting what was a transitional governance arrangement into permanent local government units. The plebiscites were successfully conducted and commended by Res. 462.4

Q. Can Parliament rename municipalities under this power?

A. The BOL names five actions: "creation, division, merger, abolition or alteration of boundaries." Renaming is not expressly listed. However, Parliament has exercised renaming authority through enacted bills (PB 284 — Sultan Mastura to Subpangen; PB 328 — Datu Blah T. Sinsuat to West Upi; PB 380 — South Upi to Teduray Municipality). This is plausibly grounded in the general legislative power of Parliament (Art. VII, Sec. 2) and the power over "administrative organization" (Art. V, Sec. 2(b)), rather than Art. V, Sec. 2(l) specifically.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap Type Gap What Law / Institution Should Be Created What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No barangay creation framework despite Art. V, Sec. 2(l) covering barangays BAA 49 IRR or separate Barangay Creation and Reorganization Act Barangay creation criteria consistent with RA 7160, Sec. 386; boundary alteration procedures; plebiscite coordination with COMELEC; community consultation protocols Art. V, Sec. 2(l); Art. VI, Sec. 10
Legislative Newly created municipalities lack transition support framework Municipality Transition Support Act Organizational support, staffing templates, initial operating budgets, capacity-building programs, asset transfer protocols for newly created municipalities Art. VI, Sec. 10; Art. V, Sec. 2(l)
Institutional RA 7160 compliance verification process unclear within BARMM MILG Circular on LGU Creation Compliance Formal procedure for MILG to certify income, population, and land area compliance before Parliament acts; coordination with DILG and PSA for data verification Art. VI, Sec. 10 — RA 7160 criteria requirement

(m)
Creation of GOCCs and Pioneer Firms
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(m)

Power 13 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(m) Creation of Government-Owned or Controlled Corporations and Pioneer Firms

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(m) — "Creation of government-owned or controlled corporations and pioneer firms;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(m) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes — Art. XII, Sec. 30 requires "compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10149" (GOCC Governance Act of 2011) and creation "upon coordination with the National Government"
Lead Ministry/Office Bangsamoro Parliament (BTA) — legislative charter authority; Ministry of Finance, and Budget and Management (MFBM) — fiscal oversight
Supporting Agencies Governance Commission for GOCCs (GCG) — national oversight; Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board (IFPB) — determines BARMM participation in existing GOCCs; Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) — alternative incorporation pathway
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy)
Priority Code None named — but a Bangsamoro GOCC Act would fill the regulatory framework gap

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(m) "Creation of government-owned or controlled corporations and pioneer firms;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority to create GOCCs and pioneer firms. This is a broad grant encompassing both legislative-chartered GOCCs and SEC-registered government corporations, as well as pioneer business entities for economic development.
Art. XII, Sec. 30 "Upon coordination with the National Government, the Parliament shall have the power to create government-owned or controlled corporations in compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10149, otherwise known as the 'GOCC Governance Act of 2011,' by a grant of legislative charter, or under Batas Pambansa Blg. 68, otherwise known as 'The Corporation Code of the Philippines:' Provided, That those duly registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission may likewise operate outside the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." The core GOCC creation provision. Imposes three requirements: (1) coordination with the National Government; (2) compliance with RA 10149 (GOCC Governance Act); (3) creation by legislative charter (Parliament enactment) or Corporation Code incorporation (SEC registration). GOCCs registered with SEC may operate beyond BARMM territory.
Art. XII, Sec. 30 (para. 2) "The Parliament may create pioneering firms and other business entities to boost economic development in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." Separate authority for pioneer firms — these are not GOCCs but government-created business entities for economic development purposes. The BOL does not specify the legal form (corporation, partnership, or other entity) or impose the same RA 10149 compliance requirement as for GOCCs.
Art. XII, Sec. 31 "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the authority and control over existing government-owned or controlled corporations operating exclusively in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region after determination by the Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board of their feasibility: Provided, That they shall have the option to transfer their operations outside of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, which shall be effected through the necessary changes in their governing boards." Authority over existing GOCCs operating exclusively within BARMM — conditional on IFPB feasibility determination. Existing GOCCs may opt out by transferring operations outside BARMM.
Art. XII, Sec. 32 "The Bangsamoro Government shall be represented in the board of directors or in the policy-making bodies of government-owned or controlled corporations that operate a substantial portion of their businesses directly or through their subsidiaries in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region or where the Bangsamoro Government has a substantial interest." Board representation in national GOCCs operating substantially in BARMM. The IFPB determines the extent of participation and BARMM's share from GOCC operations, with a guaranteed minimum of one (1) seat on governing boards.
Art. XII, Sec. 33 "Within six (6) months from the establishment of the Bangsamoro Transition Authority, the Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board shall determine the participation of the Bangsamoro Government in the Al-Amanah Islamic Investment Bank of the Philippines and the Southern Philippines Development Authority as provided in Presidential Decree No. 690, as amended." Specific mandate for IFPB to determine BARMM participation in two named entities: Al-Amanah Islamic Investment Bank (AAIIBP) and Southern Philippines Development Authority (SPDA). The 6-month timeline has lapsed without full implementation.
Art. XII, Sec. 6(f) "Dividends from Bangsamoro government-owned or controlled corporations and other corporations, and shares from the dividends of national government-owned or controlled corporations and their subsidiaries in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region as may be determined by the Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board;" GOCC dividends are an enumerated revenue source for the Bangsamoro Government — both from Bangsamoro GOCCs and from national GOCCs operating in BARMM. The IFPB determines BARMM's share from national GOCC dividends.
Art. VII, Sec. 39 (para. 3) "This law shall govern the conduct of civil servants, the qualifications for nonelective positions, adopt the merit and fitness system, and protect civil service eligibles in various government positions, including government-owned or controlled corporations with original charters in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." Bangsamoro civil service law covers GOCCs with original charters — linking GOCC governance to the civil service framework.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
[UNVERIFIED: BAA number not in local archive as of BAA 89] PB 376 Bangsamoro Enterprise Support Corporation (BESC) Creates a GOCC for enterprise support — the first GOCC created by the Bangsamoro Parliament exercising Art. V, Sec. 2(m) Enacted
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Provides the general organizational framework for the Bangsamoro Government, including MFBM's fiscal oversight role. Does not create specific GOCCs. In Force
BAA 85 — General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter III allocates budget to MFBM, which exercises fiscal oversight over Bangsamoro GOCCs In Force
No comprehensive Bangsamoro GOCC governance act has been enacted as of BAA 89. BESC (PB 376) is the only standalone GOCC creation law.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 153 — Calling OCM to nominate BARMM representatives to GOCC governing boards Implements Art. XII, Sec. 32 — BARMM board representation in national GOCCs operating in the region
Res. No. 168 — Requesting report on assets transferred from SPDA to ARMM/BARMM Addresses Art. XII, Sec. 33 — SPDA asset disposition and BARMM participation determination

National Laws

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 10149 (2011) GOCC Governance Act of 2011 BOL Art. XII, Sec. 30 expressly requires compliance. Establishes the Governance Commission for GOCCs (GCG), governance standards, charter requirements, dividend remittance, compensation framework, and performance evaluation for all GOCCs. Directly applicable: Bangsamoro GOCCs must comply. GCG exercises oversight authority. All GOCC creation by Parliament must meet RA 10149 standards.
RA 11232 (2019) Revised Corporation Code of the Philippines Alternative formation pathway — BOL Art. XII, Sec. 30 cites Batas Pambansa Blg. 68 (original Corporation Code), now superseded by RA 11232. GOCCs registered with SEC under this code may operate outside BARMM. Directly applicable: Bangsamoro GOCCs incorporated under the Corporation Code register with SEC and are governed by RA 11232 in addition to RA 10149.
RA 7656 (1993) Requiring GOCCs to Declare Dividends to the National Government Mandates GOCCs to remit at least 50% of net earnings as dividends to the National Government Applicable to national GOCCs operating in BARMM. For Bangsamoro-created GOCCs, dividend policy should be established by BARMM legislation — but RA 10149 compliance may subject them to national dividend requirements.
PD 690 (1975, as amended) Southern Philippines Development Authority (SPDA) Charter BOL Art. XII, Sec. 33 expressly references PD 690. IFPB determines BARMM participation in SPDA. Directly applicable: SPDA's assets and operations in BARMM are subject to IFPB determination per Art. XII, Sec. 33.
RA 6848 (1989) Charter of Al-Amanah Islamic Investment Bank of the Philippines BOL Art. XII, Sec. 33 names AAIIBP specifically. IFPB determines BARMM participation. Directly applicable: AAIIBP is the Philippines' only Islamic bank — its charter links directly to BARMM's Islamic banking power (Art. V, Sec. 2(ii)) and the GOCC creation power.

Shari'ah Sources

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Maslahah (Public Interest / Welfare) Legislation that serves the common good is justified even where no specific textual authority exists, provided it does not contradict Shari'ah. Creating GOCCs for economic development, infrastructure, and service delivery serves maslahah — particularly in sectors where private enterprise is insufficient (e.g., Islamic banking, halal industry development, post-conflict economic zones).
Amanah (Trust / Stewardship) Those entrusted with managing public wealth must act as faithful stewards — with transparency, accountability, and prohibition against self-dealing. GOCC officers manage public funds and assets. The amanah principle reinforces RA 10149's governance standards — board independence, financial transparency, performance accountability, and prohibition of conflicts of interest.
Prohibition of Riba (Interest/Usury) All forms of predetermined return on money lent — regardless of label — constitute riba and are prohibited. Relevant to GOCCs operating in financial services. Al-Amanah Islamic Investment Bank (Art. XII, Sec. 33) is an existing Shari'ah-compliant GOCC. Any Bangsamoro GOCC in the financial sector should provide Shari'ah-compliant products as an option.

Jurisprudence

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro GOCC creation authority under the BOL. The leading jurisprudence on GOCC governance — including GCG authority and the distinction between charter-created and non-charter GOCCs — applies to Bangsamoro GOCCs through the RA 10149 compliance requirement.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
PB 376 Establishing the Bangsamoro Enterprise Support Corporation (BESC) First GOCC created by Parliament — enterprise support for BARMM economic development Enacted
No additional GOCC creation bills have been identified in the pending bill index as of this writing.

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development GOCCs and pioneer firms enable state-driven economic development in sectors where private enterprise is insufficient — economic zones, halal industry, Islamic banking
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 13: Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy GOCC dividends (Art. XII, Sec. 6(f)) are an enumerated revenue source — expanding GOCC operations increases fiscal capacity
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience Enterprise support through BESC and potential future GOCCs strengthens the region's economic base

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over GOCCs and pioneer firms?

A. Article V, Section 2(m) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "creation of government-owned or controlled corporations and pioneer firms" as one of the 55 powers. Art. XII, Sec. 30 elaborates with detailed operational provisions. This power encompasses: (1) creating new GOCCs by legislative charter or Corporation Code incorporation; (2) creating pioneer firms and other business entities for economic development; (3) exercising authority over existing GOCCs operating within BARMM (Art. XII, Sec. 31); and (4) securing representation on boards of national GOCCs operating substantially in the region (Art. XII, Sec. 32).1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Five provisions elaborate:

(1) Art. XII, Sec. 30 defines the creation mechanism: "Upon coordination with the National Government, the Parliament shall have the power to create government-owned or controlled corporations in compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10149 ... by a grant of legislative charter, or under Batas Pambansa Blg. 68." GOCCs registered with SEC "may likewise operate outside the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." A separate paragraph authorizes "pioneering firms and other business entities to boost economic development."2

(2) Art. XII, Sec. 31 grants "authority and control over existing government-owned or controlled corporations operating exclusively in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region" — conditioned on IFPB feasibility determination. Existing GOCCs may transfer operations outside BARMM.3

(3) Art. XII, Sec. 32 guarantees board representation in national GOCCs operating substantially in BARMM, with a minimum of "at least one (1) seat in the governing boards." The IFPB determines the extent of participation and BARMM's share from operations.4

(4) Art. XII, Sec. 33 specifically names AAIIBP and SPDA — requiring IFPB to determine BARMM participation "within six (6) months from the establishment of the Bangsamoro Transition Authority."5

(5) Art. XII, Sec. 6(f) classifies GOCC dividends — both from Bangsamoro GOCCs and from national GOCCs — as an enumerated revenue source.6

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. No. The BOL does not name a specific "GOCC code" or "GOCC governance act" by title. The authority to create GOCCs is self-executing through the legislative charter mechanism. However, Art. XII, Sec. 30 requires compliance with RA 10149, which effectively imports that law's framework. A standalone Bangsamoro GOCC Act would provide region-specific governance, dividend, and oversight provisions.2

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body for this power?

A. The BOL does not create a specific Bangsamoro GOCC governance body. It assigns the IFPB — an intergovernmental body (Art. VI, Sec. 4; Art. XII, Sec. 37-41) — to determine BARMM participation in existing GOCCs. Nationally, the GCG (created by RA 10149) exercises oversight over all GOCCs, including those created by Parliament.7

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Three express conditions:

(1) National Government coordination: Art. XII, Sec. 30 requires GOCC creation "upon coordination with the National Government" — this is a procedural requirement, not a veto power. The nature of "coordination" (notification, consultation, or approval) is not specified.2

(2) RA 10149 compliance: Bangsamoro GOCCs must comply with the GOCC Governance Act — governance standards, board composition requirements, compensation limits, dividend obligations, and GCG oversight.2

(3) IFPB determination: BARMM's authority over existing GOCCs (Art. XII, Sec. 31), board representation (Art. XII, Sec. 32), and participation in AAIIBP/SPDA (Art. XII, Sec. 33) all require IFPB action.345

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to GOCC creation?

A. Three categories:

(1) Autonomous Region Framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): The universal constitutional boundary on all 55 powers.8

(2) GOCC Creation (Art. XII, Sec. 16): The Constitution requires that GOCCs be created by "a special law" (legislative charter). The 1987 Constitution prohibits the creation of private corporations by special law — but permits GOCCs to be chartered by legislation. The BOL's provision for legislative charter creation is consistent with this constitutional requirement.9

(3) Accountability of Public Officers (Art. XI): GOCC officers are public officers subject to constitutional accountability standards — including SALN filing, anti-graft provisions, and Ombudsman jurisdiction. This applies to Bangsamoro GOCC officers.10

Q. What constitutional mandates exist for this area?

A. The Constitution does not mandate GOCC creation — it permits it. Art. XII, Sec. 16 permits the creation of GOCCs by special law. The constitutional fiscal provisions (Art. XII, Sec. 29 — no public money spent except by appropriation) apply to all GOCCs, requiring that their budgets go through the appropriations process.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for GOCCs?

A. RA 10149 (GOCC Governance Act) establishes a comprehensive framework: (1) GCG as the central oversight body with authority to review GOCC charters, appoint directors, set compensation, and evaluate performance; (2) governance standards — board composition requirements (independent directors, fit-and-proper test), compensation and position classification, performance evaluation, dividend remittance; (3) charter review — GCG reviews all existing and proposed GOCC charters; (4) reporting and transparency requirements. RA 7656 requires GOCCs to declare at least 50% of net earnings as dividends to the National Government. RA 11232 (Revised Corporation Code) provides the alternative SEC-registration pathway.111213

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. (1) RA 10149 — applies directly by express BOL mandate (Art. XII, Sec. 30). GCG oversight extends to Bangsamoro-created GOCCs. (2) RA 11232 — applies directly as the alternative incorporation pathway. (3) RA 7656 — applies to national GOCCs operating in BARMM; applicability to Bangsamoro-created GOCCs depends on whether they fall under the national dividend requirement framework. (4) PD 690 (SPDA) and RA 6848 (AAIIBP) — apply directly per Art. XII, Sec. 33.111213

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. (1) GCG — exercises oversight over all GOCCs including those created by Parliament, per RA 10149. (2) SEC — registers GOCCs incorporated under the Corporation Code. (3) IFPB — determines BARMM participation in existing GOCCs (Arts. XII, Sec. 31-33). (4) Commission on Audit (COA) — audits all GOCCs per constitutional mandate (Art. XII, Sec. 2 of the BOL reiterating Art. IX-D of the Constitution).

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which institution is primarily responsible?

A. The Bangsamoro Parliament is the primary actor — exercising the legislative charter authority. MFBM provides fiscal oversight. BAA 13 does not assign a specific ministry for GOCC creation management — this is a gap. BAA 85, Chapter III allocates budget to MFBM for fiscal management functions.14

Q. What standalone legislation has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. PB 376 (Bangsamoro Enterprise Support Corporation — BESC) is the first and only GOCC created by Parliament as of this writing. No comprehensive GOCC governance framework has been enacted.

Q. What are MFBM's relevant functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13 assigns MFBM responsibility for fiscal management, including budget formulation, revenue administration, and financial oversight. While MFBM does not have an express GOCC oversight function under BAA 13, its fiscal management role positions it as the natural counterpart to GCG for Bangsamoro GOCCs — managing dividend remittance, capital structure review, and fiscal performance monitoring.

Q. What can the Bangsamoro Government NOT do without a standalone GOCC Act?

A. Without a dedicated Bangsamoro GOCC Act, the government cannot: (1) establish a Bangsamoro GOCC governance body equivalent to GCG that exercises region-specific oversight; (2) define dividend policy for Bangsamoro-created GOCCs (whether dividends flow to the Bangsamoro treasury, the national government, or both); (3) establish GOCC performance standards tailored to BARMM's development priorities; (4) create a clear coordination protocol with the National Government for the Art. XII, Sec. 30 "coordination" requirement; (5) define the relationship between Bangsamoro GOCCs and GCG — whether GCG exercises full oversight or whether BARMM creates a complementary oversight mechanism.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is the institutional assignment correct for this power?

A. Partially. The Parliament correctly exercises the legislative creation function. However, there is no dedicated executive-level GOCC oversight body within BARMM. Nationally, GCG fills this role. MFBM is the logical home for a Bangsamoro GOCC oversight function, but BAA 13 does not expressly assign it. A Bangsamoro GOCC Act should clarify the institutional architecture.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power?

A. (1) Bangsamoro Parliament — exercises legislative charter authority; (2) MFBM — fiscal oversight (BAA 13, BAA 85 Ch. III); (3) IFPB — intergovernmental body determining BARMM participation in existing GOCCs (Art. XII, Sec. 37-41); (4) BESC — the first Bangsamoro GOCC (PB 376). No dedicated GOCC oversight office exists within the Bangsamoro executive branch.

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. The national government retains significant authority: (1) GCG — reviews GOCC charters (including those enacted by Parliament), evaluates governance compliance, sets compensation standards, and monitors performance under RA 10149; (2) SEC — registers GOCCs incorporated under the Corporation Code; (3) COA — audits all GOCCs; (4) National GOCCs operate within BARMM — NPC/NGCP (power), NFA (food), PhilHealth, SSS, GSIS, and other national GOCCs deliver services within the region.

Q. What is the status of IFPB determinations under Art. XII, Sec. 33?

A. The IFPB was mandated to determine BARMM participation in AAIIBP and SPDA "within six (6) months from the establishment of the Bangsamoro Transition Authority." The BTA was established in March 2019 — the 6-month deadline has long passed. Res. No. 153 called on OCM to nominate BARMM representatives to GOCC boards, and Res. No. 168 requested a report on SPDA assets. [UNVERIFIED: The exact status of IFPB determinations for AAIIBP and SPDA could not be confirmed from available local sources.]

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has BARMM accomplished so far?

A. (1) Enacted PB 376 (BESC) — the first Bangsamoro GOCC, demonstrating the legislative charter mechanism; (2) Adopted Res. 153 — calling for BARMM board representation in national GOCCs; (3) Adopted Res. 168 — requesting SPDA asset accounting; (4) Established the institutional framework (Parliament, MFBM) for future GOCC creation. However, no comprehensive GOCC governance framework has been enacted.

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. The primary constraint is the absence of a Bangsamoro GOCC Act establishing the governance framework, coordination protocols with the National Government and GCG, dividend policy, and oversight mechanisms. The secondary constraint is the incomplete IFPB determinations — BARMM's participation in AAIIBP and SPDA (Art. XII, Sec. 33) and representation in national GOCC boards (Art. XII, Sec. 32) depend on IFPB action that has not been fully completed.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built that BARMM uses?

A. The national system provides: RA 10149's comprehensive GOCC framework — governance standards, board requirements, compensation limits, dividend obligations, and GCG oversight; SEC's incorporation system for non-charter GOCCs; COA's audit framework for GOCC financial accountability; and 30+ years of GOCC operational experience with established regulatory patterns.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. (1) No Shari'ah-compliant GOCC governance track — RA 10149 does not address Islamic governance principles for GOCCs in the financial or halal industry sectors; (2) No post-conflict economic development GOCC model — the national framework does not provide for GOCCs designed specifically for conflict-affected regions; (3) IFPB implementation delays — the intergovernmental mechanism for GOCC participation determination has not delivered timely results; (4) GCG oversight is Manila-centric — its capacity to review and monitor Bangsamoro GOCCs is limited by geographic distance and institutional priorities.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. The Shari'ah dimension is sector-specific. GOCCs in the financial sector (particularly Islamic banking — Art. V, Sec. 2(ii)) must comply with Shari'ah principles prohibiting riba (interest), gharar (excessive uncertainty), and maysir (gambling). AAIIBP (Art. XII, Sec. 33) is already a Shari'ah-compliant GOCC. The general amanah (trust/stewardship) principle applies to all GOCCs — officers managing public funds bear fiduciary obligations consistent with both civil law and Islamic law. For GOCCs in non-financial sectors, the Shari'ah dimension is limited to the general principles of maslahah (public welfare) and 'adl (justice).

Q. How should Shari'ah compliance be integrated into Bangsamoro GOCC legislation?

A. A principled approach is recommended rather than mandatory Shari'ah compliance for all GOCCs. GOCCs operating in financial services should be required to offer Shari'ah-compliant products alongside conventional ones. GOCCs in other sectors should operate under general governance standards informed by amanah principles. Any Bangsamoro GOCC Act should include provisions for a Shari'ah Advisory Board for financial-sector GOCCs, consistent with Art. XIII, Sec. 34 (Shari'ah Supervisory Board for Islamic banks).

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can BARMM diverge from national GOCC law?

A. BARMM's divergence is moderately constrained. As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), Art. XII, Sec. 30 expressly requires "compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10149" — this is the BOL's clearest national law consistency requirement for this power. BARMM cannot: create GOCCs that fail RA 10149 governance standards, exempt GOCCs from GCG oversight, or bypass the coordination requirement with the National Government. BARMM can: define additional governance requirements beyond RA 10149's minimums (e.g., Shari'ah Advisory Boards for financial-sector GOCCs), establish a Bangsamoro GOCC oversight body that works alongside GCG, define the dividend policy for Bangsamoro GOCCs, and create pioneer firms with fewer constraints than GOCCs (the BOL does not subject pioneer firms to RA 10149).

Q. What are the implications for GOCCs operating across boundaries?

A. GOCCs registered with SEC "may likewise operate outside the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region" (Art. XII, Sec. 30). This means SEC-registered Bangsamoro GOCCs are subject to national corporate law (RA 11232) and RA 10149 everywhere they operate. Legislative-chartered GOCCs are governed by their charter, which operates within BARMM territory. Cross-boundary GOCCs face dual oversight — GCG nationally and any Bangsamoro GOCC oversight body within BARMM.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions?

A. Yes. Three overlaps: (1) GCG vs. Bangsamoro oversight — does GCG exercise full RA 10149 authority over Parliament-created GOCCs, or does BARMM create a complementary oversight body? (2) Dividend policy — do Bangsamoro GOCC dividends flow to the Bangsamoro treasury (Art. XII, Sec. 6(f)) or to the National Government (RA 7656), or both? (3) "Coordination" meaning — Art. XII, Sec. 30's "upon coordination with the National Government" is undefined — is this notification, consultation, or approval?

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. PB 376 (BESC) has been enacted — addressing the first instance of GOCC creation. No comprehensive GOCC governance bill has been identified in the pending bill index. The primary gap — a Bangsamoro GOCC Act — remains unaddressed.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP goals and Matatag pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 7 (Economic Development) depends on GOCCs and pioneer firms to drive state-led economic development in sectors where private enterprise is insufficient or nonexistent — economic zones, halal industry infrastructure, Islamic banking expansion, enterprise support. Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy) identifies GOCC dividends as a revenue source under Art. XII, Sec. 6(f). The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028), Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience), relies on enterprise support (BESC) and potentially future GOCCs in strategic sectors.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if gaps remain?

A. Without a Bangsamoro GOCC Act: the governance framework for future GOCCs relies entirely on RA 10149 (which is not tailored to BARMM); dividend policy remains unclear — potentially losing revenue for the Bangsamoro treasury; the AAIIBP participation (critical for Islamic banking) and SPDA asset disposition remain unresolved; and BARMM cannot create a pipeline of strategic GOCCs for economic development (economic zones, halal certification, development banking) without a clear governance and oversight framework.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What is the difference between a GOCC and a pioneer firm under the BOL?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 30 treats them differently. GOCCs must be created "in compliance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10149" and "upon coordination with the National Government" — either by legislative charter or SEC incorporation. Pioneer firms are authorized by a separate paragraph: "The Parliament may create pioneering firms and other business entities to boost economic development." The BOL does not subject pioneer firms to RA 10149 compliance or National Government coordination — they are a more flexible instrument for economic development. A pioneer firm could be a corporation, partnership, joint venture, or other business entity. The distinction matters because pioneer firms have fewer regulatory constraints than GOCCs.

Q. What is the IFPB's role and why does it matter?

A. The Intergovernmental Fiscal Policy Board (Art. VI, Sec. 4; Art. XII, Sec. 37-41) is the gatekeeper for three GOCC-related decisions: (1) feasibility determination for BARMM to assume authority over existing GOCCs operating within BARMM (Art. XII, Sec. 31); (2) extent of participation and revenue share from national GOCCs (Art. XII, Sec. 32); (3) AAIIBP and SPDA participation (Art. XII, Sec. 33). Without IFPB action, BARMM cannot exercise authority over existing GOCCs, secure board representation, or participate in AAIIBP/SPDA. The 6-month deadline for the AAIIBP/SPDA determination has long passed.7

Q. Can the Bangsamoro Parliament create GOCCs for sectors outside BARMM?

A. Only if the GOCC is registered with SEC under the Corporation Code. Art. XII, Sec. 30 provides: "those duly registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission may likewise operate outside the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." A legislative-chartered GOCC (created by Parliament act without SEC registration) operates within BARMM territory. If the Parliament wants a GOCC to operate nationally or internationally, it must use the SEC incorporation pathway.2

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap Type Gap What Law / Institution Should Be Created What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No comprehensive GOCC governance framework despite Art. XII, Sec. 30-33 Bangsamoro GOCC Act Creation procedures; governance standards beyond RA 10149 minimums; Shari'ah Advisory Board for financial-sector GOCCs; dividend policy (Bangsamoro vs. national treasury); coordination protocol with GCG and National Government; GOCC performance evaluation; pioneer firm definition and creation process; reporting and transparency requirements Art. XII, Sec. 30 — GOCC creation power; Art. XII, Sec. 6(f) — GOCC dividends as revenue source
Institutional IFPB has not completed Art. XII, Sec. 33 determinations for AAIIBP and SPDA IFPB Resolution on AAIIBP and SPDA participation BARMM participation level, board representation, revenue sharing, asset transfer (SPDA), and governance arrangements Art. XII, Sec. 33 — mandatory IFPB determination
Legislative No GOCC dividend framework for Bangsamoro-created GOCCs Bangsamoro GOCC Dividend Policy (integrated into GOCC Act or Revenue Code) Dividend remittance rates; distribution between Bangsamoro treasury and operational reserves; relationship with RA 7656 national dividend requirements; performance-based dividend waivers for development-stage GOCCs Art. XII, Sec. 6(f) — GOCC dividends as revenue source
