(o)
Cultural Exchange and Economic Cooperation
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(o)

Power 15 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(o) Cultural Exchange, and Economic and Technical Cooperation

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(o) — "Cultural exchange, and economic and technical cooperation;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(o) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes — Art. XII, Sec. 28: "upon review and approval of the National Government" — all international linkages require national government clearance
Lead Ministry/Office Office of the Chief Minister (OCM) — international linkages and external relations function; Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila serves as liaison to international offices/embassies (BAA 13, Book IV, Sec. (8))
Supporting Agencies Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT); Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority (BPDA); Bangsamoro Barter Trade Council (BBTC — for BIMP-EAGA/ASEAN economic cooperation); Department of Foreign Affairs (national)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 13 (Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy); Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity and Heritage)
Priority Code — (no named legislative mandate for this power)

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(o) "Cultural exchange, and economic and technical cooperation;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority to engage in international cultural exchange, economic cooperation, and technical cooperation — subject to the qualifications in Art. XII, Sec. 28.
Art. XII, Sec. 28 "The Bangsamoro Government may establish linkages for cultural exchange, economic and technical cooperation with countries having diplomatic relations with the Philippines upon review and approval of the National Government. The Bangsamoro Government may recommend to the National Government the assignment of qualified persons in Philippine embassies and consulates, and their participation in international delegations." Core implementing provision. Defines three elements: (1) the scope — linkages for cultural exchange, economic and technical cooperation; (2) the condition — only with countries having diplomatic relations with the Philippines, and only upon review and approval of the National Government; (3) the personnel dimension — the Bangsamoro Government may recommend persons for embassy/consulate assignments and international delegations.
Art. XII, Sec. 29 "The Bangsamoro Government shall be entitled to benefits resulting from conventions to which the National Government is a party. Such benefits shall be equitable, considering the available human and material resources and comparative advantage of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, as well as its socioeconomic conditions and needs." Extends benefits of international conventions to BARMM. The Bangsamoro Government does not negotiate or accede to these conventions — it receives equitable benefits from them through the National Government.
Art. XIII, Sec. 24 (last para.) "The Bangsamoro Government shall promote trade and industry in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region by providing avenues through which other countries may learn about its unique industries, economic opportunities, and culture through participation in trade missions, trade fairs, and other promotional activities. It may also organize trade missions to other countries observing the necessary coordination with the relevant government agencies." Trade promotion dimension — authorizes the Bangsamoro Government to participate in and organize trade missions and fairs as economic cooperation activities. Requires coordination with relevant government agencies.
Art. XIII, Sec. 26 "The Bangsamoro Government shall regulate traditional barter trade with the Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East ASEAN Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA) Member States and countertrade with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) Member States." A specific form of economic cooperation — barter trade with BIMP-EAGA and countertrade with ASEAN. This is one of the broadest international economic functions granted to the Bangsamoro Government, with no express "review and approval" qualifier (though it operates within the diplomatic relations framework).
Art. XII, Sec. 26 "Departments, bureaus, offices, and state universities and colleges of the Bangsamoro Government may accept donations, contributions, grants, bequests, or gifts, in cash or in kind, from domestic or foreign sources for purposes relevant to their functions."

"In case of such grants or donations from governments of foreign countries, their agencies and instrumentalities or multilateral institutions or organizations, acceptance thereof shall be subject to the prior clearance and approval by the President or his authorized representative."		 Foreign grants and donations from foreign governments and multilateral institutions require Presidential clearance. Domestic donations do not.
Art. XIII, Sec. 6 "To ensure that the Bangsamoro development plans are reflected in the national development plans, a representative of the Bangsamoro Government shall sit as member of a national development plan steering committee..." Participation in national planning as a form of intergovernmental cooperation that enables the Bangsamoro Government to position its development priorities within national economic strategies, including those with international dimensions.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Establishes (a) Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila (Book IV, Sec. (8)) — liaison to international offices/embassies and coordinating office for foreign scholarship programs; (b) Bangsamoro Barter Trade Council (Book VI, Title XIV, Ch. 8, Sec. 37-39) — regulates barter trade with BIMP-EAGA and countertrade with ASEAN In Force
BAA 85 — General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter II allocates operational budget to OCM including the Liaison Office; Chapter V allocates budget to MTIT for trade promotion In Force
No standalone international cooperation framework act has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. 124 — Condemning violence against Palestinians International solidarity — expression of Bangsamoro foreign policy position
Res. 233 — Expressing concern on civilian casualties in Ukraine International solidarity — humanitarian dimension of international engagement
Res. 396 — Commending Sabaha Salik for Qur'an recitation win in Malaysia Cultural exchange outcome — Bangsamoro participation in international cultural events
Res. 407 — Condemning violence against Palestinians, calling for ceasefire in Gaza International solidarity and humanitarian advocacy
Res. 419 — Reiterating call for ceasefire in Gaza International solidarity — continued engagement with global humanitarian issues
No enacted resolution directly establishes an international cooperation framework or protocol.

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
1987 Constitution, Art. II, Sec. 2 Declaration of state policy on foreign relations "The Philippines renounces war as an instrument of national policy, adopts the generally accepted principles of international law as part of the law of the land and adheres to the policy of peace, equality, justice, freedom, cooperation, and amity with all nations." Constitutional constraint — all Bangsamoro international linkages operate within the national foreign policy framework.
1987 Constitution, Art. VII, Sec. 21 Treaty-making power "No treaty or international agreement shall be valid and effective unless concurred in by at least two-thirds of all the Members of the Senate." Constitutional constraint — the Bangsamoro Government cannot enter into treaties or binding international agreements. Only the President negotiates, and the Senate concurs.
RA 7157 (1991) Philippine Foreign Service Act of 1991 Governs Philippine embassies and consulates — the institutional framework within which BOL Art. XII, Sec. 28's embassy/consulate assignment provision operates Directly applicable — Bangsamoro personnel recommended for embassy/consulate assignments must comply with Foreign Service Act requirements.
RA 8182 (1996) Official Development Assistance (ODA) Act Governs acceptance and utilization of ODA — Art. XII, Sec. 25 requires ODA to follow this law Directly applicable — BARMM acceptance of ODA through international cooperation agreements must comply with RA 8182.
EO 459, s. 1997 Guidelines for Negotiation and Approval of International Agreements Establishes the national approval process for international agreements — the ***"review and approval of the National Government"*** per Art. XII, Sec. 28 follows this framework Directly applicable — Bangsamoro cooperation agreements with foreign countries must comply with EO 459's approval protocols.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Maslahah (Public Interest / Welfare) Legislation serving the common good is justified under Islamic jurisprudence, even where no specific textual authority exists. International cooperation that brings economic development, technology transfer, and cultural enrichment to the Bangsamoro people serves maslahah. Cultural exchange with Muslim-majority countries strengthens Islamic identity and solidarity [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].
Ta'aruf (Mutual Acquaintance / Understanding) Qur'an 49:13: "O mankind, indeed We have created you from male and female and made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another." The Qur'anic principle of ta'aruf — mutual acquaintance across cultures — provides an Islamic basis for cultural exchange programs. Exchange is not merely economic but spiritual and civilizational [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].
Ummah (Global Muslim Community) The concept of the ummah — the global community of Muslims united by faith — provides a framework for understanding Bangsamoro engagement with Muslim-majority countries. Bangsamoro cultural exchange and technical cooperation with OIC member states reflects the ummah dimension. The BOL's diplomatic relations requirement channels this engagement through Philippine foreign policy [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area — defining boundaries of autonomy, resolving jurisdiction questions, or interpreting related provisions.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address a sub-national government's authority to engage in international cooperation. The general principle — that foreign affairs is an inherently national power — is well-established in Philippine constitutional law.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs. Shows legislative intent and pending proposals.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
No pending Parliament Bill specifically addresses the establishment of an international cooperation framework. Related bills in the trade and industry area may touch on international promotional activities but none directly implement Art. XII, Sec. 28.

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development International trade linkages, BIMP-EAGA engagement, investment attraction from OIC/ASEAN countries
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 13: Plan Financing and Fiscal Strategy International economic cooperation as channel for development financing and technical assistance — ODA, grants, technical cooperation
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 6: Cultural Diversity and Heritage Cultural exchange programs for heritage preservation and cross-cultural understanding with Muslim-majority nations
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Economic Resilience International economic cooperation strengthens economic resilience through trade, investment, and technology transfer

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over cultural exchange and economic/technical cooperation?

A. Article V, Section 2(o) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "cultural exchange, and economic and technical cooperation" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This power covers three dimensions of international engagement: cultural exchange (cross-cultural programs and activities), economic cooperation (trade, investment, and economic development partnerships), and technical cooperation (capacity building, skills transfer, and institutional development). The power is qualified by Art. XII, Sec. 28, which requires "review and approval of the National Government" for all international linkages.12

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Six provisions elaborate on this power:

(1) Art. XII, Sec. 28 — the core implementing provision (quoted in full in the BOL Provisions table). It establishes three elements: scope (cultural exchange, economic/technical cooperation), condition (countries with diplomatic relations + national government approval), and personnel (embassy/consulate assignments and international delegations).2

(2) Art. XII, Sec. 29 — benefits from international conventions: the Bangsamoro Government is entitled to "equitable" benefits from conventions to which the National Government is a party.3

(3) Art. XIII, Sec. 24 (last para.) — trade missions and fairs: the Bangsamoro Government may participate in and organize trade missions and promotional activities with "necessary coordination with the relevant government agencies."4

(4) Art. XIII, Sec. 26 — barter trade and countertrade: regulates traditional barter trade with "BIMP-EAGA Member States" and countertrade with "ASEAN Member States."5

(5) Art. XII, Sec. 26 — grants and donations: acceptance from foreign governments and multilateral institutions requires "prior clearance and approval by the President or his authorized representative." Domestic donations do not require Presidential clearance.6

(6) Art. XIII, Sec. 6 — national development plan participation: ensures Bangsamoro representation in national planning steering committees.7

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. No. The BOL does not name a specific legislation for international cooperation. The power is exercised through the executive function of the Chief Minister and the OCM, with national government approval. However, the Parliament could enact legislation to establish institutional mechanisms, protocols, and priorities for international engagement.

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power?

A. The BOL does not create a dedicated international cooperation body. Art. XII, Sec. 28 vests the function in the Bangsamoro Government broadly. Under BAA 13, the function is exercised through: (a) the Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila (liaison to international offices/embassies — BAA 13, Book IV, Sec. (8)); and (b) the Bangsamoro Barter Trade Council (BBTC — for BIMP-EAGA/ASEAN economic cooperation — BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Ch. 8).

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Three significant limitations:

(1) National government review and approval: Art. XII, Sec. 28 — "upon review and approval of the National Government." This is the single most important qualifier — the Bangsamoro Government cannot independently establish international linkages.2

(2) Diplomatic relations requirement: Art. XII, Sec. 28 — only with "countries having diplomatic relations with the Philippines." The Bangsamoro Government cannot engage with states not recognized by the Philippines.2

(3) Presidential clearance for grants: Art. XII, Sec. 26 — foreign grants require "prior clearance and approval by the President."6

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to this subject area?

A. Four categories of constitutional provisions apply:

(1) Foreign affairs as national power: The 1987 Constitution vests the treaty-making power exclusively in the President (Art. VII, Sec. 21) with Senate concurrence. The conduct of foreign affairs is an inherently national function. Art. II, Sec. 2 declares the Philippines "adheres to the policy of peace, equality, justice, freedom, cooperation, and amity with all nations."89

(2) Autonomous region framework (Art. X, Sec. 15): All autonomous region powers operate "within the framework of this Constitution." International cooperation by a sub-national government must not infringe on national sovereignty or foreign affairs prerogatives.10

(3) Autonomous region legislative powers (Art. X, Sec. 20(6)): The organic act of autonomous regions may provide for "economic, social, and tourism development." This provides constitutional basis for economic cooperation activities within the autonomous region framework.11

(4) Due process (Art. III, Sec. 1): Government action affecting international cooperation agreements must satisfy procedural requirements.12

Q. What is the constitutional boundary on this power?

A. The Bangsamoro Government cannot: (a) enter into treaties or binding international agreements — this is a Presidential/Senate prerogative; (b) establish diplomatic relations or recognize foreign states; (c) conduct foreign policy independent of the National Government; (d) engage with countries that do not have diplomatic relations with the Philippines. The Bangsamoro Government can: (a) establish cooperation linkages (MOUs, technical agreements, cultural exchanges) with national government approval; (b) participate in trade missions and fairs; (c) recommend personnel for embassy assignments; (d) receive equitable benefits from international conventions.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for this subject area?

A. The national framework is structured around: RA 7157 (Philippine Foreign Service Act) governing embassies and consulates; EO 459, s. 1997 establishing the approval process for international agreements; RA 8182 (ODA Act) governing development assistance; and the Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) as the lead national agency for foreign affairs coordination. No national law specifically addresses sub-national government international cooperation — the BOL is the primary framework.131415

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. (1) RA 7157 — directly applicable for embassy/consulate assignments recommended under Art. XII, Sec. 28.13 (2) EO 459 — directly applicable for the approval process of international cooperation agreements.14 (3) RA 8182 — directly applicable for ODA received through international cooperation.15 (4) Constitution, Art. VII, Sec. 21 — directly applicable as the constitutional limit on treaty-making.

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. The Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) exercises primary authority over international engagement. The National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) coordinates ODA programming. The Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) coordinates trade missions and fairs. None of these functions have been devolved to the Bangsamoro Government — the BOL establishes a coordination-based model rather than devolution.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power?

A. The Office of the Chief Minister (OCM) is the primary institution. BAA 13 assigns two specific functions: (a) the Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila (Book IV, Sec. (8)) serves as "liaison office of the Chief Minister to international offices/embassies" and "coordinating office of foreign scholarship programs"; (b) the Chief Minister heads the Bangsamoro Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council which coordinates international humanitarian assistance. BAA 85, Chapter II allocates the OCM's operational budget.1617

Q. What standalone legislation (BAAs) has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. No standalone BAA has been enacted for international cooperation. The power is exercised through OCM's executive authority and BAA 13 institutional provisions.

Q. What are the OCM's mandate and functions under BAA 13 relevant to international cooperation?

A. BAA 13 assigns two relevant units to the OCM:

Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila (Book IV, Sec. (8)): - (a) "Serve as liaison office of the Chief Minister to international offices/embassies, national offices/departments, agencies, and bureaus;" - (b) "Provide administrative and technical support to the Chief Minister and BARMM officials/employees while on official business in Metro Manila;" - (c) "Coordinate with National Government Agencies in Metro Manila on matters related to the BARMM;" - (d) "Act as coordinating office of foreign scholarship programs;" - (e) "Perform other functions as directed by the Chief Minister or by his/her authorized representative."16

Bangsamoro Barter Trade Council (BBTC) (Book VI, Title XIV, Ch. 8, Sec. 37-39): - Mandate: "to accredit, regulate, supervise, coordinate and harmonize all policies, operations, programs and activities of barter trade in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region" (Sec. 37) - Key functions: (a) "Create an environment conducive to barter and counter trade development and growth in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region, BIMP-EAGA and ASEAN"; (b) issue rules and regulations on barter trader accreditation; (c) issue guidelines governing barter trade; (d) exercise regulatory powers; (e) "Coordinate with other appropriate government agencies/institutions...such as Coast Guard, Bureau of Customs, Bureau of Immigrations and Deportation, Department of Foreign Affairs" (Sec. 38)18

Q. What can the OCM NOT do without a standalone BAA?

A. Without legislation, the OCM cannot: (a) establish a permanent international cooperation office with defined authority, staffing, and protocols; (b) create a standardized approval process for international cooperation proposals within the Bangsamoro Government before submission to the National Government; (c) define eligible cooperation activities and partner country priorities; (d) establish a Bangsamoro International Cooperation Fund for co-financing partnerships; (e) codify the embassy/consulate recommendation process for qualified Bangsamoro persons.

Q. Have any parliamentary resolutions signaled intent to legislate in this area?

A. No enacted resolution directly calls for international cooperation legislation. The Parliament has expressed positions on international issues through solidarity resolutions (Res. 124, 233, 407, 419) and cultural recognition resolutions (Res. 396), indicating engagement with international affairs through the resolution mechanism rather than legislation.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is the assigned lead MOA the correct one for this power?

A. Yes. The OCM is the appropriate lead because international cooperation is an executive function exercised by the head of government. The Chief Minister is the interlocutor with the National Government for approval of international linkages (Art. XII, Sec. 28) and has the political authority to engage with foreign counterparts. The Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila provides the operational link to embassies and national government agencies.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power?

A. - Lead office: OCM — Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila (BAA 13, Book IV, Sec. (8)) - Specialized body: Bangsamoro Barter Trade Council (BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Ch. 8) — BIMP-EAGA/ASEAN economic cooperation - Supporting ministry: MTIT — trade promotion and international trade fairs (BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV, Ch. 1) - Supporting agency: BPDA — coordinates development plans that inform international cooperation priorities - Youth dimension: Bangsamoro Youth Commission — BAA 13 assigns "establish and maintain linkages with national and international youth organizations" (Book XVI, Ch. 1)

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. The Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) exercises primary authority over all international engagements involving BARMM. DFA reviews and approves Bangsamoro cooperation proposals per Art. XII, Sec. 28. NEDA coordinates ODA programming for BARMM. DTI facilitates trade mission participation. In practice, the Chief Minister's Office engages directly with foreign embassies and international organizations in Metro Manila through the Liaison Office, with DFA providing the formal approval channel.

Q. What is the status of devolution for this power?

A. This power is not devolvable in the traditional sense. International relations remain a national prerogative under the Constitution. The BOL creates a coordination model — the Bangsamoro Government initiates and recommends, the National Government reviews and approves. The function that can mature is the Bangsamoro Government's capacity to propose, negotiate (with national supervision), and implement international cooperation activities.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power?

A. The Bangsamoro Government has: (a) established the Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila as operational link to embassies and national agencies; (b) participated in BIMP-EAGA activities through the BBTC; (c) engaged with OIC member states and international organizations (UNDP, World Bank, JICA, USAID, etc.) for development cooperation; (d) participated in international conferences and delegations; (e) received foreign grants and technical assistance through the National Government channel. The international engagement has been primarily executive-driven rather than legislatively structured.

Q. Where is the BARMM stronger than the national government in this area?

A. BARMM has a unique advantage in Muslim-majority country engagement — the Bangsamoro identity creates natural affinities with OIC member states (particularly Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei, Turkey, and Gulf states) that the National Government's secular diplomatic apparatus does not fully leverage. Bangsamoro cultural exchange with Muslim-majority countries strengthens both development cooperation and cultural identity preservation.

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. The primary constraint is the absence of institutional infrastructure — no dedicated international cooperation office with clear authority, staffing, and protocols. The Bangsamoro Liaison Office in Metro Manila has a broad mandate (national agencies, embassies, scholarship coordination) without the specialized capacity for systematic international cooperation management. The secondary constraint is the national government approval process, which can be slow without a standardized protocol.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built for this area that BARMM does not yet have?

A. The national government has: (a) a professional Foreign Service with 117 embassies and consulates worldwide; (b) EO 459 establishing a standardized international agreement approval process; (c) NEDA's ODA portfolio managing billions of pesos in development assistance; (d) DTI's trade promotion network including trade attaches in key markets. BARMM has none of these at the regional level.

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM?

A. The national system: (a) does not prioritize Bangsamoro-specific international cooperation — BARMM's unique relationship with Muslim-majority countries is not systematically leveraged; (b) the approval process under Art. XII, Sec. 28 has no standardized protocol for BARMM proposals; (c) no Bangsamoro foreign service personnel have been formally recommended for embassy/consulate assignments under Art. XII, Sec. 28.

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. The Shari'ah dimension is principled rather than regulatory. International cooperation is governed by diplomatic and administrative law, not Shari'ah. However, three Islamic principles inform the Bangsamoro Government's approach:

(1) Ta'aruf (mutual acquaintance) — the Qur'anic principle in Surah 49:13 supports cultural exchange as a means of mutual understanding across peoples and cultures [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

(2) Maslahah (public interest) — international cooperation that brings development to the Bangsamoro people serves the public interest [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

(3) Ummah — the global Muslim community provides a natural network for cultural exchange and technical cooperation with OIC member states, though the BOL channels this engagement through Philippine diplomatic relations [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

Q. What does the BOL say about Shari'ah in relation to this power?

A. The BOL is silent on Shari'ah in relation to international cooperation. No provision of Art. XII, Sec. 28-29 or Art. XIII, Sec. 24-26 references Islamic law, Shari'ah institutions, or OIC engagement.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national law and policy in this area?

A. BARMM's ability to diverge is minimal because international cooperation is constitutionally a national function. As established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), Art. XII, Sec. 28 requires national government approval. As established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), the treaty-making power is exclusively Presidential/Senatorial. The Bangsamoro Government can diverge only in: (a) establishing internal institutional mechanisms for managing international cooperation; (b) setting regional priorities for which countries and cooperation areas to pursue; (c) developing selection criteria for embassy/consulate personnel recommendations. In all cases, the external-facing dimension requires national government approval.

Q. What happens to national law when the Parliament enacts a BAA on this subject?

A. A Bangsamoro International Cooperation Act would govern the internal process — how the Bangsamoro Government identifies, evaluates, and proposes international cooperation activities. National law (EO 459, RA 7157, RA 8182) would continue to govern the approval and implementation processes involving the National Government.

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority?

A. The principal ambiguity is in the approval process: Art. XII, Sec. 28 says "upon review and approval of the National Government" but does not specify which national government body approves, what the review criteria are, or the timeline for approval. Is it DFA? The Office of the President? NEDA for economic cooperation? In practice, different types of cooperation may require different approval channels — but no protocol exists.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. No pending Parliament Bill addresses international cooperation. This is among the least legislated of the 55 enumerated powers.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP Chapter 7 (Economic Development) targets international trade linkages and investment attraction from OIC/ASEAN countries. BDP Chapter 13 (Plan Financing) identifies international cooperation as a channel for development financing and technical assistance. BDP Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity) envisions cultural exchange programs. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda, Pillar 3 (Economic Resilience), depends on international economic partnerships.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if the legislative gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without an institutional framework: (a) ODA and technical assistance opportunities may be missed due to lack of systematic identification and proposal development; (b) the embassy/consulate recommendation provision (Art. XII, Sec. 28) remains unexercised — no Bangsamoro voice in Philippine foreign service posts; (c) BIMP-EAGA engagement remains limited to the BBTC's barter trade mandate without broader economic cooperation; (d) cultural exchange programs with Muslim-majority countries lack institutional support; (e) international cooperation depends on ad hoc executive initiative rather than systematic strategy.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. What is the scope of "linkages" under Art. XII, Sec. 28 — what types of agreements can the Bangsamoro Government enter?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 28 uses the term "linkages" rather than "agreements" or "treaties." This suggests a broad but soft scope: memoranda of understanding, cultural exchange programs, technical cooperation arrangements, trade promotion activities, educational partnerships, and institutional cooperation agreements. These linkages are not treaties under Art. VII, Sec. 21 of the Constitution — they do not require Senate concurrence. They are sub-national cooperation arrangements subject to national executive approval.

Q. How does power (o) relate to power (xx) Technical cooperation for human resource development?

A. Power (xx) — "technical cooperation for human resource development" — is the human capital dimension of the broader power (o). Art. XII, Sec. 28 applies to both: the same "review and approval of the National Government" applies. The distinction is that power (o) covers all three dimensions (cultural, economic, technical) while power (xx) focuses specifically on human resource development through technical cooperation. The Development Academy of the Bangsamoro (DAB) (BAA 13, Book XVI, Ch. 7) is the lead agency for power (xx), working in coordination with OCM for power (o).

Q. How does the BIMP-EAGA dimension work under this power?

A. BIMP-EAGA engagement operates under two BOL provisions: (a) Art. XIII, Sec. 26 — barter trade regulation with "BIMP-EAGA Member States"; and (b) Art. XII, Sec. 28 — broader economic cooperation. The BBTC handles barter trade regulation, while OCM handles the broader BIMP-EAGA economic cooperation framework. BIMP-EAGA is significant because it represents the strongest international economic cooperation mandate in the BOL — the Bangsamoro Government is authorized to regulate barter trade with BIMP-EAGA members with no express "review and approval" qualifier in Art. XIII, Sec. 26 (though the general Art. XII, Sec. 28 requirement applies to broader cooperation).

Q. What is the practical significance of the embassy/consulate provision?

A. Art. XII, Sec. 28's provision that the Bangsamoro Government "may recommend to the National Government the assignment of qualified persons in Philippine embassies and consulates, and their participation in international delegations" is unprecedented in Philippine autonomous region law. It recognizes the Bangsamoro's unique international dimension — particularly relations with OIC member states where Bangsamoro cultural knowledge and language skills (Arabic, Malay) are valuable. The provision remains largely unexercised as of 2026. A systematic recommendation process could place Bangsamoro professionals in key embassies (Kuala Lumpur, Jakarta, Riyadh, Ankara, Islamabad) where they could serve as cultural and economic bridges.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

What laws should the Bangsamoro Parliament pass to fully exercise this power? Note: most powers are already exercised by MOAs through their BAA 13 (Administrative Code) mandates, but standalone legislation codifies standards, creates regulatory frameworks, and establishes institutional structures that a general mandate cannot.

Gap Type Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No framework for international cooperation activities Bangsamoro International Cooperation Act Institutional mechanism (dedicated International Cooperation Office under OCM); eligible cooperation activities and partner country priorities; internal approval process before national government submission; embassy/consulate recommendation procedures; international cooperation fund; monitoring and reporting Art. V, Sec. 2(o); Art. XII, Sec. 28-29
Legislative Embassy/consulate recommendation process not codified Bangsamoro Foreign Service Recommendation Protocol (by legislation or executive policy) Selection criteria for qualified persons; target embassies/consulates; recommendation process; coordination with DFA and CSC Art. XII, Sec. 28 (last sentence)
Institutional No standardized approval protocol with national government Intergovernmental Cooperation Protocol (by MOA with DFA/OP or legislation) Standardized submission format; review timeline; approval criteria; coordination channels for different cooperation types (cultural, economic, technical, ODA) Art. XII, Sec. 28; Art. VI (intergovernmental relations)

(p)
Culture and Language
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(p)

Power 16 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(p) Culture and Language

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(p) — "Culture and language;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(p) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required No — the BOL does not impose a national law consistency requirement on culture and language. Art. IX, Sec. 25 directs the BCPCH to "coordinate and work closely with" national cultural agencies — a coordination relationship, not subordination.
Lead Ministry/Office Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage (BCPCH) — attached to the Office of the Chief Minister (BAA 13, Title XVI, Ch. 4, Sec. 46)
Supporting Agencies Ministry of Basic, Higher and Technical Education (MBHTE — cultural education); Office of the Wali of the Bangsamoro (OTWB — ceremonial/cultural functions); Bangsamoro Library and Archives (OCM — BAA 13, Book II, Sec. (9))
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity and Heritage)
Priority Code — (no named legislative mandate, but BOL creates BCPCH by name)

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(p) "Culture and language;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over the full scope of cultural preservation, promotion, and development, as well as language policy within the region.
Art. IX, Sec. 24 "The Bangsamoro Government shall preserve the history, culture, arts, traditions, and the rich cultural heritage of the Bangsamoro people and their Sultanates, such as those of Sulu, Maguindanao, Kabuntalan, Buayan, the Royal Houses of Ranao and of the Iranun, and the non-Moro indigenous peoples of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. For this purpose, it shall create the Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage in coordination with the appropriate and relevant national government agencies." Core implementing provision. Defines the scope of cultural preservation — history, culture, arts, traditions, heritage of all constituent peoples (Moro Sultanates, Royal Houses, and non-Moro indigenous peoples). Creates the BCPCH by name — one of the few BOL-mandated institutions.
Art. IX, Sec. 25 "The Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage shall have the primary responsibility to write the history of the Bangsamoro people and to establish and sustain the cultural institutions, programs, and projects in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. The Commission shall coordinate and work closely with the National Commission for Culture and the Arts, National Historical Commission of the Philippines, National Museum of the Philippines, and other concerned cultural agencies for the preservation of cultural heritage, shall establish libraries and museums, declare and restore historical shrines and cultural sites to preserve the Bangsamoro heritage for posterity. The Commission shall ensure the inclusion of Philippine history, culture, and heritage in the establishment of museums and similar institutions, programs and projects to foster unity among Filipinos." Defines BCPCH's five mandates: (1) write Bangsamoro history; (2) establish and sustain cultural institutions/programs; (3) coordinate with NCCA, NHCP, National Museum; (4) establish libraries and museums, declare and restore historical shrines and cultural sites; (5) include Philippine national history in Bangsamoro cultural institutions. The last mandate ensures BCPCH institutions contribute to national unity, not separatism.
Art. IX, Sec. 26 "The National Government shall transfer the management of Bangsamoro historical and cultural sites currently under the jurisdiction of the National Museum of the Philippines, National Historical Commission of the Philippines, or other agencies of the National Government to the Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage through the intergovernmental relations mechanisms. The Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage shall coordinate with relevant agencies of the National Government on the regulation, excavation, and preservation of cultural artifacts and on the recovery of lost historical and cultural heritage." Mandatory transfer of site management from national agencies to BCPCH — this is one of the strongest devolution mandates in the BOL (***"shall transfer"***). Requires intergovernmental relations mechanisms. The BCPCH must continue to coordinate with national agencies on regulation, excavation, and preservation.
Art. II, Sec. 2 "The Parliament shall adopt the official flag, emblem, and hymn of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." Empowers the Parliament to adopt cultural identity symbols — the most visible expression of Bangsamoro cultural authority. Implemented by BAA 1 (Flag), BAA 2 (Emblem), BAA 7 (Hymn), BAA 16 (Parliament Seal).
Art. IX, Sec. 4 "The customs, beliefs, and traditions of the Bangsamoro people shall be recognized, protected, and guaranteed." Constitutional-level protection of customary cultural practices — broader than institutional heritage and covers living cultural traditions.
Art. IX, Sec. 3 "The rights of the non-Moro indigenous peoples within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region...shall include, but shall not be limited to the following:...(6) The right to the preservation and development of their cultures, traditions, and institutions..." Cultural rights of non-Moro indigenous peoples — Teduray, Lambangian, Dulangan Manobo, B'laan, Higaonon, and others. BCPCH must protect IP cultural heritage alongside Bangsamoro cultural heritage.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 — Bangsamoro Administrative Code Title XVI, Ch. 4, Sec. 46-60: creates BCPCH with 7 Commissioners, mandate, powers, functions, organizational structure, and provincial offices In Force
BAA 20 — Dual Calendar (Hijri/Gregorian) Mandates dual calendar use in official government operations — implements Islamic temporal culture in governance In Force
BAA 39 — Bangsamoro Holidays Establishes regional and special holidays reflecting Bangsamoro cultural and religious identity In Force
BAA 60 — Hashim and Mimbantas Memorial Site Declares burial site as memorial — preserves revolutionary heritage In Force
BAA 61 — Camp Abubakar Memorial Marker and Eco-Park Memorial for Bangsamoro shuhada (martyrs) — cultural heritage preservation In Force
BAA 85 — General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter XXII allocates operational budget to BCPCH In Force
Additionally, BAA 1 (Official Flag), BAA 2 (Official Emblem), BAA 7 (Official Hymn), and BAA 16 (Official Parliament Seal) implement the cultural identity dimension under Art. II, Sec. 2 — classified as general/administrative but culturally significant.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 10066 (2009) National Cultural Heritage Act of 2009 National framework for cultural heritage protection, conservation, and promotion — includes cultural property registration, cultural heritage zones, and heritage enforcement Suppletory — governs cultural heritage protection in BARMM absent a comprehensive Bangsamoro Cultural Heritage Code. BCPCH exercises parallel functions within the region.
RA 11961 (2023) Strengthening Conservation and Protection of Philippine Cultural Heritage through Cultural Mapping Amends RA 10066 — enhances cultural mapping and cultural heritage education programs Suppletory — cultural mapping initiatives in BARMM should align with national standards while reflecting Bangsamoro cultural specificity.
RA 7356 (1992) Creating the National Commission for Culture and the Arts (NCCA) BOL Art. IX, Sec. 25 directs BCPCH to ***"coordinate and work closely with the National Commission for Culture and the Arts"*** Coordination — BCPCH works with NCCA on cultural programs. NCCA retains national coordination role.
RA 10086 (2010) Strengthening the National Historical Commission of the Philippines (NHCP) BOL Art. IX, Sec. 26 requires transfer of Bangsamoro sites from NHCP to BCPCH Transfer mandate — Bangsamoro historical sites under NHCP are to be transferred to BCPCH management.
RA 11333 (2019) Strengthening the National Museum of the Philippines BOL Art. IX, Sec. 26 requires transfer of Bangsamoro sites from National Museum to BCPCH Transfer mandate — Bangsamoro cultural sites and collections under the National Museum are to be transferred to BCPCH.
RA 10573 (2013) Sheikh Karimul Makhdum Mosque as National Historical Landmark Declares the oldest mosque in the Philippines (Simunul, Tawi-Tawi) as a national historical landmark Directly applicable — this landmark is within BARMM territory. Management transfer to BCPCH falls under Art. IX, Sec. 26.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Hifz al-Din (Preservation of Faith) One of the five objectives (maqasid) of Shari'ah — preserving religion includes preserving religious cultural heritage, mosques, madaris, and Islamic scholarly traditions. Bangsamoro cultural heritage preservation is inseparable from Islamic heritage preservation. BCPCH's mandate to preserve Sultanate heritage, Islamic cultural sites, and Bangsamoro history embodies this objective [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].
Hifz al-Nasl (Preservation of Lineage/Identity) Preserving family, lineage, and collective identity is among the maqasid al-Shari'ah. Preservation of Bangsamoro identity — including Sultanate genealogies, clan structures, and ethno-linguistic identity — is an exercise of hifz al-nasl [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].
Arabic as Liturgical Language Arabic is the language of the Qur'an and the primary language of Islamic scholarship, prayer, and legal discourse. Language policy in BARMM has an Islamic dimension — Arabic language preservation and promotion supports Islamic education and religious practice. BAA 20's dual Hijri/Gregorian calendar and the proposed English-Arabic signage bill (PB 176) reflect this dimension.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area — defining boundaries of autonomy, resolving jurisdiction questions, or interpreting related provisions.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro cultural heritage authority or the jurisdictional boundary between BCPCH and national cultural agencies. The general principle — that autonomous regions may exercise cultural preservation powers within the constitutional framework — is established by Art. X, Sec. 20(8) of the 1987 Constitution.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs. Shows legislative intent and pending proposals.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus

PB 176 Implementing English-Arabic as official language for signages Language policy — first legislative step toward codifying Bangsamoro language policy Filed

PB 199 Establishing framework for promotion of the Bangsamoro Brand Cultural branding — identity promotion through economic branding Filed
PB 203 Establishing Peace and Culture Centers in Bangsamoro communities Cultural infrastructure — community-level cultural institutions Filed

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 6: Cultural Diversity and Heritage Preserve Bangsamoro identity, protect cultural heritage sites, promote intercultural understanding — the primary BDP chapter for this power
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 10: Peace and Security Cultural preservation and identity as foundation for social cohesion and peace
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 5: Social Cohesion Cultural diversity and language preservation as foundations for inclusive Bangsamoro identity

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow: BOL grant → constitutional floor → national law → implementation → Shari'ah dimension → divergence → gaps → development.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government over culture and language?

A. Article V, Section 2(p) of Republic Act No. 11054 enumerates "culture and language" as one of the 55 powers over which "the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority." This is a broad grant covering cultural heritage preservation, promotion, and development, as well as language policy within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. Art. IX, Sections 24 through 26 elaborate this power extensively — creating the BCPCH by name, defining its mandate, and requiring the transfer of historical and cultural site management from national agencies.12

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2?

A. Seven provisions elaborate on this power:

(1) Art. IX, Sec. 24 — creates the BCPCH and defines the scope of cultural preservation: "the history, culture, arts, traditions, and the rich cultural heritage of the Bangsamoro people and their Sultanates." The provision names specific heritage streams — Sultanates of Sulu and Maguindanao, Kabuntalan, Buayan, Royal Houses of Ranao and Iranun, and "the non-Moro indigenous peoples."2

(2) Art. IX, Sec. 25 — defines BCPCH's five mandates: write Bangsamoro history, establish cultural institutions, coordinate with NCCA/NHCP/National Museum, establish libraries and museums, and include Philippine national history in Bangsamoro institutions.3

(3) Art. IX, Sec. 26 — mandates transfer of site management: "The National Government shall transfer the management of Bangsamoro historical and cultural sites."4

(4) Art. II, Sec. 2 — cultural symbols: "The Parliament shall adopt the official flag, emblem, and hymn."5

(5) Art. IX, Sec. 4 — customary cultural protection: "The customs, beliefs, and traditions of the Bangsamoro people shall be recognized, protected, and guaranteed."6

(6) Art. IX, Sec. 3(6) — IP cultural rights: the right to "preservation and development of their cultures, traditions, and institutions."7

(7) Art. V, Sec. 2(kk) — the related power "Libraries and museums, and historical, cultural, and archaeological sites" — directly overlaps with BCPCH's institutional mandate.8

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power?

A. No — the BOL does not name a specific cultural heritage code or language act. However, Art. IX, Sec. 24 mandates the creation of the BCPCH ("it shall create"), which BAA 13 has fulfilled. The implied legislative mandate is for comprehensive enabling legislation that provides the BCPCH with the regulatory framework, heritage classification system, and enforcement tools it needs.

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power?

A. Yes. The BOL expressly creates the BCPCH in Art. IX, Sec. 24: "it shall create the Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage." This is one of the few institutions the BOL creates by name — alongside the Bangsamoro Human Rights Commission (Art. IX, Sec. 7), the Bangsamoro Sports Commission (Art. IX, Sec. 20), and others.

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power?

A. Two operational qualifications:

(1) Coordination requirement: Art. IX, Sec. 25 — BCPCH must "coordinate and work closely with" the NCCA, NHCP, National Museum, and other cultural agencies. This is a coordination obligation, not a subordination or consistency requirement.3

(2) National unity mandate: Art. IX, Sec. 25 — BCPCH must "ensure the inclusion of Philippine history, culture, and heritage in the establishment of museums and similar institutions, programs and projects to foster unity among Filipinos." Bangsamoro cultural institutions must not be exclusively parochial — they must include the national narrative.3

No national law consistency requirement is imposed — unlike powers such as civil service (Art. VII, Sec. 39) or education (Art. IX, Sec. 16).

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to culture and language?

A. Five categories apply:

(1) State policy on culture (Art. II, Sec. 17): "The State shall give priority to education, science and technology, arts, culture, and sports to foster patriotism and nationalism."9

(2) National culture (Art. XIV, Sec. 14): "The State shall foster the preservation, enrichment, and dynamic evolution of a Filipino national culture based on the principle of unity in diversity in a climate of free artistic and intellectual expression."10

(3) Arts and letters (Art. XIV, Sec. 15): "Arts and letters shall enjoy the patronage of the State. The State shall conserve, promote, and popularize the nation's historical and cultural heritage and resources."11

(4) Cultural treasure (Art. XIV, Sec. 16): "All the country's artistic and historic wealth constitutes the cultural treasure of the nation and shall be under the protection of the State."12

(5) IP cultural rights (Art. XIV, Sec. 17): "The State shall recognize, respect, and protect the rights of indigenous cultural communities to preserve and develop their cultures, traditions, and institutions."13

(6) Autonomous region powers (Art. X, Sec. 20(8)): "Preservation and development of the cultural heritage" is expressly listed as an autonomous region legislative power.14

(7) Language (Art. XIV, Sec. 6-9): Filipino is the national language; Filipino and English are the official languages; regional languages are auxiliary.15

Q. What constitutional mandates for cultural legislation or institutions exist?

A. Art. XIV, Sec. 14-18 establishes the State's affirmative obligations for cultural preservation, arts patronage, and cultural access. Art. X, Sec. 20(8) expressly authorizes autonomous regions to legislate on "preservation and development of the cultural heritage." These constitutional mandates provide the strongest basis for comprehensive Bangsamoro cultural heritage legislation.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for this subject area?

A. The national framework centers on: RA 10066 (National Cultural Heritage Act, as amended by RA 11961) — comprehensive heritage protection, conservation, and management; RA 7356 (NCCA) — national cultural coordination; RA 10086 (NHCP) — national historical commission; RA 11333 (National Museum) — national museum system. Together, these laws provide: a heritage classification system, regulatory framework for cultural property, institutional infrastructure, and enforcement mechanisms.16171819

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM?

A. (1) RA 10066 / RA 11961 — suppletory: governs cultural heritage protection in BARMM absent a comprehensive Bangsamoro Cultural Heritage Code.16 (2) RA 7356 — coordination: BCPCH coordinates with NCCA per Art. IX, Sec. 25.17 (3) RA 10086 — transfer mandate: NHCP sites in BARMM to be transferred to BCPCH per Art. IX, Sec. 26.18 (4) RA 11333 — transfer mandate: National Museum sites in BARMM to be transferred to BCPCH.19 (5) RA 10573 — directly applicable: Sheikh Karimul Makhdum Mosque in Tawi-Tawi declared national historical landmark.

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area?

A. NCCA exercises national cultural coordination. NHCP manages historical sites and markers. National Museum manages museum collections and cultural properties. All three retain operational presence in BARMM pending full implementation of the Art. IX, Sec. 26 transfer mandate.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power?

A. The BCPCH is the lead institution. BAA 13, Title XVI, Chapter 4, Sec. 46-60 defines its institutional status, mandate, powers, functions, and organizational structure. BAA 85, Chapter XXII allocates its operational budget.2021

Q. What standalone legislation (BAAs) has the Parliament enacted for this power?

A. Five BAAs directly implement this power: BAA 20 (dual calendar), BAA 39 (Bangsamoro holidays), BAA 60 (Hashim/Mimbantas memorial), BAA 61 (Camp Abubakar memorial). Additionally, BAA 1 (flag), BAA 2 (emblem), BAA 7 (hymn), and BAA 16 (Parliament seal) implement the cultural identity dimension.

Q. What are BCPCH's mandate and functions under BAA 13?

A. BAA 13, Title XVI, Chapter 4 (Sec. 46-60) defines BCPCH comprehensively:

Institutional status (Sec. 46): BCPCH is composed of a Chairperson and six (6) Commissioners, with "the same status as the other agencies or offices of the Bangsamoro Government attached to the Office of the Chief Minister."20

Mandate (Sec. 47): Repeats the BOL Art. IX, Sec. 25 mandate verbatim — write Bangsamoro history, establish and sustain cultural institutions, coordinate with NCCA/NHCP/National Museum, establish libraries and museums, declare and restore historical shrines and cultural sites.22

Site transfer (Sec. 48): Repeats the BOL Art. IX, Sec. 26 mandate — "The National Government shall transfer the management of Bangsamoro historical and cultural sites."23

Powers (Sec. 49) — six enumerated powers: - (a) "Identify, declare, validate, and monitor historical shrines and sites, including the institution or markers and landmarks" - (b) "Recommend to the Bangsamoro Government regional policies and legislation on culture, arts, heritage promotion and development" - (c) "Establish consultative mechanisms...for continuing dialogue between the Bangsamoro Government and the cultural groups" - (d) "Accredit, register, and oversee cultural mapping initiatives and registry of cultural property" - (e) "Solicit and accept donations, gifts, requests, and grants...subject to the approval of the Cabinet" - (f) "Perform any and all other acts incident to or required by virtue of its creation"24

Functions (Sec. 50) — eight enumerated functions: - (a) Support retrieval and conservation of Bangsamoro cultural artifacts - (b) Encourage preservation of "endangered human cultural resources such as weavers, chanters, dancers, and other craftsmen" - (c) Support preservation of cultural and historical monuments, markers, sites - (d) Encourage establishment of "museums, libraries, archives" - (e) Encourage private sector to establish museums and libraries - (f) Encourage scholarly research into Bangsamoro cultural traditions - (g) Encourage "writing of history from the Bangsamoro perspective" - (h) Ensure standards of excellence through symposia, workshops, and publications25

Composition (Sec. 51): Seven Commissioners — five representing the major political subdivisions (Maguindanao, Lanao del Sur, Sulu, Basilan, Tawi-Tawi), plus two ex officio from non-Moro indigenous peoples and settler communities.26

Provincial offices (Sec. 56-57): Each Commissioner heads a provincial office executing cultural development programs at the provincial level.27

Executive Director (Sec. 58): Appointed by the Chief Minister, responsible for day-to-day operations, four-year fixed term.28

Q. What can BCPCH NOT do without a standalone cultural heritage code?

A. BCPCH can identify, declare, validate, and monitor heritage sites under BAA 13. However, without standalone legislation, it cannot: (a) establish a systematic heritage classification system (tangible/intangible, movable/immovable, grades of significance); (b) impose conservation standards and penalties for heritage destruction; (c) create a comprehensive cultural property registry with legal effect; (d) regulate excavation and artifact trade; (e) establish a language preservation and promotion framework; (f) define heritage buffer zones and development controls around cultural sites; (g) codify the site transfer process from national agencies (Art. IX, Sec. 26).

Q. Have any parliamentary resolutions signaled intent to legislate in this area?

A. The Parliament has expressed extensive engagement through 17 resolutions touching culture and heritage (see Resolutions table above). These demonstrate strong legislative interest but are non-binding expressions — the legislative gap is in enacted law, not intent.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is the assigned lead MOA the correct one for this power?

A. Yes. The BOL expressly creates the BCPCH for this power (Art. IX, Sec. 24). The assignment is constitutionally and legislatively unambiguous. BCPCH covers the full scope of cultural heritage preservation. The language dimension is not fully covered by BCPCH — language preservation and promotion may require a separate institutional mechanism or an expansion of BCPCH's mandate.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power?

A. - Lead agency: BCPCH (BAA 13, Title XVI, Ch. 4; BAA 85, Ch. XXII) - Supporting: MBHTE — cultural education in schools (Art. IX, Sec. 14-19) - Supporting: OTWB — ceremonial and cultural functions of the Wali - Supporting: Bangsamoro Library and Archives (BAA 13, Book II, Sec. (9)) — institutional repository - Cultural identity dimension: Parliament has enacted BAAs 1, 2, 7, 16 (cultural symbols)

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM?

A. NCCA provides cultural coordination and grant funding for cultural programs. NHCP manages historical markers and sites including those in BARMM (pending transfer). National Museum manages cultural properties and collections (pending transfer). These agencies maintain their operations in BARMM, though the BOL mandates management transfer for Bangsamoro-specific sites.

Q. What is the status of devolution for this power?

A. Devolution is partially complete. BCPCH has been created and funded, but the Art. IX, Sec. 26 transfer of site management from NHCP, National Museum, and other agencies appears to remain incomplete. No inventory of sites to be transferred has been enacted, and no BAA codifies the transfer process, timeline, or post-transfer management standards.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power?

A. The Bangsamoro Government has: (a) created BCPCH as a fully constituted commission with 7 Commissioners and provincial offices; (b) enacted 4 culture-specific BAAs (BAA 20, 39, 60, 61) and 4 cultural identity BAAs (BAA 1, 2, 7, 16); (c) adopted 17 parliamentary resolutions on cultural matters; (d) established the Bangsamoro Library and Archives; (e) begun cultural mapping and heritage identification at the provincial level. This is among the most actively legislated power areas, though the legislation is piecemeal rather than comprehensive.

Q. Where is the BARMM stronger than the national government in this area?

A. BARMM has three unique advantages: (1) Islamic cultural heritage expertise — BCPCH understands Bangsamoro Islamic cultural heritage (Sultanate records, mosque architecture, okir traditions, Arabic manuscripts) in ways that national cultural agencies do not. (2) Ethno-linguistic depth — BCPCH can operate in Meranao, Maguindanaon, Tausug, Sama, and Yakan — reaching communities that national agencies serve primarily in Filipino and English. (3) Living cultural resource protection — BAA 13 Sec. 50(b) mandates preservation of "endangered human cultural resources such as weavers, chanters, dancers, and other craftsmen" — a mandate the national system addresses less directly.

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation?

A. The primary constraint is the absence of a comprehensive Bangsamoro Cultural Heritage Code — without it, BCPCH operates on the general mandate of BAA 13 without systematic heritage classification, conservation standards, penalty provisions, or regulatory tools. The secondary constraint is the incomplete transfer of site management from national agencies (Art. IX, Sec. 26).

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension?

A. Yes — culture and language have a significant Shari'ah dimension for the Bangsamoro people. Bangsamoro cultural heritage is inseparable from Islamic heritage — the Sultanates, mosques, madaris, Islamic scholarship traditions, Arabic-script manuscripts, and Islamic artistic traditions (okir, geometric patterns, calligraphy) are simultaneously cultural and religious heritage. Three Islamic principles apply:

(1) Hifz al-Din (preservation of faith) — preserving Islamic cultural heritage, including mosques, religious manuscripts, and Sultanate records, is an exercise of this maqasid objective [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

(2) Hifz al-Nasl (preservation of lineage/identity) — preserving Sultanate genealogies, clan structures (tarsila), and ethno-linguistic identity serves this objective [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

(3) Arabic as liturgical language — Arabic language preservation supports Qur'anic literacy, Islamic education, and religious practice [fiqh — not enacted in BARMM law].

Q. What does the BOL say about Shari'ah in relation to this power?

A. The BOL does not specifically reference Shari'ah in Art. IX, Sec. 24-26. However, Art. IX, Sec. 24's reference to "the rich cultural heritage of the Bangsamoro people and their Sultanates" necessarily encompasses Islamic cultural heritage. The dual calendar (BAA 20) and Bangsamoro holidays (BAA 39) reflect the Islamic dimension of cultural policy.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national law and policy in this area?

A. BARMM has broad authority to diverge because: (1) as established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), the BOL imposes no national law consistency requirement — only a coordination obligation; (2) as established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), Art. X, Sec. 20(8) expressly lists "preservation and development of the cultural heritage" as an autonomous region legislative power; (3) the BOL creates BCPCH by name as the Bangsamoro cultural institution, signaling congressional intent for a distinct cultural governance framework.

BARMM can: (a) establish a distinct heritage classification system recognizing Islamic, Sultanate, and indigenous heritage categories that RA 10066 does not specifically address; (b) create Bangsamoro-specific heritage categories (e.g., Sultanate heritage, liberation movement heritage, Islamic architectural heritage); (c) enact language preservation legislation that the national framework does not provide; (d) develop culturally appropriate conservation standards that account for Islamic requirements (mosque preservation, halal considerations in heritage sites).

The constitutional floor is Art. XIV, Sec. 14 ("unity in diversity") and Art. IX, Sec. 25's own requirement to "ensure the inclusion of Philippine history, culture, and heritage."

G. Legislative Gaps

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority?

A. The principal overlap is between BCPCH and the three national cultural agencies (NCCA, NHCP, National Museum). Art. IX, Sec. 26 mandates transfer of site management — but until a comprehensive code codifies the transfer, both BCPCH and national agencies may exercise parallel functions over the same sites. Additionally, the cultural property registry function (BCPCH Sec. 49(d)) overlaps with NCCA's cultural mapping functions under RA 11961.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps?

A. PB 204 (comprehensive cultural heritage code) is the most significant — if enacted, it would address heritage classification, conservation, management, and enforcement. PB 176 (English-Arabic signage) addresses language policy. PB 203 (Peace and Culture Centers) and PB 299 (Bangsamoro history museum) address cultural infrastructure. Even if all pending bills pass, a language preservation and promotion framework and a site transfer codification remain unaddressed.

H. Development Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power?

A. BDP Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity and Heritage) is entirely dependent on this power — its goals of preserving Bangsamoro identity, protecting heritage sites, and promoting intercultural understanding require comprehensive cultural heritage legislation. BDP Chapter 10 (Peace and Security) depends on cultural preservation as a foundation for social cohesion. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda, Pillar 5 (Social Cohesion), requires cultural diversity and language preservation as foundations for an inclusive Bangsamoro identity that embraces all constituent peoples.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if the legislative gaps remain unaddressed?

A. Without a comprehensive cultural heritage code: (a) heritage sites continue to deteriorate without systematic conservation standards; (b) the Marawi cultural recovery (PB 181) lacks a legal framework for recovering and protecting cultural properties lost during the siege; (c) intangible cultural heritage — languages, oral traditions, performing arts, traditional crafts — continue to erode without preservation programs; (d) the Art. IX, Sec. 26 site transfer remains incomplete, with Bangsamoro heritage sites managed by national agencies unfamiliar with their cultural significance; (e) language diversity declines as Bangsamoro languages lack official support in education and governance.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. How does this power relate to power (kk) Libraries, museums, and historical, cultural, and archaeological sites?

A. Powers (p) and (kk) overlap significantly. Art. V, Sec. 2(kk) — "Libraries and museums, and historical, cultural, and archaeological sites" — is the institutional dimension of cultural heritage. Art. V, Sec. 2(p) — "Culture and language" — is the broader policy dimension. BCPCH exercises both powers under a single institutional mandate. Art. IX, Sec. 25 explicitly assigns BCPCH the function of establishing "libraries and museums" and declaring and restoring "historical shrines and cultural sites." A comprehensive cultural heritage code should address both powers together.

Q. What languages are spoken in BARMM and how does the BOL address them?

A. BARMM's major languages include Maguindanaon (Maguindanao provinces), Meranao (Lanao del Sur), Tausug (Sulu), Sama (Tawi-Tawi, Basilan), Yakan (Basilan), plus Iranun, Teduray, Lambangian, and other indigenous languages. Arabic serves as a liturgical and scholarly language. The BOL is largely silent on specific language policy — Art. V, Sec. 2(p) grants the power but no provision specifies which languages should be preserved, promoted, or used in governance. BAA 20's dual calendar (Hijri/Gregorian) is the closest to language-adjacent legislation. A Bangsamoro Languages Act is needed to codify language rights, preservation programs, and official language use in regional governance and education.

Q. What is the significance of the "history writing" mandate?

A. Art. IX, Sec. 25 assigns BCPCH the "primary responsibility to write the history of the Bangsamoro people." This is an extraordinary mandate — it authorizes a government institution to produce an official historical narrative. The significance is that Bangsamoro history has historically been written from external perspectives (colonial, national). The BOL empowers the Bangsamoro people to write their own history — including the narrative of the Sultanates, colonialism, the Moro liberation struggle, and the peace process. BAA 13 Sec. 50(g) reinforces this: "encourage and support the writing of history from the Bangsamoro perspective." This mandate has both cultural and political dimensions — it shapes collective identity and informs the Bangsamoro people's understanding of their place in Philippine and world history.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

What laws should the Bangsamoro Parliament pass to fully exercise this power? Note: most powers are already exercised by MOAs through their BAA 13 (Administrative Code) mandates, but standalone legislation codifies standards, creates regulatory frameworks, and establishes institutional structures that a general mandate cannot.

Gap Type Gap What Law Should Be Passed What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No comprehensive cultural heritage framework despite BOL-mandated BCPCH Bangsamoro Cultural Heritage Code (PB 204 pending) Heritage classification system (tangible/intangible, Sultanate/Islamic/indigenous categories); conservation standards and penalties; cultural property registry with legal effect; heritage buffer zones; excavation and artifact trade regulation; cultural impact assessment; post-transfer management standards for Art. IX, Sec. 26 sites Art. IX, Sec. 24-26; Art. V, Sec. 2(p)(kk)
Legislative No language preservation and promotion framework Bangsamoro Languages Act Language rights; official language use in regional governance; language preservation programs for endangered languages (Sama, Yakan, Teduray, Lambangian); Arabic language promotion; language in education; signage standards (PB 176 related) Art. V, Sec. 2(p)
Institutional Art. IX, Sec. 26 site transfer process remains uncodified Cultural Site Transfer Act (or title within the Heritage Code) Inventory of sites to be transferred; transfer process and timeline; management standards; BCPCH-NHCP-National Museum coordination protocols; funding for transferred site maintenance Art. IX, Sec. 26 ("shall transfer")
Legislative No cultural institutions framework beyond two memorial sites Bangsamoro Museums and Cultural Institutions Act (PB 203, 299 related) Standards for museum establishment; cultural centers in provinces; community-level cultural infrastructure; accreditation and funding mechanisms Art. IX, Sec. 25 ("establish and sustain cultural institutions")
