(vv)
Social Services
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(vv)

Power 42 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(vv) Social Services, Social Welfare, and Charities

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(vv) — "Social services, social welfare, and charities;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(vv) — enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required No express consistency requirement with national social welfare law. Art. V, Sec. 2 is "Subject to Section 20, Article X of the Constitution" — establishing a constitutional floor. Art. IX, Sec. 8 mandates delivery of "basic and responsive health programs, quality education, appropriate services, livelihood opportunities" but does not require consistency with any national statute. Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandates a "special agency" for persons with special needs and disadvantaged groups — again without a national law consistency qualifier.
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Social Services and Development (MSSD) — BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIII, Sec. 1-22
Supporting Agencies Bangsamoro Women Commission (BWC, BAA 8); Bangsamoro Youth Commission (BYC, BAA 10); Bangsamoro Human Rights Commission (BHRC, BAA 4); Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission (BAA 83); Provincial/City/Municipal Social Welfare Offices (BAA 13, Title XIII, Ch. 4)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 9 (Social Services — Education, Health, Social Welfare); Chapter 2 (Regional Trends — social protection targets)
Named Legislative Mandate No named code. Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandates creation of a "special agency" for persons with special needs — not yet established as a standalone body. Art. IX, Sec. 14 mandates protection of children's rights.

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data — BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power — verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(vv) "Social services, social welfare, and charities;" Grants the Bangsamoro Government authority over the full scope of social services delivery, social welfare programs, and regulation of charitable activities within the region. The three-part enumeration — services, welfare, and charities — encompasses both direct service provision and the regulatory dimension of charitable solicitation.
Art. V, Sec. 2 (chapeau) "Subject to Section 20, Article X of the Constitution and this Organic Law, the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority over the following matters without prejudice to the general supervision of the President of the Republic of the Philippines:" The constitutional floor: all enumerated powers operate within the framework of the Constitution. The President retains general supervision but not control over how the Bangsamoro Government exercises this power.
Art. IX, Sec. 8 "The Bangsamoro Government shall provide, maintain, and ensure the delivery of basic and responsive health programs, quality education, appropriate services, livelihood opportunities, affordable and progressive housing projects, power and electricity, and water supply, among others, to the Bangsamoro people and other inhabitants of the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. It shall maintain appropriate disaster-preparedness units for immediate and effective relief services to victims of natural and man-made calamities. It shall also ensure the rehabilitation of calamity-affected areas and victims of calamities." The right of access to basic services — establishes a positive obligation on the Bangsamoro Government to provide social services. The phrase "appropriate services" encompasses social welfare interventions. Also creates a disaster-preparedness and rehabilitation mandate that intersects with MSSD's disaster response function.
Art. IX, Sec. 12 "The Bangsamoro Government shall uphold and protect the fundamental rights of women, including the right to engage in lawful employment, and to be protected from exploitation, abuse, or discrimination, as embodied in the Convention on the Elimination of all Forms of Discrimination Against Women. The Parliament shall enact the necessary laws for the implementation of this section." Mandates protection of women's rights and requires Parliament to enact implementing legislation. Directly intersects with MSSD's protective services mandate for women victims of gender-based violence.
Art. IX, Sec. 13 "The Bangsamoro Government shall recognize the vital role of the youth in nation-building, promote and protect their physical, moral, spiritual, intellectual, and social well-being. It shall inculcate patriotism and nationalism, encourage involvement in public and civic affairs, and promote mental and physical fitness through sports. The Parliament shall create by law a commission on youth and shall define its powers, functions, and composition." Mandates youth protection and development. The BYC (BAA 10) fulfills the commission mandate. MSSD's welfare programs for street children, children in conflict with the law, and orphans complement the BYC's broader youth development mandate.
Art. IX, Sec. 14 "The Bangsamoro Government shall respect, protect, and promote the rights of children, especially orphans of tender age. They shall be protected from exploitation, abuse, or discrimination. Their education and development, both physical and mental, shall be fully addressed." Child protection mandate — specifically names orphans. Provides the BOL basis for MSSD's orphanage institutions, centers for street children, and rehabilitation centers for children in conflict with the law (BAA 13, Title XIII, Sec. 12).
Art. IX, Sec. 23 "The Bangsamoro Government shall establish a special agency, support facilities and livelihood or skills training for persons with special needs, and other disadvantaged persons for their rehabilitation and productive integration into mainstream society. The Bangsamoro Government shall coordinate all existing governmental and nongovernmental agencies concerned with the rehabilitation and education of these persons. The Bangsamoro Government shall ensure the welfare of senior citizens by upholding their rights and providing the benefits and privileges they presently enjoy." Three mandates in one provision: (1) create a special agency for persons with special needs and disadvantaged persons; (2) coordinate all governmental and non-governmental rehabilitation agencies; (3) ensure senior citizens' welfare — with the floor set by benefits and privileges they "presently enjoy" (i.e., those under RA 9994 and RA 7432). This is the strongest BOL mandate for social welfare institutional development.
Art. XII, Sec. 19 "The Parliament shall assign the highest budgetary priority to education, health, and social services as may be provided in its appropriations law." Fiscal priority mandate — social services is one of three sectors that must receive the "highest budgetary priority" in the annual appropriations law. This constitutionalizes the funding floor for MSSD and related agencies.
Art. VII, Sec. 38 "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to create offices and organize its own bureaucracy. It may establish other offices and institutions necessary for the exercise of its functions and delivery of social services in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region." General organizational authority — explicitly mentions "delivery of social services" as a basis for creating offices and institutions. Provides the legal basis for MSSD's organizational structure and any new social welfare bodies.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 -- Bangsamoro Administrative Code Book VI, Title XIII, Sec. 1-22: establishes MSSD with 18 enumerated powers and functions, organizational structure (7 services/divisions), provincial/city/municipal social welfare offices, welfare facilities mandate (9 facility types), fund drive regulation, and social welfare agency accreditation In Force
BAA 8 -- Bangsamoro Women Commission Creates BWC — gender and development body that coordinates with MSSD on protective services for women, including VAWC survivors and former women combatants In Force
BAA 10 -- Bangsamoro Youth Commission Creates BYC per Art. IX, Sec. 13 mandate — youth welfare and development; coordinates with MSSD on programs for street children, orphans, and children in conflict with the law In Force
BAA 57 -- Mujahideen Under Special Circumstance Act Provides financial assistance to senior and disabled MILF-MNLF mujahideen — a social welfare program for a specific conflict-affected population. Directly implements the (vv) social services power for ex-combatants In Force
BAA 62 -- Rights of Internally Displaced Persons Act Protects IDP rights — intersects with MSSD's disaster response and rehabilitation mandate for conflict- and disaster-displaced populations In Force
BAA 83 -- Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission Act Creates the Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission — nutrition programs for children, pregnant and lactating women, and vulnerable populations; coordinates with MSSD on malnutrition response In Force
BAA 85 -- General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter XIV allocates operational budget to MSSD, confirming institutional existence and operational funding In Force
No standalone social welfare code or comprehensive social protection act has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 43 -- Calling for the Creation of a Bureau for Orphans and Widows of War Signals Parliament's intent to create a dedicated institution for conflict orphans and widows — a population not fully served by MSSD's general protective services mandate
Res. No. 65 -- Confirming Committee Membership of the Committee on Social Services Establishes the parliamentary committee that oversees social welfare legislation — institutional governance structure
Res. No. 115 -- Calling for Rationalized Guidelines for Livelihood Projects Urges standardization of livelihood assistance — a core MSSD function under BAA 13, Sec. 3(a)
Res. No. 116 -- Calling for Monitoring of RA 11310 Implementation (Conditional Cash Transfers) Monitors Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program (4Ps) implementation in BARMM — the largest national social protection program operating in the region, administered by DSWD
Res. No. 117 -- Requesting Creation of Database for Bangsamoro Indigents Signals need for a social registry — a targeting mechanism for social welfare programs that MSSD currently lacks
Res. No. 147 -- Calling for Establishment of Drug Treatment and Rehabilitation Centers Specialized social welfare facility — intersects with MSSD's rehabilitation mandate under BAA 13, Sec. 2(c)
Res. No. 179 -- Supporting IDPs' Call to Return to Properties in Marawi Most Affected Area Post-conflict social welfare — Marawi displacement remains a major social services challenge involving MSSD's disaster response and IDP support functions
Res. No. 450 -- Condemning the Killing of an MSSD Employee Reflects the security risks faced by social welfare field workers in conflict-affected areas

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 7277 (1992) Magna Carta for Disabled Persons (as amended by RA 9442, RA 10524, RA 10754) Comprehensive framework for PWD rehabilitation, self-development, and integration into mainstream society; mandates PWD offices in LGUs (RA 10070) Suppletory: applies in BARMM pending a Bangsamoro social welfare code. Art. IX, Sec. 23's mandate to create a "special agency" for persons with special needs parallels but does not supersede RA 7277's protections. The benefits and privileges PWDs "presently enjoy" under RA 7277 are the constitutional floor.
RA 9994 (2010) Expanded Senior Citizens Act (amending RA 7432) Grants senior citizens 20% discount, VAT exemption, free medical/dental services, mandatory PhilHealth coverage (RA 10645); mandates Office for Senior Citizens Affairs in LGUs Applies directly: Art. IX, Sec. 23 expressly states the Bangsamoro Government shall ensure senior citizens receive ***"the benefits and privileges they presently enjoy"*** — this is RA 9994. Cannot be diminished by BAA.
RA 7610 (1992) Special Protection of Children Against Abuse, Exploitation and Discrimination Act (as amended by RA 9231, RA 11648) Comprehensive child protection — defines child abuse, establishes reporting mechanisms, imposes criminal penalties; as amended by RA 9231 (worst forms of child labor) and RA 11648 (statutory rape age raised to 16) Applies directly: criminal law provisions are residual national power; protective services components apply suppletorily where no BAA covers the specific area.
RA 9262 (2004) Anti-Violence Against Women and Their Children Act Defines VAWC, establishes protection orders (barangay, temporary, permanent), mandates shelters and counseling; creates criminal liability Applies directly: criminal law is residual national power. MSSD operates VAWC crisis intervention facilities (BAA 13, Sec. 12(e)) as the service delivery complement to RA 9262's protection order system.
RA 9344 (2006) Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act (as amended by RA 10630) Comprehensive juvenile justice system — minimum age of criminal responsibility (15), diversion, community-based rehabilitation, Bahay Pag-asa facilities Applies directly: criminal law is residual national power. MSSD operates rehabilitation centers for children in conflict with the law (BAA 13, Sec. 12(f)) as the welfare component.
RA 11310 (2019) Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program (4Ps) Act Institutionalizes the conditional cash transfer program — the largest social protection program in the Philippines; DSWD as implementing agency Applies directly: national social protection program administered by DSWD. Parliament Resolution No. 116 called for monitoring its BARMM implementation. DSWD retains operational authority; MSSD coordinates at the regional level.
RA 9710 (2009) Magna Carta of Women Comprehensive women's rights framework — discrimination-free access to services, GAD budgeting (5% of total budget), VAW response system Applies as law of general application: GAD budgeting requirement applies to all government agencies including BARMM. BWC (BAA 8) is the Bangsamoro counterpart implementing this mandate.
RA 11596 (2021) Anti-Child Marriage Act Criminalizes child marriage, solemnization thereof, and cohabitation with minors; imposes prision mayor penalties Applies directly: criminal law is residual national power. Penalties exceed BARMM's arresto menor jurisdiction. As established in the constitutional analysis framework, RA 11596 implements Art. II, Sec. 13 (children's welfare) and CEDAW commitments.
RA 6972 (1990) Barangay-Level Total Development and Protection of Children Act Mandates a day care center in every barangay; institutes community-based child development program Suppletory: applies to BARMM barangays. MSSD's child welfare programs at the municipal/barangay level build on this framework. BAA 49 (Local Governance Code) may address LGU social welfare functions.
RA 10821 (2016) Children's Emergency Relief and Protection Act Mandates provision of emergency relief and protection for children before, during, and after disasters Suppletory: intersects with MSSD's disaster response mandate (BAA 13, Sec. 3(p)) and the broader Art. IX, Sec. 8 calamity rehabilitation obligation.
RA 9208 (2003) Anti-Trafficking in Persons Act (as amended by RA 10364) Criminalizes trafficking, especially of women and children; mandates support services for victims Applies directly: criminal law is residual national power. MSSD provides protective services for trafficking survivors through its crisis intervention facilities.
RA 9775 (2009) Anti-Child Pornography Act Defines and criminalizes child pornography; mandates protective services for child victims Applies directly: criminal law is residual national power.
RA 8972 (2000) Solo Parents' Welfare Act Benefits and privileges for solo parents and their children — flexible work schedules, parental leave, livelihood development Suppletory: applies to BARMM solo parents. No BAA addresses solo parent welfare specifically.
RA 11228 (2019) Mandatory PhilHealth Coverage for All PWDs Mandates automatic PhilHealth enrollment and premium subsidy for all PWDs Applies directly: national health insurance program. Complements Art. IX, Sec. 23's PWD welfare mandate.
RA 10754 (2016) Expanded Benefits and Privileges of PWDs 20% discount on goods and services, tax exemptions, educational assistance, PWD ID system Applies directly: PWD benefits and privileges form the floor that cannot be diminished under Art. IX, Sec. 23.
RA 8552 (1998) Domestic Adoption Act Rules and policies on domestic adoption of Filipino children; DSWD as central authority Suppletory: DSWD retains central authority for adoption proceedings. MSSD role is limited to local assessment and case management support.
EO 123 (1987) Reorganizing the Ministry of Social Services and Development (now DSWD) Established the national DSWD organizational framework Historical: the national DSWD structure was the basis for MSSD's organizational design under BAA 13. BARMM has its own organizational authority under Art. VII, Sec. 38.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Maqasid al-Shari'ah Hifz al-Nafs (preservation of life) and Hifz al-Nasl (preservation of progeny/lineage) — two of the five essential objectives of Islamic law Directly underpin social welfare legislation: protecting life encompasses disaster response, health-related social services, and support for the destitute; preserving progeny/lineage encompasses child protection, orphan care, and family welfare
Maqasid al-Shari'ah Maslahah (public interest / welfare) — the overarching principle that legislation must serve the common good The principal justification for government social welfare programs in Islamic jurisprudence: the state has an obligation (wajib) to ensure the basic needs of all citizens are met, particularly the poor, orphans, widows, and disabled
Qur'anic mandate Zakat — the eight categories of zakat recipients (Qur'an 9:60) include the poor (fuqara'), the needy (masakin), and wayfarers (ibn al-sabil) Provides the Islamic legal foundation for targeted social welfare: zakat distribution is the Islamic counterpart to means-tested social assistance. A Bangsamoro social welfare framework could integrate zakat as a supplementary funding mechanism alongside government appropriations
Islamic obligation Kafala (Islamic guardianship/fostering) — the preferred alternative to formal adoption in Islamic law, as adoption (tabanni) that severs biological lineage is not recognized in classical fiqh Directly relevant to orphan care: MSSD's orphanage institutions (BAA 13, Sec. 12(h)) should accommodate kafala as a Shari'ah-compliant alternative to the adoption framework under RA 8552. This is a key area where Bangsamoro social welfare legislation can diverge from the national framework
PD 1083, Art. 70-72 Code of Muslim Personal Laws provisions on guardianship (walaya) — covers guardianship of minors, incompetents, and their property Intersects with child welfare: Shari'ah courts have jurisdiction over guardianship of Muslim minors. MSSD's child protection work must coordinate with the Shari'ah court system on custody and guardianship matters

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
Ali et al. v. BTA Parliament, G.R. No. E-02219 Sept. 30, 2025 BARMM legislative authority "must always operate within the bounds of the Constitution and national statutes." The Art. X, Sec. 20 limitation is "clear and categorical." Any Bangsamoro social welfare legislation must comply with constitutional rights (equal protection, due process) and cannot diminish rights established by national social welfare laws — particularly those cited in Art. IX, Sec. 23's "benefits and privileges they presently enjoy" language.
Province of Sulu v. Medialdea, G.R. Nos. 242255, 243246, 243693 Sept. 9, 2024 BOL constitutional; BARMM's powers under the BOL upheld against constitutional challenge Confirms the validity of BARMM's enumerated powers including (vv) social services. The Bangsamoro Government's authority to legislate on social welfare rests on firm constitutional ground.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs. Shows legislative intent and pending proposals.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
PB 245 Establishing a Program for Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder Specialized social welfare program for children with ASD — addresses the Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandate for persons with special needs Filed
PB 250 Establishing the Bangsamoro Disability Affairs Office Would create the special agency mandated by Art. IX, Sec. 23 for persons with special needs — the single most important unfulfilled institutional mandate for this power Filed
PB 292 Establishing Economic Empowerment Framework for Former Women Combatants Social welfare program for female ex-combatants — extends BAA 57's mujahideen welfare model to women combatants specifically Filed
PB 293 Providing Financial Aid for Medication Maintenance of Senior Citizens Would expand senior citizen benefits beyond RA 9994 — going above the national floor as permitted under Art. IX, Sec. 23 Filed
PB 294 Ensuring Equitable Healthcare through Medical Assistance for Indigents Means-tested medical assistance — intersects with MSSD's indigent welfare programs and the Bangsamoro indigent database called for in Res. No. 117 Filed
PB 301 Providing Haven for Women Victims of Gender-Based Violence Would establish dedicated women's shelters — complements MSSD's existing VAWC crisis intervention facilities (BAA 13, Sec. 12(e)) Filed
PB 326 Providing Assistance and Benefits to Families of Non-Decommissioned Martyred Mujahideen Extends the BAA 57 social assistance model to a broader category of conflict-affected families Filed
PB 375 Establishing Community-Based Health and Wellness Program for Senior Citizens Community-level senior citizen welfare — builds on RA 9994's senior citizen center mandate with a Bangsamoro-specific community health model Filed
PB 379 Granting Death Benefit and Disability Assistance to Families of Barangay Officials Social welfare coverage for barangay officials — addresses a gap in the social protection system for local governance workers Filed
PB 381 Institutionalizing Empowerment and Welfare of Ulama and Faith-Based Counselors Social welfare program for ulama — a uniquely Bangsamoro category of social welfare beneficiaries with no national counterpart Filed
PB 382 Establishing Bangsamoro Women's Welfare Centers Dedicated women's welfare centers — institutional complement to BWC's policy mandate Filed

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 9: Social Services Social welfare is a core BDP sector — targets include poverty reduction, social protection coverage, and improved welfare service delivery
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 2: Regional Trends Social protection will be efficiently and adequately delivered to internally displaced and marginalized communities — a stated outcome target
Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028) Pillar 3: Resilient Communities Social services delivery, disaster preparedness, and protection of vulnerable populations are central to community resilience

Legal Briefer

A granular Q&A analysis. Answers stay as close as possible to the language of the law. All verbatim statutory text in bold italic. Key terms in bold. Themes follow the legal hierarchy and analytical flow.

A. BOL Grant and Scope

Q. What power does the BOL grant the Bangsamoro Government in this area? A. Art. V, Sec. 2(vv) grants authority over "Social services, social welfare, and charities." This three-part enumeration encompasses: (1) social services — direct delivery of welfare programs, interventions, and support facilities to individuals, families, and communities; (2) social welfare — the policy, regulatory, and institutional framework governing welfare programs; and (3) charities — the regulation of charitable solicitation, fund drives, and donations for welfare purposes. The power is broad and does not contain an internal limitation or qualifier — it is subject only to the Art. V, Sec. 2 chapeau's constitutional floor.1

Q. What specific BOL provisions elaborate on this power beyond Art. V, Sec. 2? A. Seven provisions elaborate on this power:

  • Art. IX, Sec. 8 (Right of Access to Basic Services) — imposes a positive obligation to provide "basic and responsive health programs, quality education, appropriate services, livelihood opportunities" and mandates disaster-preparedness and calamity rehabilitation.2 The phrase "appropriate services" encompasses social welfare interventions.
  • Art. IX, Sec. 12 (Protection of Women) — mandates protection from "exploitation, abuse, or discrimination" and requires implementing legislation.3
  • Art. IX, Sec. 13 (Rights of the Youth) — mandates protection of youth "physical, moral, spiritual, intellectual, and social well-being" and creation of a youth commission (fulfilled by BAA 10).4
  • Art. IX, Sec. 14 (Rights of Children) — mandates protection of children, "especially orphans of tender age," from exploitation, abuse, or discrimination.5
  • Art. IX, Sec. 23 (Support for Persons with Special Needs, Senior Citizens, and Disadvantaged Groups) — mandates creation of a "special agency, support facilities and livelihood or skills training" for persons with special needs; coordination of all rehabilitation agencies; and preservation of senior citizens' existing benefits and privileges.6
  • Art. XII, Sec. 19 (Allocation of the Block Grant) — mandates "highest budgetary priority to education, health, and social services."7
  • Art. VII, Sec. 38 (Organization of the Bureaucracy) — authorizes creation of offices "necessary for the exercise of its functions and delivery of social services."8

Q. Does the BOL mandate the Parliament to enact specific legislation for this power? A. The BOL does not name a specific code (unlike the "Bangsamoro cooperative code" in Art. XIII, Sec. 24). However, Art. IX, Sec. 12 mandates: "The Parliament shall enact the necessary laws for the implementation of this section" — referring to women's protection. Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandates the establishment of a "special agency" for persons with special needs — an institutional mandate that requires legislation. These are implicit legislative mandates, not named codes.

Q. Does the BOL create any specific body, office, or institution for this power? A. The BOL does not directly create MSSD — that was accomplished by BAA 13. But Art. IX, Sec. 23 directs the Bangsamoro Government to "establish a special agency" for persons with special needs. Art. IX, Sec. 13 directs Parliament to "create by law a commission on youth" (fulfilled by BAA 10/BYC). Art. IX, Sec. 7 directs creation of a human rights commission (fulfilled by BAA 4/BHRC). Art. VII, Sec. 42 authorizes Parliament to create offices for women, youth, settler communities, DRRM, and planning.

Q. What conditions, limitations, or qualifications does the BOL place on this power? A. Three qualifications: 1. Constitutional floor (Art. V, Sec. 2 chapeau) — "Subject to Section 20, Article X of the Constitution" — all social welfare legislation must operate within the constitutional framework.1 2. Senior citizens' benefits floor (Art. IX, Sec. 23) — "the benefits and privileges they presently enjoy" — existing national benefits under RA 9994 and related laws cannot be diminished.6 3. Budgetary priority (Art. XII, Sec. 19) — social services must receive "highest budgetary priority" — the Parliament cannot deprioritize social services funding.7

No express national law consistency requirement applies to this power.

B. Constitutional Considerations

Q. What constitutional provisions apply to this subject area? A. Social services and welfare engages some of the Constitution's strongest and most numerous mandates:

  • Universal constraint: Art. X, Sec. 15 — autonomous regions operate "within the framework of this Constitution."
  • Art. II, Sec. 9 — the State shall "promote a just and dynamic social order" and "provide adequate social services."
  • Art. II, Sec. 10 — the State shall "promote social justice in all phases of national development."
  • Art. II, Sec. 12 — the State "recognizes the sanctity of family life" and shall "protect and strengthen the family as a basic autonomous social institution."
  • Art. II, Sec. 13 — the State "recognizes the vital role of the youth in nation-building" and shall "promote and protect their physical, moral, spiritual, intellectual, and social well-being."
  • Art. II, Sec. 14 — the State "recognizes the role of women in nation-building" and shall "ensure the fundamental equality before the law of women and men."
  • Art. XIII, Sec. 1 — Congress shall give "highest priority to the enactment of measures that protect and enhance the right of all the people to human dignity."
  • Art. XIII, Sec. 11 — the State shall "make essential goods, health and other social services available to all the people at affordable cost" with "priority for the needs of the underprivileged sick, elderly, disabled, women, and children."
  • Art. XIII, Sec. 13 — the State shall "establish a special agency for disabled persons for rehabilitation, self-development and self-reliance, and their integration into the mainstream of society."
  • Art. XIII, Sec. 14 — the State shall "protect working women" and provide facilities to "enhance their welfare."

Q. What rights and privileges does the Constitution guarantee in this area? A. The Constitution guarantees: (1) equal protection (Art. III, Sec. 1) — social welfare programs must not discriminate on unconstitutional grounds; (2) due process (Art. III, Sec. 1) — removal of benefits requires procedural safeguards; (3) right to social services (Art. II, Sec. 9; Art. XIII, Sec. 11) — state obligation to provide affordable social services; (4) children's welfare (Art. II, Sec. 13; Art. XV, Sec. 3(2)) — the State shall defend the right of children to assistance; (5) women's equality (Art. II, Sec. 14) — fundamental equality before the law; (6) PWD integration (Art. XIII, Sec. 13) — rehabilitation and mainstreaming.

These constitutional rights set the floor — BARMM cannot reduce protections below what the Constitution mandates, but can exceed them.

Q. What constitutional mandates exist for legislation or institutions in this area? A. Art. XIII, Sec. 13 mandates a "special agency for disabled persons" — the national counterpart is the National Council on Disability Affairs (NCDA). Art. IX, Sec. 23 of the BOL mirrors this mandate for the Bangsamoro level. Congress enacted RA 7277 (Magna Carta for Disabled Persons) and RA 10070 (PWD Affairs Offices in LGUs) to implement this constitutional mandate — these laws carry constitutional weight even without a BOL consistency requirement.

C. National Law Considerations

Q. What does the current national legal framework provide for this subject area? A. The national social welfare framework is extensive, comprising over a dozen major statutes covering: - Child protection: RA 7610 (child abuse), RA 9344/10630 (juvenile justice), RA 6972 (day care centers), RA 10821 (children's emergency relief), RA 11596 (anti-child marriage) - Women's protection: RA 9262 (VAWC), RA 9710 (Magna Carta of Women), RA 9208/10364 (anti-trafficking) - PWD welfare: RA 7277/9442/10524/10754 (Magna Carta for PWDs), RA 10070 (PWD offices in LGUs), RA 11228 (PhilHealth for PWDs) - Senior citizen welfare: RA 9994 (Expanded Senior Citizens Act), RA 10645 (PhilHealth for seniors) - Social protection: RA 11310 (4Ps/Pantawid), RA 8972 (solo parents) - Adoption: RA 8552 (domestic adoption), RA 8043 (inter-country adoption)

Q. What national laws currently apply in BARMM? A. All listed national laws apply in BARMM — most directly because their criminal law and benefits provisions are residual national powers. Key distinctions: - Criminal law provisions (RA 7610, RA 9262, RA 11596, RA 9208, RA 9775) — apply directly; penalties beyond arresto menor exceed BARMM jurisdiction - Benefits and privileges (RA 9994, RA 10754, RA 11228) — apply directly; Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandates these be preserved - Social protection programs (RA 11310/4Ps) — apply directly; DSWD administers nationally - Institutional mandates (RA 10070, RA 6972) — apply suppletorily where BAA 49 or a future Bangsamoro social welfare code addresses the same function

Q. What national institutions exercise authority in BARMM for this area? A. Three national agencies exercise significant authority: 1. DSWD — administers the 4Ps (RA 11310), adoption proceedings (RA 8552), VAWC shelters, and supplementary feeding programs. Retains BARMM regional presence through DSWD-BARMM field office. 2. NCDA — coordinates national PWD policy; issues PWD IDs in coordination with LGUs. 3. Council for the Welfare of Children (CWC) — coordinates national child welfare policy; monitors RA 7610 compliance.

DSWD's operational presence in BARMM is the most significant overlap — the 4Ps alone serves hundreds of thousands of BARMM beneficiaries and its transfer to MSSD would require legislation, a social registry, and institutional capacity that MSSD does not yet possess.

D. Implementation

D.1 How the Bangsamoro Government Implements This Power

Q. Which ministry or office is primarily responsible for exercising this power? A. The Ministry of Social Services and Development (MSSD) — established by BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIII, Sec. 1-22. BAA 85 (FY 2026 GAA) allocates its operational budget.910

Q. What standalone legislation (BAAs) has the Parliament enacted for this power? A. No standalone social welfare code exists. However, five BAAs directly implement aspects of this power: BAA 57 (mujahideen welfare), BAA 62 (IDP rights), BAA 83 (nutrition), BAA 8 (BWC/women), and BAA 10 (BYC/youth). These are sector-specific rather than comprehensive.

Q. What are the lead ministry's mandate and functions under BAA 13? A. BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIII, Sec. 2 states the MSSD mandate: "The Ministry of Social Services and Development shall provide a balanced and responsive approach to social welfare whereby the rights, needs, and interests of all citizens within its jurisdiction, with special focus on the marginalized and vulnerable sectors, including women, children, senior citizens, differently abled persons, indigenous cultural communities, internally displaced persons, and those similarly situated by analogy, are protected and addressed at all times, especially during the existence of a crisis, whether natural or human-induced, that affects their well-being and their participation in community affairs."9

Sec. 3 enumerates 18 powers and functions: (a) Formulate, develop and implement plans/programs in social welfare; (b) adopt policies for effective implementation; (c) promote/support/coordinate non-governmental social welfare facilities; (d) establish/operate/maintain institutional facilities; (e) promote/build people's organizations for self-directing welfare; (f) promote/coordinate networks for welfare delivery; (g) accredit social welfare institutions and organizations; (h) undertake researches and studies; (i) initiate bilateral/multilateral linkages; (j) provide advisory services and training programs; (k) disseminate information; (l) deputize law enforcement for protection of exploited/abused/disadvantaged; (m) regulate fund drives, public solicitations, and charitable donations; (n) set standards, accredit, and monitor social welfare activities; (o) exercise functional/technical supervision over social workers in other government settings; (p) deputize LGUs for disaster relief; (q) coordinate programs for marginalized sectors (women, children, senior citizens, PWDs, ICCs, IDPs); and (r) perform other functions as provided by law.9

Sec. 12 authorizes MSSD to establish and operate 9 types of welfare facilities: (a) Vocational Rehabilitation and Special Learning Centers for PWDs; (b) Senior Citizens Centers; (c) Centers for Street Children; (d) Crisis Intervention Facilities; (e) VAWC Survivor Facilities; (f) Rehabilitation Centers for Children in Conflict with the Law; (g) Reception and Study Centers for Children; (h) Orphanage Institutions; and (i) other facilities as necessary.9

Q. What can the lead ministry NOT do without a standalone BAA beyond BAA 13? A. BAA 13 provides the institutional framework but cannot deliver: - Comprehensive regulatory standards — licensing requirements for social welfare agencies, service delivery standards, quality assurance mechanisms - Detailed benefits framework — a Bangsamoro-specific social protection floor defining benefits, eligibility, and delivery mechanisms - Penalty provisions — sanctions for violations of social welfare standards (child abuse, exploitation, non-compliance with facility standards) - Shari'ah-compliant models — kafala guardianship system, zakat-based social protection, Islamic orphan care standards - Social registry — a Bangsamoro targeting database for social welfare programs (called for in Res. No. 117) - Jurisdictional boundaries — formal delineation of MSSD vs. DSWD functions, especially for the 4Ps and adoption proceedings

Q. Have any parliamentary resolutions signaled intent to legislate in this area? A. Yes — eight resolutions signal legislative intent: Res. No. 43 (orphans/widows bureau), Res. No. 115 (livelihood guidelines), Res. No. 116 (4Ps monitoring), Res. No. 117 (indigent database), Res. No. 147 (drug rehabilitation centers), Res. No. 179 (Marawi IDP return), and Res. No. 450 (MSSD worker safety). These collectively signal demand for a more comprehensive social welfare framework than BAA 13's general mandate provides.

D.1.A Institutional Validation

Q. Is the assigned lead MOA the correct one for this power? A. Yes — MSSD's BAA 13 mandate closely matches the full scope of Art. V, Sec. 2(vv). Its 18 enumerated functions cover social services delivery, welfare regulation, and charities. However, the power's scope extends to populations also served by BWC (women), BYC (youth), BHRC (human rights dimensions), and the Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission (BAA 83). MSSD is the correct lead but must coordinate with at least four supporting agencies and the LGU-level social welfare offices.

Q. What BARMM institutions currently exist for this power? A. The institutional landscape: - Lead ministry: MSSD (BAA 13, Title XIII; BAA 85) - Supporting commissions: BWC (BAA 8) — women's welfare; BYC (BAA 10) — youth welfare; BHRC (BAA 4) — human rights monitoring - Specialized body: Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission (BAA 83) — nutrition programs - Sector-specific legislation: BAA 57 — mujahideen welfare; BAA 62 — IDP rights - LGU-level: Provincial/City Social Welfare Offices under MSSD (BAA 13, Sec. 10-11); Municipal Social Welfare Offices (BAA 13, Sec. 14) - Not yet operational: Bangsamoro Disability Affairs Office (PB 250 — filed, not enacted)

All listed institutions are operational except the proposed Disability Affairs Office.

Q. What institutions does the BOL mandate that have NOT been created yet? A. Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandates "a special agency" for persons with special needs and disadvantaged groups. This has not been created as a standalone body. PB 250 (Bangsamoro Disability Affairs Office) would fulfill this mandate but has not been enacted. Currently, MSSD handles PWD services through its general mandate, but the BOL contemplates a dedicated agency.

Q. Does this power require creating NEW institutions that the BOL does not specifically name? A. Yes — full exercise of this power requires: 1. Social registry/targeting system — a database of Bangsamoro indigents and welfare beneficiaries (called for in Res. No. 117) to enable means-tested social protection 2. Social welfare regulatory body or division — to license and monitor private social welfare agencies, which MSSD currently does through its general accreditation function (Sec. 3(g)/(n)) but without a regulatory code 3. Shari'ah social welfare advisory mechanism — to ensure welfare programs comply with Islamic principles, particularly for orphan care (kafala), zakat distribution, and family welfare

D.2 How the National Government Implements This Power

Q. How does the national government currently exercise authority in this area within BARMM? A. DSWD is the dominant national agency operating in BARMM for social services. It administers: the 4Ps conditional cash transfer program (RA 11310) — the largest social protection program in the country with significant BARMM coverage; the Supplementary Feeding Program; VAWC assistance; adoption proceedings; and disaster response through its Quick Response Fund. DSWD maintains a BARMM regional field office that operates independently of MSSD.

Q. What national government programs and services operate in BARMM for this area? A. Key programs: (1) Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program (4Ps) — conditional cash transfers to poor households; (2) Supplementary Feeding Program — nutrition assistance for underweight children; (3) Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) — emergency financial assistance; (4) Sustainable Livelihood Program (SLP) — livelihood development; (5) KALAHI-CIDSS — community-driven development; (6) Listahanan — the National Household Targeting System for poverty reduction, which is the social registry BARMM lacks.

Q. What is the status of devolution for this power — has authority been transferred from national agencies to BARMM? A. Devolution is partial and incomplete. MSSD was established and operational social welfare employees were absorbed and transferred to the Bangsamoro Government. However, DSWD retains operational authority over the 4Ps, adoption proceedings, and the Listahanan social registry. The devolution of the 4Ps alone — which requires the social registry infrastructure, payment systems, and compliance verification mechanisms — represents the single largest unresolved devolution issue for this power.

Q. Are there areas where both national and Bangsamoro agencies operate concurrently? A. Yes — significant overlap exists in: (1) disaster response — both DSWD and MSSD deploy to disaster-affected areas, sometimes with unclear coordination protocols; (2) VAWC services — both DSWD and MSSD operate shelters and crisis intervention facilities; (3) child welfare — DSWD retains adoption authority while MSSD operates children's facilities; (4) livelihood programs — both run livelihood assistance with different targeting systems. This concurrency is by default (devolution incomplete), not by BOL design.

D.3 BARMM Implementation

Q. What has the BARMM accomplished so far in exercising this power? A. Key accomplishments: (1) Institutional establishment — MSSD fully operational with provincial/city offices across all five provinces and the Special Geographic Area; (2) Welfare facilities — crisis intervention facilities, children's centers, and senior citizen facilities established in multiple locations; (3) Mujahideen welfare — BAA 57 implemented, providing financial assistance to senior/disabled ex-combatants; (4) IDP protection — BAA 62 enacted, establishing rights framework for displaced persons; (5) Nutrition commission — BAA 83 created the Bangsamoro Nutrition Commission; (6) Fund drive regulation — MSSD exercises solicitation permit authority under BAA 13, Sec. 15-18.

Q. Where is the BARMM stronger than the national government in this area? A. BARMM's unique advantages: - Conflict-sensitive design — BAA 57 (mujahideen welfare) and BAA 62 (IDP rights) address populations and situations unique to the Bangsamoro context that the national framework does not specifically serve - Cultural and linguistic fit — MSSD delivers services in local languages through staff who understand Bangsamoro cultural norms - Shari'ah integration potential — the ability to integrate Islamic welfare principles (kafala, zakat-based targeting) into social welfare design, which the national system cannot accommodate - Institutional proximity — MSSD's provincial/city offices have closer community access than DSWD's regional structure

Q. What is holding BARMM back from full implementation? A. The single biggest constraint is the absence of a standalone social welfare code that would: (a) establish a Bangsamoro social registry to enable means-tested targeting; (b) define jurisdictional boundaries with DSWD, particularly for the 4Ps; (c) create Shari'ah-compliant welfare models; and (d) set regulatory standards for private social welfare agencies. Without this legislation, MSSD operates on BAA 13's general mandate, which provides institutional existence but not the regulatory framework to fully exercise the (vv) power. The secondary constraint is incomplete devolution — DSWD continues to administer major social protection programs within BARMM.

D.4 National Government Implementation

Q. What has the national government built for this area that BARMM does not yet have? A. The national system provides: - Legislative depth — over a dozen major social welfare statutes with decades of IRRs, judicial interpretation, and amendment cycles - Listahanan (NHTS-PR) — a national social registry covering approximately 15 million poor households, enabling means-tested targeting for all social protection programs. BARMM has no equivalent. - 4Ps payment and compliance system — automated cash transfer disbursement, school attendance verification, health checkup monitoring — a mature operational system - DSWD institutional infrastructure — 17 regional offices, 81 provincial offices, specialized bureaus (Standards, Protective Services, Community Development), and a network of government-run residential care facilities - Adoption and foster care system — centralized database, accreditation of child-placing agencies, inter-country adoption protocols

Q. Where does the national system fall short for BARMM? A. The national framework fails BARMM in several critical areas: - No Shari'ah-compliant alternatives — RA 8552's adoption framework does not accommodate kafala (Islamic guardianship), which many Bangsamoro Muslim families prefer to formal adoption - Conflict-insensitive targeting — Listahanan's poverty assessment does not account for conflict-affected populations, IDPs, or ex-combatants as distinct vulnerability categories - Language barriers — national social welfare forms, communications, and training materials are in Filipino/English, not in Maguindanaon, Maranao, or Tausug - Underserved presence — DSWD's operational presence in BARMM is proportionally lower than in other regions, particularly in island provinces (Sulu, Tawi-Tawi, Basilan) - No recognition of Islamic welfare institutions — the national framework does not integrate mosque-based welfare, zakat distribution, or ulama-based counseling into the formal social protection system

Q. What should BARMM replicate, adapt, or replace from the national system? A. - Replicate: Financial reporting and audit standards for social welfare programs; social welfare agency licensing requirements (adapt to include Shari'ah-based organizations) - Adapt: Listahanan model — build a Bangsamoro social registry using DSWD's methodology but incorporating conflict-sensitivity indicators, IDP status, and ex-combatant categories; 4Ps compliance system — adapt for local languages and cultural contexts - Replace: RA 8552's adoption framework — enact a kafala-based alternative for Muslim families while maintaining RA 8552 for non-Muslim residents; orphan care institutional model — replace residential institutionalization with community-based kafala fostering as the primary model

E. Shari'ah Considerations

Q. Does this power have a Shari'ah dimension? A. Yes — social welfare has a strong Shari'ah dimension. Islam places extensive obligations on both individuals and the state to care for the vulnerable. The five maqasid al-Shari'ah (objectives of Islamic law) — preservation of religion (din), life (nafs), intellect ('aql), progeny (nasl), and wealth (mal) — collectively require a social safety net. Orphan care, widow support, and poverty alleviation are among the most emphasized obligations in the Qur'an and hadith.

Q. What does the BOL say about Shari'ah in relation to this power? A. The BOL does not expressly require Shari'ah compliance for social welfare programs. However, Art. X, Sec. 4 authorizes Parliament to enact "laws on personal, family, and property law jurisdiction" — all of which intersect with social welfare (guardianship, inheritance, orphan care) — all of which intersect with social welfare (guardianship, inheritance, orphan care). PD 1083 (Code of Muslim Personal Laws), Art. 70-72, governs guardianship (walaya) of Muslim minors through Shari'ah courts.

Q. What Shari'ah principles are relevant to legislation in this area? A. Key principles: - Hifz al-Nafs (preservation of life) — the state must protect life, which encompasses disaster response, health-related social services, and support for the destitute - Hifz al-Nasl (preservation of progeny) — child protection, orphan care, and family welfare are direct applications - Maslahah (public interest) — justifies government social welfare programs and expenditures - Kafala (Islamic fostering/guardianship) — the Shari'ah-preferred model for orphan care, distinct from Western-style adoption that severs biological lineage - Zakat — the third pillar of Islam; its eight recipient categories (asnaf) overlap substantially with social welfare beneficiary categories (the poor, the needy, wayfarers, those in debt) - Waqf (Islamic endowment) — a potential institutional mechanism for sustained social welfare funding through endowed assets

Q. What existing Shari'ah legislation or jurisprudence applies? A. PD 1083, Art. 70-72 governs guardianship of Muslim minors — Shari'ah courts exercise jurisdiction over custody and guardianship disputes. There is no enacted Bangsamoro Shari'ah legislation on social welfare as of BAA 89. The Shari'ah Legislative Review Board (SLRB) would review any proposed social welfare code for Islamic compliance.

Q. How should Shari'ah compliance be integrated into Bangsamoro social welfare legislation? A. A dual-track approach is appropriate: (1) mandatory Shari'ah compliance for programs serving exclusively Muslim beneficiaries (orphan care, guardianship, family welfare) — kafala rather than adoption, halal food in welfare facilities, gender-appropriate service delivery; (2) optional Shari'ah-compliant track for programs serving mixed populations (livelihood assistance, disaster response) — conventional and Islamic models available side by side; (3) principled — all social welfare legislation guided by maslahah, 'adl (justice), and amanah (accountability) without mandating specific compliance. The SLRB should review proposed social welfare legislation and the Darul Ifta' should be consulted on specific Islamic welfare models.

F. Divergence Analysis

Q. To what extent can the BARMM diverge from national law and policy in this area? A. Social services and welfare falls in Tier 4 — Broad on the divergence spectrum (constitutional floor only). No BOL provision requires consistency with national social welfare laws. The divergence space is as follows:

  1. No consistency requirement — the BOL does not contain "consistent with" or "in accordance with" language referencing national social welfare statutes for this power. BARMM has broad legislative authority.
  2. Constitutional floor applies — as established in the Constitutional Considerations section (Section B), Art. XIII, Sec. 11 and Art. XIII, Sec. 13 mandate social services availability and a special agency for PWDs. Any BAA must serve these constitutional purposes.
  3. "Presently enjoy" floor for senior citizens — as established in the BOL Grant and Scope section (Section A), Art. IX, Sec. 23's language creates a one-way ratchet: BARMM can go above RA 9994's benefits but cannot reduce them.
  4. Criminal law stays national — as established in the National Law Considerations section (Section C), criminal provisions in RA 7610, RA 9262, RA 11596, RA 9208, and RA 9344 are residual national powers. BARMM cannot modify criminal penalties for child abuse, VAWC, trafficking, or related offenses.
  5. Shari'ah as unique basis for divergence — as established in the Shari'ah Considerations section (Section E), kafala, zakat, and Islamic welfare principles provide a uniquely Bangsamoro justification for different social welfare models. This is the strongest basis for substantive divergence: BARMM can establish a kafala-based orphan care system, zakat-integrated social protection, and Islamic-informed welfare standards that the national system cannot provide.

Per Ali v. BTA Parliament (GR-02219, 2025), BARMM legislative authority "must always operate within the bounds of the Constitution and national statutes." For social welfare, this means: divergence is broad for service delivery models, institutional design, and welfare standards; it is limited for criminal protections and existing benefits.

Q. If BARMM enacts different standards, what are the implications for rights holders and cross-boundary operations? A. A Bangsamoro social welfare code that establishes kafala instead of adoption would create a dual-track system: Muslim families within BARMM use kafala for orphan placement; non-Muslim families and cross-boundary cases revert to RA 8552. Mutual recognition provisions would be needed for: (1) social welfare agency accreditation recognized across BARMM/non-BARMM boundaries; (2) protection order enforcement (RA 9262 protection orders issued by BARMM courts must be recognized nationally, and vice versa); (3) senior citizen and PWD benefit portability — benefits earned under RA 9994/RA 10754 must be honored within BARMM and vice versa.

Q. What happens to national law when the Parliament enacts a BAA on this subject? A. The BAA governs within BARMM for matters within the enumerated power. National criminal law provisions (RA 7610, RA 9262, RA 11596, RA 9208, RA 9344) continue to apply as residual national powers. RA 11310 (4Ps) continues to apply until a formal devolution agreement transfers the program to MSSD. National laws on benefits and privileges (RA 9994, RA 10754) continue to apply as the floor — the BAA can add but not subtract.

G. Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Q. Are there overlapping or unclear jurisdictions between Bangsamoro and national authority? A. The most significant jurisdictional overlap is between MSSD and DSWD in BARMM. Specific overlaps: (1) 4Ps administration — DSWD administers nationally, MSSD has no formal role despite being the Bangsamoro social welfare ministry; (2) adoption proceedings — DSWD retains central authority under RA 8552, while MSSD handles local child welfare; (3) disaster response — both deploy teams with sometimes unclear coordination; (4) social welfare agency accreditation — MSSD accredits under BAA 13, Sec. 3(g), while DSWD also accredits social welfare agencies under its national mandate. Resolution requires either a formal MOA delineating functions or legislation that clearly assigns BARMM-level authority to MSSD.

Q. What pending Parliament Bills address the gaps? A. Eleven pending PBs address various aspects (listed in the Parliament Bills table above). However, they are sector-specific — addressing individual populations (ASD children, women combatants, senior citizens, ulama, mujahideen families) rather than providing a comprehensive framework. Even if all were enacted, BARMM would still lack: (a) a unified social welfare code with regulatory standards; (b) a social registry; (c) kafala legislation; and (d) jurisdictional delineation with DSWD. PB 250 (Disability Affairs Office) is the most structurally significant because it fulfills the Art. IX, Sec. 23 institutional mandate.

H. Development and Policy Connection

Q. Which BDP 2023-2028 goals and Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda pillars depend on this power? A. BDP Chapter 9 (Social Services) sets targets for poverty reduction, social protection coverage, and improved welfare service delivery. BDP Chapter 2 (Regional Trends) identifies that "social protection will be efficiently and adequately delivered to internally displaced and marginalized communities" as a desired outcome. The Mas Matatag na Bangsamoro Agenda (2026-2028), Pillar 3 (Resilient Communities) depends directly on functional social services delivery — disaster preparedness, protection of vulnerable populations, and community-level welfare.

BARMM has the highest poverty incidence among Philippine regions. Social services are not just a welfare function but the primary mechanism for poverty reduction, post-conflict recovery, and human development in the region.

Q. What development outcomes are at risk if the legislative gaps remain unaddressed? A. Without a comprehensive social welfare code: (1) poverty targeting remains blind — no Bangsamoro social registry means welfare programs cannot effectively identify and reach the poorest households; (2) 4Ps transition stalls — MSSD cannot assume the region's largest social protection program without legislative authority and institutional infrastructure; (3) orphan care remains institutional — without kafala legislation, orphans are placed in institutional facilities rather than family-based care, contrary to both Islamic values and international best practice; (4) PWD services remain fragmented — the Art. IX, Sec. 23 special agency mandate remains unfulfilled, and PWD services lack a dedicated institutional home; (5) post-conflict welfare gaps persist — Marawi IDPs, ex-combatants, and conflict widows/orphans lack a comprehensive framework beyond sector-specific BAAs.

I. Power-Specific Questions

Q. How does the (vv) social services power relate to (r) development programs for women, children, youth, and labor? A. Art. V, Sec. 2(r) covers "development programs for women, children, youth, and labor." The (vv) social services power covers the welfare, protective, and rehabilitative dimension for these same populations, while (r) covers the development, empowerment, and participatory dimension. In practice: BWC (BAA 8) and BYC (BAA 10) implement the (r) power; MSSD implements the (vv) power. The overlap is at the population level (both serve women, children, youth) but distinct at the intervention level (development programs vs. welfare services). MSSD's coordination function (BAA 13, Sec. 3(q)) is the mechanism for harmonizing (vv) and (r) programs.

Q. What is the legal basis for MSSD's regulation of charities and fund drives? A. BAA 13, Title XIII, Chapter 5 (Sec. 15-18) provides the regulatory framework. Sec. 15 requires any entity "desiring to solicit or receive contributions for charitable or public welfare purposes" to secure a permit from MSSD. The Minister may issue permanent or temporary permits or disapprove applications. Three categories are exempt: (1) entities with express legislative authority to solicit; (2) religious solicitations within mosques/churches; and (3) personal solicitations among relatives and friends (Sec. 16). Sec. 17 requires reporting on collections and expenditures. Sec. 18 authorizes fee collection.9 This is one of the few areas where BAA 13 already provides a relatively complete regulatory framework.

Q. Can BARMM establish a zakat-based social protection system? A. Yes — there is a strong legal basis. Art. V, Sec. 2(vv) grants authority over social services and charities; Art. X, Sec. 4 authorizes Parliament to enact Shari'ah laws on property matters (zakat is a property-related obligation). A Bangsamoro Zakat Act could: (a) establish a Bangsamoro Zakat Board to collect and distribute zakat; (b) define the eight asnaf categories as the targeting framework; (c) integrate zakat collections with MSSD's social welfare programs; (d) provide for voluntary zakat remittance through formal channels. This would be a uniquely Bangsamoro social protection instrument with no national counterpart — and one of the clearest expressions of the divergence authority available under this power.

Q. How should MSSD handle the kafala vs. adoption question? A. The tension: RA 8552 (Domestic Adoption Act) establishes a national adoption system administered by DSWD. Classical Islamic jurisprudence does not recognize adoption (tabanni) that severs biological lineage but endorses kafala (fostering/sponsorship that preserves the child's lineage). A Bangsamoro social welfare code should: (1) establish kafala as the primary orphan care model for Muslim children in BARMM; (2) maintain RA 8552 as the suppletory framework for non-Muslim families and inter-country cases; (3) ensure Shari'ah court jurisdiction over kafala proceedings (Art. 70-72, PD 1083, already provides guardianship jurisdiction); (4) create a kafala registry administered by MSSD. This dual-track approach respects both Islamic law and the national framework.

Q. What unique populations does BARMM's social welfare system serve that the national system does not specifically address? A. Five populations unique to the Bangsamoro context: 1. Mujahideen — senior and disabled MILF-MNLF ex-combatants (BAA 57) 2. Conflict orphans and widows — children and spouses of those killed in the Bangsamoro armed conflict (Res. No. 43) 3. Marawi IDPs — populations displaced by the 2017 Marawi siege who remain in transitional shelters (Res. No. 179) 4. Ulama and faith-based counselors — religious leaders who provide community welfare services but lack formal social protection (PB 381) 5. Former women combatants — female ex-fighters requiring economic empowerment and social reintegration (PB 292)

These populations require specialized social welfare programs that the national DSWD framework does not provide.

Legislative Gaps and Recommended Legislation

Gap Type Gap What Law / Institution Should Be Created What It Would Cover BOL Basis
Legislative No comprehensive social welfare code Bangsamoro Social Welfare Code Regulatory standards for social welfare programs; social welfare agency licensing; benefit frameworks for PWDs, senior citizens, children, women, and IDPs; social registry system; Shari'ah-compliant welfare models (kafala, zakat integration); jurisdictional delineation with DSWD; penalty provisions Art. V, Sec. 2(vv); Art. IX, Sec. 8, 12, 14, 23
Institutional No special agency for PWDs (Art. IX, Sec. 23 mandate unfulfilled) Bangsamoro Disability Affairs Office (PB 250 pending) PWD registration, ID system, benefits monitoring, rehabilitation program coordination, accessibility standards enforcement, livelihood assistance Art. IX, Sec. 23
Legislative No kafala legislation Bangsamoro Kafala (Islamic Fostering) Act Kafala proceedings and registry, qualifications for kafil (guardian), child welfare standards, Shari'ah court jurisdiction, relationship with RA 8552 adoption framework Art. V, Sec. 2(vv); Art. X, Sec. 4; PD 1083, Art. 70-72
Legislative No Bangsamoro social registry Bangsamoro Social Registry Act Indigent and vulnerable household database, poverty assessment methodology (conflict-sensitive), data sharing with MSSD/LGUs, privacy protections, basis for means-tested social protection targeting Art. V, Sec. 2(vv); Art. IX, Sec. 8; Res. No. 117
Legislative No zakat regulation Bangsamoro Zakat Act Zakat Board establishment, collection mechanisms, eight asnaf distribution framework, integration with MSSD social welfare programs, accountability and audit requirements Art. V, Sec. 2(vv); Art. X, Sec. 4
Institutional No formal MSSD-DSWD coordination mechanism Intergovernmental Social Welfare Agreement (MOA or Executive Order) 4Ps transition plan, adoption authority transfer timeline, disaster response protocols, social registry interoperability, dual-accreditation system Art. V, Sec. 2; Art. V, Sec. 2(vv); Art. XII, Sec. 19

(yy)
Tourism Development
BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(yy)

Power 43 of 55 — Enumerated under Republic Act No. 11054

Legal Sources + 9-Theme Q&A Legal Briefer

(yy) Tourism Development

BOL Art. V, Sec. 2(yy) --- "Tourism development;"

Power Overview

Attribute Value
BOL Basis Art. V, Sec. 2(yy) --- enumerated power
National Law Consistency Required Yes --- Art. XIII, Sec. 35 requires tourism enterprise zone designation "in accordance with Republic Act No. 9593" (The Tourism Act of 2009). Additionally, BOL Art. XVIII, Sec. 4(r) modifies Sections 59, 60, 61, and 62 of RA 9593 as they apply to BARMM --- establishing a hybrid framework where BARMM recommends TEZs to TIEZA under the national law, but the BOL modifies the TEZ provisions to account for Bangsamoro autonomy.
Lead Ministry/Office Ministry of Trade, Investments and Tourism (MTIT) --- BAA 13, Book VI, Title XIV; Bureau of Tourism (BAA 13, Sec. 15-16) is the operational unit within MTIT
Supporting Agencies Bangsamoro Planning and Development Authority (BPDA); Bangsamoro Commission for the Preservation of Cultural Heritage (BCPCH); Ministry of Environment, Natural Resources and Energy (MENRE); Ministry of Public Works (MOPW); Ministry of Transportation and Communications (MTC); Bangsamoro Economic Zone Authority (BEZA)
BDP Chapter(s) Chapter 7 (Economic Development); Chapter 6 (Cultural Diversity and Heritage); Chapter 11 (Infrastructure Development)
Named Legislative Mandate None. Art. XIII, Sec. 35 does not name a specific code or act for tourism. The BOL provides a general promotional mandate and a specific TEZ recommendation power, but no named "Bangsamoro Tourism Code" or equivalent.

Legal Sources

The reference tables below present the raw legal data --- BOL provisions, enacted legislation, national laws, jurisprudence, and pending bills. Read these first to see the legal landscape, then proceed to the Legal Briefer Q&A for analysis and interpretation.

BOL Provisions

All BOL provisions relevant to this power --- verbatim text with explanation of what each provision means in practice.

ProvisionVerbatim TextExplanation / Implication
Art. V, Sec. 2(yy) "Tourism development;" Enumerates tourism development as one of the 55 powers over which the Bangsamoro Government exercises authority. Grants the power to promote, regulate, and develop tourism within the region.
Art. V, Sec. 2 (chapeau) "Subject to Section 20, Article X of the Constitution and this Organic Law, the Bangsamoro Government shall exercise its authority over the following matters without prejudice to the general supervision of the President of the Republic of the Philippines:" All 55 enumerated powers, including tourism, are subject to the constitutional framework (Art. X, Sec. 20) and presidential general supervision. Tourism is specifically listed in Art. X, Sec. 20(6) --- "Economic, social, and tourism development" --- giving it express constitutional grounding.
Art. XIII, Sec. 35 "The Bangsamoro Government shall promote tourism within the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. It may recommend the designation of tourism enterprise zones to the Tourism Infrastructure and Enterprise Zone Authority, in accordance with Republic Act No. 9593, otherwise known as 'The Tourism Act of 2009.'" Two obligations: (1) a general promotional mandate --- the Bangsamoro Government "shall promote tourism" (mandatory, not discretionary); (2) a specific TEZ recommendation power --- BARMM may recommend tourism enterprise zones to TIEZA, but designation authority remains with TIEZA under RA 9593. The phrase "in accordance with Republic Act No. 9593" creates an express national law consistency requirement for TEZ designation.
Art. XIII, Sec. 1 "The Bangsamoro Government shall establish an economic system based on the principles and state policies declared in the Constitution." General economic framework. Tourism operates within this constitutional economic policy framework.
Art. XIII, Sec. 2 "The Bangsamoro Government shall promote the effective use of economic resources and endeavor to attain economic development that facilitates growth and full employment, human development, and social justice." Tourism is an instrument for economic development, growth, and full employment within the broader BOL economic vision.
Art. XIII, Sec. 30 "The Bangsamoro Government shall have the power to accredit halal-certifying bodies in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. It shall promote awareness through the development and implementation of a halal campaign program." Cross-reference: The halal accreditation power directly supports tourism through halal tourism --- a growing global market segment. BARMM's authority to accredit halal-certifying bodies enables halal-compliant hotels, restaurants, and tour operations.
Art. XIII, Sec. 26 "The Bangsamoro Government shall regulate traditional barter trade with the Brunei Darussalam-Indonesia-Malaysia-Philippines East ASEAN Growth Area (BIMP-EAGA) Member States and countertrade with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) Member States." Cross-reference: BIMP-EAGA cooperation framework supports cross-border tourism development, particularly with Malaysia (Sabah), Indonesia (North Kalimantan, North Sulawesi), and Brunei.
Art. XVI, Sec. 8(c) "Trade, Investments, and Tourism" The BOL names the Ministry of Trade, Investments, and Tourism as one of the 15 primary ministries in the transition government, establishing tourism as a core ministerial function.
Art. XVIII, Sec. 4(r) "Sections 59, 60, 61, and 62 of Republic Act No. 9593, otherwise known as 'The Tourism Act of 2009'" Critical provision: The BOL's transitory provisions modify Sections 59-62 of RA 9593 --- the core TEZ designation, development planning, and operation provisions. This means the national TEZ framework is modified, not fully applicable, within BARMM. The modification aligns TEZ procedures with the Bangsamoro Government's role as recommending authority.

Bangsamoro Legislation (BAAs)

Enacted legislation. A Parliament Bill (PB) becomes a BAA when enacted.

BAAPB No.TitleHow It Implements This PowerStatus
BAA 13 -- Bangsamoro Administrative Code Book VI, Title XIV: establishes MTIT as the primary tourism agency; Bureau of Tourism (Sec. 15-16) with 9 functions and 2 divisions (Product Development and Standards/Accreditation; Tourism Promotions and Marketing); tourism-specific MTIT functions (Sec. 3(e)-(m)); field offices in 7 provincial areas In Force
BAA 85 -- General Appropriations Act, FY 2026 Chapter V allocates operational budget to MTIT, including Bureau of Tourism operations In Force
No standalone tourism code or tourism development act has been enacted as of BAA 89.

Parliamentary Resolutions

Adopted resolutions. A Proposed Resolution (PR) becomes an enacted Resolution when adopted.

Resolution No.PR No.Title / SubjectRelevance
Res. No. 84 -- Requesting security sector to prohibit treating Marawi Most Affected Area as tourist spot Addresses conflict-sensitive tourism --- recognizes the need to protect Marawi's war-affected areas from insensitive tourism exploitation
Res. No. 472 -- Requesting PNP to establish tourist-police assistance desk in BARMM Addresses tourist safety infrastructure --- a prerequisite for tourism development in conflict-affected regions

National Laws

All Republic Acts, Executive Orders, and Presidential Decrees pertaining to this power area.

LawTitleRelationship to This PowerApplication in BARMM
RA 9593 (2009) The Tourism Act of 2009 Comprehensive national tourism framework: DOT mandate, TIEZA (TEZ designation and regulation), TPB (tourism promotions), accreditation of tourism enterprises, tourism development planning, tourism councils. BOL requires consistency (Art. XIII, Sec. 35). BOL modifies Sec. 59-62 (TEZ provisions) per Art. XVIII, Sec. 4(r). Partially modified by the BOL: Sec. 59-62 (TEZ designation, development planning, operation) are modified to account for BARMM's recommending authority. Other provisions (DOT accreditation standards, tourism enterprise definitions, tourism councils) apply suppletorily pending Bangsamoro tourism legislation.
RA 10816 (2016) Farm Tourism Development Act Promotes farm tourism --- integration of agriculture and tourism for rural development Suppletory: applies to BARMM agricultural communities. Particularly relevant to BARMM's agricultural base --- farm tourism could diversify livelihoods in conflict-affected rural areas.
RA 7160 (1991) Local Government Code Sec. 17 assigns LGUs tourism promotion functions; Sec. 443 creates municipal tourism offices Partially superseded by BAA 49 (Local Governance Code) for BARMM LGUs. LGU tourism functions in BARMM are governed by BAA 49's framework.
EO 120 (1987) Reorganizing the Ministry of Tourism Established the Department of Tourism (DOT) in its current form DOT continues to maintain a regional office in BARMM. Coordination between DOT Region XII/BARMM and MTIT's Bureau of Tourism is the primary institutional interface.
EO 111 (1999) Establishing Guidelines for Ecotourism Development in the Philippines National Ecotourism Strategy; ecotourism site identification; National Ecotourism Steering Committee Suppletory: provides the national ecotourism framework. BARMM's rich biodiversity (Liguasan Marsh, Turtle Islands, Lake Lanao) requires ecotourism guidelines that are at minimum consistent with this framework.
EO 907 (2010) Directing the Reorganization of the Philippine Tourism Authority as TIEZA Implements RA 9593 reorganization of PTA into TIEZA TIEZA is the national agency to which BARMM recommends tourism enterprise zone designations per Art. XIII, Sec. 35.

Shari'ah Sources

Islamic legal principles, existing Shari'ah legislation, and scholarly opinions relevant to this power area.

SourceProvision / PrincipleApplication to This Power
Halal Standards Islamic dietary law (Quran 2:168, 5:3, 6:145): food and drink must be halal (permissible) and tayyib (wholesome). Extends to hospitality and services. Halal tourism requires: halal-certified food in hotels and restaurants; prayer facilities; gender-appropriate accommodations; alcohol-free zones or options; Islamic finance-compliant payment systems. BARMM's halal accreditation power (Art. XIII, Sec. 30) provides the legal basis for enforcing halal tourism standards.
Hifz al-Mal (Protection of Wealth) One of the five objectives (maqasid al-Shari'ah) of Islamic law: protecting and developing wealth through lawful means. Tourism as an economic development strategy serves hifz al-mal by generating lawful income and employment for communities. Legislation should ensure tourism revenue benefits local communities, not external exploiters.
Hifz al-'Ird (Protection of Dignity/Honor) One of the five objectives of Islamic law: protecting human dignity and honor from degradation. Tourism must respect local cultural norms and religious sensibilities. Bangsamoro tourism legislation should include provisions against exploitation, cultural insensitivity, and activities that degrade community dignity --- particularly relevant for heritage sites and indigenous cultural tourism.
Amanah (Trust/Stewardship) Persons entrusted with managing resources must act as faithful stewards --- with transparency, accountability, and prohibition against exploitation. Tourism operators in BARMM manage cultural and natural heritage on behalf of the community. Amanah principles require sustainable management, community benefit-sharing, and environmental stewardship in tourism development.
Maslahah (Public Interest/Welfare) Islamic jurisprudential principle permitting legislation that serves the common good, even where no specific textual authority exists. Tourism promotion serves maslahah by reducing poverty, creating employment, and building economic resilience. Legislation promoting responsible tourism is justified under this principle. Tourism that harms communities or environment contradicts maslahah and may be restricted.

Jurisprudence

Supreme Court decisions relevant to this power area --- defining boundaries of autonomy, resolving jurisdiction questions, or interpreting related provisions.

CaseDateHolding / RelevanceImpact on This Power
No directly relevant Supreme Court decisions identified that specifically address Bangsamoro tourism authority or the jurisdictional boundary between DOT/TIEZA and BARMM tourism regulation. Tourism jurisdiction in autonomous regions has not been the subject of a reported case.

Parliament Bills (Pending)

Filed Parliament Bills (PBs) that have NOT yet been enacted into BAAs. Shows legislative intent and pending proposals.

PB No.Title / SubjectRelevance to This PowerStatus
PB No. 217 Declaring Kutawato Cave as historical heritage site and ecotourism destination Site-specific ecotourism legislation; establishes a heritage-ecotourism model Filed
PB No. 218 Declaring Bud Tukay Peak as community-based ecotourism zone Site-specific community-based ecotourism legislation; establishes local community governance over tourism site Filed
PB No. 261 Developing and promoting Bangsamoro Tourism, establishing Tourism Promotion Board Comprehensive tourism development bill; would create a Bangsamoro Tourism Promotion Board --- institutional framework for coordinated tourism marketing Filed
PB No. 280 Establishing comprehensive framework for promotion of tourism in BARMM Comprehensive tourism framework legislation --- would address the absence of a standalone tourism code Filed

Development Plan and Policy Alignment

Framework Reference Connection
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 7: Economic Development Tourism as a key strategy for economic diversification and employment generation; targets include increasing tourist arrivals and developing tourism infrastructure [UNVERIFIED]
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 6: Cultural Diversity and Heritage Cultural heritage preservation as foundation for heritage tourism and cultural tourism products
BDP 2023-2028 Chapter 11: Infrastructure Development Tourism-supporting infrastructure: roads, airports, seaports, utilities serving tourism destinations
